name: StarDist
description: Applies pre-trained or custom StarDist deep-learning models to detect and segment star-convex objects (e.g., nuclei) in 2D and 2D+time microscopy images, returning label images and/or ROIs.
input_data: 2D images and 2D+time (time-lapse) image stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ (e.g., fluorescence or H&E nuclei images); supports applying built-in models or loading a custom exported model zip via file/URL.
output_data: Instance label image and/or ROI Manager ROIs (optionally overlaid on the input image).
use_when: Use when you need instance segmentation of mostly star-convex objects (especially nuclei) in 2D or 2D time-lapse microscopy images using a pre-trained or custom StarDist model.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when your data are truly 3D volumes (z-stacks) because the Fiji plugin currently only supports 2D and 2D+time.
typical_use_cases: Segmenting fluorescent nuclei in 2D microscopy images with the built-in versatile model
Segmenting nuclei in H&E histology images using the built-in H&E model
Batch-processing a folder of 2D images via Fiji scripting to generate label images/ROIs
Segmenting nuclei in time-lapse (2D+time) sequences for per-frame object counts

name: MorphoLibJ
description: MorphoLibJ is a collection of ImageJ plugins for 2D/3D mathematical morphology, including morphological filtering and reconstruction, watershed segmentation, binary/label utilities, and region measurements.
input_data: 2D/3D binary, grayscale, or RGB images; binary and label images.
output_data: Filtered/reconstructed images, watershed label images, and measurement results tables for 2D/3D regions.
use_when: Use it for classical 2D/3D morphology operations (with structuring elements), watershed-based segmentation, binary/label cleanup, or morphometric/photometric measurements on segmented regions.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need learning-based segmentation or analysis methods not based on mathematical morphology and label/binary processing.
typical_use_cases: Applying erosion/dilation, opening/closing, gradient, Laplacian, or top-hat filters on 2D/3D images
Enhancing thin curvilinear structures using directional morphological filtering
Filling holes, removing border-touching objects, or keeping the largest connected component in binary/label images
Segmenting 2D/3D tissues or cells using watershed with a GUI
Measuring volume, surface area, and intensity statistics of labeled regions in 2D/3D

name: CLIJ2
description: CLIJ2 is a GPU-accelerated image processing library for ImageJ/Fiji that provides OpenCL-based operations for filtering, binarization, labeling, transformations, projections, measurements, and matrix/graph processing.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji images (2D/3D grayscale, binary, label/parametric images, multi-channel time-lapse) and matrices/vectors/graphs representing neighborhood relationships.
output_data: Processed images (filtered, binary, label, parametric), transformed/projection images, and measurement outputs such as tables/matrices.
use_when: Use CLIJ2 when you want to accelerate classical image processing and measurement workflows on large 2D/3D or time-lapse datasets using an OpenCL-capable GPU.
do_not_use_when: Do not use CLIJ2 when you lack a working OpenCL GPU setup or when required operations are not available in the CLIJ2 operation set.
typical_use_cases: GPU-accelerated filtering and intensity correction on large 3D stacks
Fast nuclei or cell segmentation using binarization, watershed/voronoi labeling, and label post-processing
Measuring pixel and object statistics on labeled images and exporting results tables
Processing multi-channel time-lapse data with GPU-accelerated projections and transformations
Matrix/graph-based neighborhood analysis such as counting neighbors or filtering between touching labels

name: BioVoxxel Toolbox
description: A toolbox of ImageJ/Fiji plugins and macros for analyzing, filtering, extracting, and separating objects in binary images using extended shape descriptors, overlap/speckle criteria, and improved watershed/EDM-based operations.
input_data: Primarily 8-bit binary (thresholded) images (2D; some tools support 3D stacks); some analyses can redirect to a grayscale image for intensity statistics.
output_data: Results tables/logs, ROI Manager selections, and derived binary/label or color-coded images/stacks (e.g., shape descriptor maps, extracted features, watershed-separated masks).
use_when: Use it when you need more flexible binary-object analysis and selection than standard Analyze Particles, including shape/orientation filtering, overlap-based extraction, speckle-per-object screening, or irregular-feature separation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when your objects are not available as suitable binary masks or when you need general-purpose grayscale/ML-based segmentation rather than binary-object postprocessing and measurement.
typical_use_cases: Filtering and measuring particles by Feret angle, aspect ratio, circularity, extent, compactness, or Feret AR
Generating shape-descriptor map stacks to choose cutoffs and visually identify objects by shape properties
Extracting objects from one binary mask based on overlap with a selector mask (e.g., marker-positive cells)
Classifying larger features by the number/size of contained speckles and exporting ROIs and speckle lists
Separating connected irregular objects using the irregular watershed/label-splitting approach

name: BigStitcher
description: BigStitcher aligns and registers multi-tile and multi-view microscopy datasets of very large size with interactive inspection and optional fusion and deconvolution via BigDataViewer.
input_data: Multi-tile and/or multi-angle microscopy image datasets (2D/3D, multi-channel; e.g. lightsheet, widefield, confocal) imported/defined as a BigDataViewer dataset stored as an XML project on disk (optionally with HDF5-resaved data).
output_data: BigDataViewer XML project with view transformations/registration results, plus optional fused and/or deconvolved exported images (full sample or bounding-box subvolume, at full or reduced resolution).
use_when: Use it to stitch and register large multi-tile and multi-view acquisitions (including irregular grids and missing tiles) with interactive quality control and global optimization.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you only need simple small 2D stitching or you do not need a BigDataViewer/XML-based dataset workflow.
typical_use_cases: Stitching irregular multi-tile lightsheet volumes with missing tiles from intelligent acquisitions
Registering multi-view (multi-angle) cleared-tissue lightsheet datasets and optimizing global alignment
Combining overview scans with higher-resolution acquisitions of selected regions in one project
Fusing aligned views into an exportable image volume using a defined bounding box
Running multiview deconvolution using PSFs assigned/extracted from bead-containing input images

name: BoneJ
description: BoneJ is a suite of ImageJ plugins for analyzing bone images and other porous structures.
input_data: ImageJ images of bone or porous structures (2D/3D).
output_data: Quantitative measurement results and tool-specific derived images.
use_when: Use BoneJ when you need quantitative analysis of bone or porous microstructure in ImageJ, often from 3D datasets such as micro-CT.
do_not_use_when: Do not use BoneJ when you need general-purpose image processing unrelated to bone/porous structure analysis or when required inputs for a specific BoneJ tool are not available.
typical_use_cases: Analyzing bone images in ImageJ
Quantifying porous structure properties in 3D image stacks
Measuring microstructure metrics from micro-CT volumes

name: Bio-Formats
description: Bio-Formats reads and writes proprietary and open microscopy image data and metadata using standardized open formats and the OME data model.
input_data: Proprietary and open microscopy image files (over 160 supported formats), including complex multidimensional datasets (e.g., high content screening, time lapse, digital pathology) with metadata.
output_data: Images opened in ImageJ/Fiji with metadata mapped to the OME data model; written/converted image files in standardized open formats.
use_when: Use it to open or convert proprietary/complex microscopy datasets while preserving multidimensional structure and acquisition metadata.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when working only with simple standard image formats and you do not need microscopy metadata or format conversion.
typical_use_cases: Importing proprietary microscope image files into Fiji with metadata preserved
Converting microscopy datasets to standardized open formats for sharing or archiving
Opening high content screening datasets with many channels/plates/timepoints
Reading time lapse or digital pathology files that standard readers cannot open reliably

name: Labkit
description: Labkit is a Fiji plugin for manual and random-forest-based pixel classification segmentation of 2D/3D microscopy images and timelapse movies, designed to work efficiently on very large datasets with optional GPU/HPC acceleration.
input_data: Single- and multi-channel microscopy images and timelapse movies in 2D or 3D, including very large (terabyte-scale) datasets.
output_data: Segmentation results (pixel-wise label/segmentation image), ground-truth annotations, and a reusable trained pixel-classification model.
use_when: Use when you need interactive manual annotation and/or random-forest pixel classification to segment 2D/3D or time-lapse microscopy data, especially at very large scale.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you need instance segmentation of individual objects or when a simple non-ML method (e.g., thresholding) is sufficient.
typical_use_cases: Training a random-forest pixel classifier from sparse manual annotations
Segmenting multi-channel 2D microscopy images via pixel classification
Segmenting 3D microscopy volumes and curating the results
Processing very large timelapse datasets with GPU acceleration
Running distributed segmentation of huge datasets on an HPC cluster

name: LimeSeg
description: LimeSeg is a Fiji plugin that segments one or multiple 3D objects in volumetric images using a particle-based active contour initialized from seed ROIs.
input_data: 3D image stacks (e.g., TIFF) where objects are labeled on their outline (membrane/edge signal), plus seed ROIs in the ROI Manager (circular ROIs or ROI skeleton).
output_data: Segmented 3D surfaces/meshes (surfels) with optional 2D/3D visualization and a results table reporting per-object measurements (e.g., center, surface, volume, Euler characteristic, free edges).
use_when: Use it when you have a 3D stack with strong boundary signal and can provide one or more seed ROIs to drive active-contour surface segmentation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when the objects are not outline-labeled or when you need segmentation without providing seed ROIs.
typical_use_cases: Segment a single vesicle from one circular seed ROI using Sphere Seg
Segment multiple adjacent cells in parallel from multiple seed ROIs with repelling surfaces
Refine an initial coarse segmentation by decreasing D0 using Coarsen/Refine then Resume Seg
Segment a tortuous structure (e.g., ER) using multiple spherical seeds merged into one surface
Compute per-object surface and volume (and related mesh quality metrics) from the output table

name: SNT
description: SNT is an ImageJ/Fiji toolbox for tracing, visualizing, quantifying, and modeling neuronal and other filamentous morphologies from multidimensional light-microscopy data using manual, semi-automated, and automated routines.
input_data: Multidimensional light-microscopy images (including multichannel and timelapse; grayscale or segmented) and neuronal reconstruction data from major morphology databases.
output_data: Editable tracings/reconstructions, quantitative metrics and specialized statistics (including Sholl and Strahler), and 2D/3D visualizations and interactive 3D scenes.
use_when: Use SNT when you need to reconstruct and analyze neuronal arbors or other filamentous structures from multidimensional microscopy images, including automated tracing and morphology metrics.
do_not_use_when: Do not use SNT if you are on a legacy Fiji/Java 8 installation or if you only need general-purpose image segmentation unrelated to filament tracing and morphology analysis.
typical_use_cases: Semi-automated tracing of neurites in multichannel 3D stacks and timelapse data
Fully automated tracing from grayscale images or from segmented images
Running Sholl and Strahler analyses directly from images without tracing
Automated soma detection followed by neuronal morphology quantification
Out-of-core processing of very large (TB-scale) microscopy datasets for tracing and analysis

name: FeatureJ
description: FeatureJ is a suite of ImageJ plugins for extracting differential features from images.
input_data: images (type and dimensionality not specified in the documentation)
output_data: differential feature images (derived images; specific outputs not specified in the documentation)
use_when: Use it when you need to extract derivative-based (differential) feature representations from an image.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need non-differential feature extraction or when you require documentation of specific algorithms and outputs.
typical_use_cases: Computing differential feature images for downstream analysis
Generating derivative-based feature representations prior to segmentation
Extracting local differential features for quantitative microscopy workflows

name: CSBDeep / N2V
description: Provides CSBDeep-based deep-learning plugins in Fiji for image restoration/denoising (CARE, Noise2Void) and related segmentation tools (StarDist, DenoiSeg).
input_data: Images opened in ImageJ/Fiji (microscopy images; 2D images and 3D stacks).
output_data: Enhanced/restored images (e.g., denoised outputs).
use_when: Use when you want to improve noisy microscopy images using CSBDeep-based deep-learning models such as CARE or Noise2Void.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you only need classical (non-deep-learning) filtering or when required TensorFlow/GPU support is not available.
typical_use_cases: Noise2Void denoising of fluorescence microscopy images without paired training data
CARE-based restoration of low-SNR microscopy images
Preprocessing images to improve downstream segmentation or quantification in Fiji
Using DenoiSeg or StarDist workflows for CSBDeep-related segmentation tasks

name: TurboReg / StackReg
description: StackReg recursively registers a 2D image stack by aligning each slice to the previous one using a chosen geometric transform, with the current slice as the global anchor, via TurboReg.
input_data: 2D ImageJ images or image stacks of admissible types (all data types except RGB-stack and HSB-stack); stacks of RGB color images are admissible.
output_data: Replaces the original image/stack in-place with the registered result (same name and type).
use_when: Use it to align a 2D image or stack by propagating translation, rigid body, scaled rotation, or affine transforms from slice to slice using the current slice as the anchor.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if TurboReg is not installed, if the input is an RGB-stack or HSB-stack, or if you need bilinear or non-rigid/elastic registration.
typical_use_cases: Aligning serial histology sections in a stack using an anchor slice
Correcting drift in a time-lapse stack using translation or rigid-body transforms
Registering a stack with gradual rotation and scale changes between consecutive slices
Aligning a stack of RGB color images by estimating geometry from a grayscale PCA-based representation

name: bUnwarpJ
description: Performs 2D elastic image registration using B-spline deformations with optional landmarks and masks, with bidirectional consistency to enforce pseudo-invertibility (or unidirectional in Mono mode).
input_data: Two open 2D images (8-, 16-, 32-bit grayscale or RGB); optionally point selections/landmarks, polygon masks, or image+mask provided as a 2-slice stack (slice 1 image, slice 2 mask); can also load saved transformation/landmark files.
output_data: Registered (deformed) images; in Accurate/Fast mode two result stacks (source→target and target→source) each containing the warped image, the fixed image, and a warped mask, plus a Results window (and optionally deformation field/grid and logs in verbose mode).
use_when: Use it when two 2D images need non-rigid alignment due to local elastic distortions and you want a B-spline warp optionally constrained by landmarks and/or masks.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for 3D/volumetric registration or when a rigid/affine transform is sufficient.
typical_use_cases: Non-rigidly aligning serial 2D tissue sections with local warping
Registering 2D images with different distortions using landmark constraints
Registering large 2D images using subsampling to speed computation
Applying a previously saved elastic transformation file to a source image

name: ClearVolume
description: ClearVolume is a Fiji volume renderer for interactive 3D visualization of volumetric multi-channel image data, with optional network streaming from a remote source.
input_data: Volumetric 3D image stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ (including multi-channel data) and optionally volumetric data streamed from a remote source via the ClearVolume network client.
output_data: Interactive 3D volume-rendered viewer with adjustable channel LUTs, transparency and rendering quality.
use_when: Use it when you need interactive 3D volume rendering of multi-channel volumetric microscopy data, including optionally viewing volumes streamed from a remote source.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if OpenCL is not installed/working on your system or if you need quantitative analysis outputs rather than visualization.
typical_use_cases: Interactive 3D visualization of multi-channel microscopy z-stacks
Adjusting channel LUTs and transparency to inspect structures in thick volumes
Exploratory viewing of light-sheet or other volumetric datasets in Fiji
Receiving and visualizing volumetric data streamed from a remote microscope

name: SciView
description: Provides interactive 3D visualization and virtual reality viewing of ImageJ2 images and meshes in Fiji using the scenery library.
input_data: ImageJ2 images (including 3D volumes) and ImageJ Mesh mesh data.
output_data: Interactive 3D/VR scene for viewing and interacting with image and mesh data.
use_when: Use it when you need to explore and interact with image volumes and/or meshes in 3D, including virtual reality, within Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when your goal is quantitative measurement, segmentation, or batch processing rather than interactive visualization.
typical_use_cases: Interactive viewing of 3D microscopy image volumes
Inspecting meshes together with underlying image data in 3D
Exploring image and mesh datasets in virtual reality

name: OMERO Insight
description: Integrates Fiji/ImageJ with an OMERO server to open OMERO-hosted images for analysis and save results such as ROIs and measurements back to OMERO.
input_data: Images stored on an OMERO server (including associated metadata/annotations).
output_data: Images opened in Fiji from OMERO, plus ROIs and measurement results saved back to OMERO.
use_when: Use this when your images are managed in OMERO and you want to analyze them in Fiji and store ROIs/measurements back in OMERO.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you are not working with an OMERO server or only need local file import/export.
typical_use_cases: Opening OMERO images in Fiji for manual inspection and processing
Cropping an OMERO image in Fiji and importing the result back to OMERO
Manual segmentation in Fiji and saving ROIs to OMERO
Running ImageJ macros or Groovy scripts on OMERO images and saving measurements

name: N5 Viewer
description: BigDataViewer-based Fiji plugin for viewing N5/Zarr/HDF5 datasets (local or cloud) and optionally extracting cropped subvolumes to ImageJ.
input_data: HDF5 containers (local file only) and N5/Zarr containers on filesystem, AWS S3, or Google Cloud Storage, including multiscale pyramids and OME-NGFF/COSEM or N5 Viewer metadata.
output_data: Interactive BigDataViewer sources and optionally an extracted/cropped ImageJ image (ImagePlus) suitable for saving as formats like TIFF series.
use_when: Use this to interactively browse very large multiscale N5/Zarr/HDF5 datasets (including cloud-hosted) and extract a defined field-of-view into ImageJ.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need to process or write back entire datasets, or if you only need to open small conventional image files.
typical_use_cases: Viewing an OME-NGFF Zarr multiscale volume stored on S3 or Google Cloud Storage
Selecting datasets from an N5/Zarr container tree and opening them as BigDataViewer sources
Opening a local HDF5 BigDataViewer dataset for interactive navigation
Extracting a cropped subvolume at a chosen scale level into ImageJ for export (e.g., TIFF series)

name: Rolling Ball Background Subtraction
description: Corrects uneven illumination by estimating a local background with a rolling-ball algorithm and subtracting it from the original image.
input_data: Intensity images with unevenly illuminated background.
output_data: Background-subtracted image (optionally displayed in a new window).
use_when: Use to remove large-scale background intensity variations before segmentation or intensity measurements.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the features of interest are larger than or similar to the chosen ball radius.
typical_use_cases: Correcting shading in microscopy images
Preprocessing fluorescence images before thresholding
Reducing background gradients to improve intensity quantification
Previewing rolling-ball background subtraction parameter effects

name: Auto Local Threshold
description: Binarises 8-bit images using a choice of local thresholding methods where the threshold is computed per pixel from a circular neighborhood window of a given radius.
input_data: 8-bit grayscale images and stacks in ImageJ/Fiji.
output_data: Binary (0/255) image or stack; optionally a montage of results for all methods (Try all).
use_when: Use it when you need adaptive (locally varying) binarization due to uneven background, illumination, or contrast across the image.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for non-8-bit images without converting to 8-bit, or when global thresholding is adequate (use Auto Threshold).
typical_use_cases: Segmenting bright objects on a dark background with locally varying illumination
Segmenting dark objects on a bright background using the invert (white object on black background) option
Applying local thresholding slice-by-slice to an 8-bit stack
Comparing Bernsen, Mean/Median/MidGrey, Niblack, Otsu, Sauvola and Phansalkar via the Try all montage

name: Auto Threshold
description: Binarises 8-bit and 16-bit grayscale images (including stacks) using multiple global histogram-derived automatic thresholding methods, with the segmented phase shown in white.
input_data: 8-bit or 16-bit grayscale images and image stacks.
output_data: Binary image/mask (0 and 255 for 8-bit output; 16-bit single-slice stack output may use 65535) and optionally a montage of results for all methods plus threshold values in the log.
use_when: Use it when you need automatic global (histogram-based) thresholding of 8-bit/16-bit images or stacks, including options to ignore saturated black/white pixels and to threshold per-slice or using a stack histogram.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need local/adaptive thresholding for uneven illumination or when very large 16-bit images/stacks make true 16-bit histogram processing too slow or memory-intensive (especially with Try all montages).
typical_use_cases: Creating a binary mask from an 8-bit microscopy image using Otsu, Triangle, or another global method
Thresholding a 16-bit image using a true 16-bit histogram (not 256-bin downsampling)
Applying per-slice thresholds across a z-stack or time stack
Applying one threshold to all slices using the stack histogram option
Comparing all threshold methods via Try all and recording the chosen threshold from the log

name: OrientationJ
description: A suite of ImageJ/Fiji plugins for directional image analysis that computes local orientation and coherency from the gradient structure tensor and provides visualization, histograms, vector fields, ROI measurements, and Harris corner keypoints.
input_data: 2D images (typically grayscale) with optional user-defined ROIs.
output_data: Orientation/coherency images (including HSB color survey), vector field maps/overlays, orientation histograms, results tables/log output for ROI measurements, and Harris corner keypoints.
use_when: Use it to quantify and visualize local directionality (orientation and coherency/isotropy) in 2D images and within selected ROIs.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need native 3D volume orientation analysis or when the image has no meaningful directional texture.
typical_use_cases: Colorizing fibrous images with hue as local orientation and saturation as coherency
Computing a coherency-weighted histogram of orientations with min-coherency and min-energy thresholds
Generating a patch-based vector field showing dominant local direction and coherency magnitude
Measuring dominant orientation and coherency inside multiple ROIs and exporting results
Detecting corner-like keypoints using Harris corner detection

name: Shape Filter
description: Filters and extracts particles from a binary image by applying inclusion/exclusion ranges on extended shape descriptors and orientations, producing the same output types as Analyze Particles.
input_data: 8-bit binary (segmented) images with particles/objects; optional redirected grayscale image for intensity statistics.
output_data: Analyze Particles-style outputs for the selected particles (results table; optional masks/ROIs depending on Analyze Particles output settings).
use_when: Use this when you have a binary mask and need to include/exclude objects using additional shape/orientation criteria (e.g., Feret angle, extent, compactness, Feret AR) beyond standard Analyze Particles.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not already a binary particle mask or when you only need the standard Analyze Particles criteria without extra descriptor/angle filtering.
typical_use_cases: Select particles within a specified Feret angle range
Exclude elongated objects using aspect ratio or circularity limits
Filter objects by extent, compactness, or Feret aspect ratio
Measure intensity CoV/skewness/kurtosis per particle via redirect to a grayscale image

name: Measure Stack
description: Measures intensity statistics across all slices in an image stack. Generates per-slice measurements including mean, min, max, standard deviation, and integrated density.
input_data: Image stacks (z-stacks or time-lapse sequences), optionally with ROI selections
output_data: Results table with per-slice measurements (mean, min, max, StdDev, integrated density, area)
use_when: You need intensity measurements across all slices of a stack or time-lapse, rather than measuring only the current slice
do_not_use_when: You only need to measure a single 2D image (use Measure), or you need per-object measurements (use Analyze Particles)
typical_use_cases: Measuring fluorescence intensity changes over time in time-lapse data
Quantifying signal intensity across z-stack slices
Tracking intensity dynamics in ROIs across a movie
Generating intensity profiles along the z-axis

name: CLIJ Assistant
description: An intuitive user interface in Fiji for building custom GPU-accelerated image processing workflows using CLIJ2.
input_data: Images opened in Fiji/ImageJ (typically 2D/3D) for processing with CLIJ2.
output_data: Custom CLIJ2 GPU-accelerated processing workflow and resulting processed images.
use_when: Use this when you want to interactively build and test a GPU-accelerated image processing workflow with CLIJ2 in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you lack a compatible OpenCL GPU environment or you do not want GPU-accelerated processing.
typical_use_cases: Interactive prototyping of GPU-accelerated preprocessing workflows
Chaining and testing CLIJ2 operations with visual feedback in Fiji
Creating repeatable CLIJ2 workflows for later reuse
Learning or teaching CLIJ2 workflow construction via a GUI

name: Colocalization Threshold
description: Sets intensity thresholds for two-channel colocalization using the Costes method and computes Manders coefficients, a 2D histogram/scatter plot, and related statistics.
input_data: Two-channel images (two grayscale channels) for pixel-wise colocalization analysis.
output_data: Per-channel thresholds, Manders coefficients, 2D histogram/scatter plot, and colocalization statistics.
use_when: Use this when you need a reproducible, non-subjective Costes threshold for two-channel colocalization and associated coefficients.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for real analyses if you need a supported/stable tool; use Coloc 2 instead.
typical_use_cases: Determining Costes thresholds for two fluorescence channels
Computing Manders coefficients for two labeled markers in the same image
Creating a 2D intensity histogram/scatter plot for colocalization reporting
Running Colocalization Test after thresholding to assess statistical significance

name: CellCounter
description: Opens a GUI for manually placing and managing typed markers on an image or stack to count objects, export marker coordinates, and report counts and pixel values at marker locations.
input_data: Current ImageJ image, including 2D images, stacks, and hyperstacks (composite color supported).
output_data: Duplicate counter image/stack window for annotation, ImageJ Results table with per-slice counts and totals, measurement table (type, slice, x, y, pixel value), and marker XML files (save/load).
use_when: Use when you need manual, type-specific point annotations and per-slice counts on an image/stack with the ability to save and reload markers.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the built-in Multi-Point Tool provides the needed functionality or when you need automated high-throughput counting.
typical_use_cases: Manually counting different cell classes in a microscopy image using multiple marker types
Counting objects per z-slice in a stack and reporting per-slice totals
Saving marker annotations to XML and reloading them for later review or correction
Measuring the pixel value at each marked location and exporting the results

name: Watershed Irregular Features
description: Separates connected irregular (non-ellipsoid) structures in a binary image using an alternative watershed approach with erosion, convexity thresholding, and separator-size constraints.
input_data: Binary images with connected particles (2D; documentation notes a newer alternative for 2D/3D).
output_data: Binary image with separation lines inserted to split connected objects.
use_when: Use when standard ImageJ watershed fails to properly split touching irregular (non-ellipsoid) objects in a binary mask.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you need reliable 3D separation or when object connections are too broad for watershed-style splitting.
typical_use_cases: Splitting touching irregularly shaped cells in a binary mask
Separating connected non-ellipsoid tissue regions after thresholding
Controlling which particle connections to split by separator-size range
Reducing unwanted peripheral over-splitting by using convexity thresholding

name: Manual Tracking
description: Provides a point-and-click interface to manually track objects across frames in an image stack and export their trajectories and basic motion measurements.
input_data: 2D time-lapse image stacks (multi-frame movies) opened in ImageJ/Fiji
output_data: Trajectory/track coordinate data and derived motion measurements (e.g., distance, velocity), with optional plots
use_when: Use it to obtain accurate tracks for a small number of objects when automated tracking is unreliable or when you need ground-truth annotations.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for high-throughput tracking with many objects or very long movies where manual clicking becomes impractical.
typical_use_cases: Manual tracking of a few migrating cells in a time-lapse stack
Tracking single-particle or vesicle motion in live-cell imaging
Creating ground-truth trajectories to benchmark automated trackers
Measuring per-track displacement and speed over time

name: IJ-OpenCV
description: Bridge between ImageJ and OpenCV for advanced computer vision operations. Enables using OpenCV algorithms (feature matching, contour detection, etc.) from within Fiji.
input_data: Any ImageJ image (ImagePlus) that you want to process with OpenCV algorithms
output_data: Processed images, detected features, contours, or matched keypoints, converted back to ImageJ format
use_when: You need computer vision algorithms from OpenCV (SIFT, SURF, template matching, contour analysis, Hough transforms) that are not natively available in ImageJ
do_not_use_when: The operation you need is already available natively in ImageJ/Fiji, or you can use a dedicated ImageJ plugin for the task
typical_use_cases: Using OpenCV feature matching (SIFT/ORB) for image registration
Applying Hough transform for line or circle detection
Advanced contour detection and analysis using OpenCV methods
Leveraging OpenCV's machine learning classifiers from within Fiji

name: Results to Excel
description: Exports ImageJ Results tables directly to Excel (.xlsx) format. Preserves all measurement data, formatting, and supports batch export of multiple tables.
input_data: ImageJ Results tables from any measurement or analysis operation
output_data: Excel (.xlsx) file containing the results data with formatting preserved
use_when: You need to export measurement results to Excel format for downstream analysis, reporting, or sharing with colleagues who use Excel
do_not_use_when: CSV output is sufficient (use File > Save As), you need programmatic data processing (use CSV/TSV), or no Results table exists
typical_use_cases: Exporting particle analysis measurements to Excel for statistical analysis
Saving intensity measurements from multiple images into a single Excel workbook
Creating formatted Excel reports of image analysis results
Batch exporting results tables from automated analysis pipelines

name: Sholl Analysis
description: Performs automated Sholl analysis on traced neuronal morphologies or directly on segmented 2D/3D images by sampling concentric shells around a user-defined focus and quantifying intersections and related profile metrics with optional curve fitting.
input_data: Binary or segmented grayscale 2D/3D images containing a single isolated neuron (requires a startup ROI: line, point, or multipoint); tracing/reconstruction files (swc, ndf, json, traces); Sholl profiles generated by other software.
output_data: Linear and log-log Sholl profiles (intersection, length, intensity/integrated-density, and volume-based where applicable), Sholl decay, curve-fit descriptors, detailed and summary results tables, plots, and optional annotation ROIs for sampling shells and intersection points.
use_when: Use this to quantify radial arbor complexity or radial signal/object distributions around a defined center using Sholl-style sampling on isolated segmented images or traced reconstructions.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the image is not properly segmented into foreground/background (or contains multiple overlapping cells) or when the structure is not meaningfully characterized by concentric-shell sampling around a focus.
typical_use_cases: Comparing dendritic branching complexity across experimental groups using Sholl profiles and curve-fit metrics
Running Sholl directly on a binary/skeletonized 3D stack of a single neuron without tracing
Separating apical vs basal dendrites using hemishell sampling and plotting separate profiles
Aggregating multiple cells with Combine Sholl Profiles to compute average group profiles and statistics
Measuring radial distribution of fluorescent markers or organelles using integrated-density Sholl profiles

name: CLAHE
description: Applies contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) to enhance local contrast in an image, optionally restricted by an ROI and/or a mask image.
input_data: Currently open ImageJ images (including 8-bit grayscale and 24-bit RGB; multi-channel composite images supported), with optional ROI selection and optional separate mask image chosen from open images.
output_data: Contrast-enhanced image (modifies the active image; supports Undo).
use_when: Use it to improve visibility of local details in images with uneven illumination or low local contrast, optionally limiting the effect to an ROI or mask.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need to preserve original intensity relationships for quantitative measurements or when the block size is not larger than the features you need to preserve.
typical_use_cases: Enhancing local contrast in TEM images to reveal fine structure
Improving visibility in low-contrast microscopy images for visual inspection
Applying CLAHE only within a selected ROI and/or using a mask image
Processing multi-channel composite images with a shared transfer function for consistent display

name: Classic Watershed
description: Performs flooding-based watershed segmentation on grayscale 2D/3D images to produce labeled catchment basins separated by watershed lines, optionally constrained by a binary mask.
input_data: Grayscale 2D/3D images (8-, 16-, or 32-bit); optional binary mask image of the same dimensions.
output_data: Labeled image with catchment basins labeled 1,2,3,... and watershed lines labeled 0.
use_when: Use it to segment a grayscale topographic surface (often an image gradient) in 2D or 3D using classic watershed flooding, optionally within a mask.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it on noisy images with many regional minima unless you pre-filter or plan to merge regions afterward, as it typically over-segments.
typical_use_cases: Watershed segmentation of a gradient image to delineate object boundaries
Separating touching objects in 2D images after smoothing (e.g., Gaussian blur)
Separating touching structures in 3D microscopy stacks using grayscale watershed
Restricting watershed segmentation to a region using a binary mask

name: Distance Transform Watershed
description: Segments 2D/3D grayscale images by marker-controlled watershed flooding from labeled seed regions, optionally restricted by a binary mask, producing labeled catchment basins with optional dams.
input_data: 2D or 3D grayscale input image (8-, 16-, or 32-bit) plus a same-size marker image (labeled regions or binary markers that can be labeled); optional same-size binary mask image.
output_data: Labeled image of catchment basins with optional watershed lines (dams).
use_when: Use it when you have seed markers (manual or automatic, e.g., local minima of a gradient image) and want controlled watershed segmentation in 2D or 3D.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you do not have meaningful markers/seed regions or when you need an unseeded/classic watershed segmentation.
typical_use_cases: Splitting touching objects by running watershed on a gradient image with local-minima markers
Segmenting 3D biological structures using precomputed labeled seed regions
Restricting watershed segmentation to a foreground region using a binary mask
Generating labeled catchment basins for downstream per-object analysis

name: FilamentDetector
description: Detects and tracks biological filaments in Fiji for easy, fast and accurate filament analysis.
input_data: Microscopy images containing biological filaments (specific image type/dimensionality not specified).
output_data: Detected and tracked filaments (specific output formats not specified).
use_when: Use this plugin when you need fast and accurate detection and tracking of biological filaments in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this plugin when you need clearly specified supported input types or defined output formats that are not provided in the documentation.
typical_use_cases: detecting filaments in microscopy images
tracking filaments in time-lapse microscopy data
quick filament detection and tracking for exploratory analysis

name: FLIMJ
description: Analyzes 3D/4D fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIM) datasets in Fiji by fitting exponential decay curves using FLIMLib to generate lifetime and other fit-parameter images.
input_data: 3D or 4D FLIM datasets (X,Y,T with optional spectral dimension) opened in Fiji, typically from SCIFIO-supported files (e.g., .sdt), optionally with a separate IRF/prompt dataset file.
output_data: Fitted parameter images selected by the user (e.g., intensity, a, tau, z, chi-squared) plus per-pixel fit preview plots in the UI, with optional export of the selected images (optionally with LUT).
use_when: Use when you have time-resolved FLIM decay data and need per-pixel, whole-image, or global exponential fitting to obtain lifetime and related parameter maps.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when your dataset is not a 3D/4D time-resolved decay stack (e.g., only 2D intensity images) or when you cannot provide the required axis/time-bin information.
typical_use_cases: Per-pixel single- or multi-exponential fitting to generate tau and chi-squared maps
Global analysis fitting of an entire image to estimate shared lifetime components
Fitting with an IRF/prompt measurement to account for instrument response
Applying kernel binning and intensity thresholding to improve fits in low-photon regions
Batch processing multiple FLIM datasets via scripting workflows

name: Kappa
description: Kappa is a Fiji plugin for curvature analysis.
input_data: Images opened in Fiji/ImageJ (format not specified).
output_data: Curvature analysis results (exact output format not specified).
use_when: Use it when you need to measure curvature from image data in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you do not need curvature measurements or require a different type of quantitative analysis.
typical_use_cases: measuring curvature of structures in microscopy images
curvature analysis of traced contours
comparing curvature measurements between samples or conditions

name: MaMuT
description: A Fiji plugin that combines BigDataViewer and TrackMate components to browse, manually or semi-automatically annotate, and curate lineage-like tracks in massive multi-view image datasets.
input_data: Large multi-view image datasets viewable in BigDataViewer (e.g., BigDataViewer XML/HDF5).
output_data: Spot-and-link annotations (tracks/lineages) with synchronized BigDataViewer views and a TrackScheme lineage graph.
use_when: Use it when you need to browse very large multi-view time-lapse data and build/curate lineage-style annotations manually or with semi-automatic assistance.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need fully automated tracking, since MaMuT does not ship a fully automated tracking algorithm.
typical_use_cases: Manual annotation and curation of cell lineages in large 3D+time datasets
Browsing multi-view light-sheet recordings with synchronized BigDataViewer viewers
Semi-automatic segmentation-assisted extension of lineage branches from single cells
Reviewing and editing spot/link track annotations using the TrackScheme lineage browser

name: MoBIE
description: MoBIE is a Fiji plugin for smooth exploration and sharing of big multi-modal image data together with associated tabular data using BigDataViewer-based views and overlays.
input_data: Large multi-modal image and segmentation datasets (anything Bio-Formats can open, OME-Zarr, BigDataViewer N5, ilastik HDF5) plus tabular data (TSV, CSV, Excel, Parquet, Google sheet links), typically as MoBIE projects or collection tables.
output_data: Interactive BigDataViewer views with multi-modal overlays, table-linked exploration, and shareable points of interest/bookmarks (views/locations).
use_when: Use this when you need interactive browsing and sharing of large multi-modal images with linked tables and overlays in a common coordinate system.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only need simple viewing/analysis of small images without big-data navigation or linked tabular exploration.
typical_use_cases: Exploring correlative light and electron microscopy datasets with overlays
Browsing high-throughput microscopy screening data with linked tables
Viewing large 3D light microscopy time series
Exploring spatial transcriptomics data alongside images and tables
Sharing big image datasets and points of interest via MoBIE projects or collection tables

name: Multi-Template Matching
description: Finds multiple occurrences of one or more template images within a target image using template matching.
input_data: 2D target images plus one or more 2D template images.
output_data: Detected match locations with scores and ROIs marking detections.
use_when: Use this when you have one or more example template crops and want to locate repeated instances of the same pattern in an image.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when objects vary strongly in scale or rotation or when you need full pixel-wise segmentation rather than detections.
typical_use_cases: locating fiducial markers in microscopy images
detecting repeated landmarks for downstream registration
finding repeated structures in assay or plate images
counting objects with consistent appearance via template matching

name: NeuronJ
description: NeuronJ is an ImageJ plugin for 2D tracing and analysis of elongated structures (e.g., neuronal processes) in grayscale images.
input_data: 2D grayscale images; NeuronJ data format (NDF) files.
output_data: Traced paths saved as NeuronJ data format (NDF) files; paths can be converted to ImageJ ROIs via SNT.
use_when: Use it when you need to trace and measure elongated structures in 2D grayscale images and save the tracings as NDF.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for multichannel or 3D reconstructions, when you need SWC export, or when you need hierarchical relationships between traced paths.
typical_use_cases: Tracing neurites in 2D grayscale microscopy images
Measuring lengths of elongated processes in 2D images
Saving 2D tracings as NDF files for later reuse
Opening NDF tracings in SNT and converting them to ImageJ ROIs

name: Time Stamper
description: Adds time stamps (or custom suffix text) to each slice/frame of an ImageJ stack or hyperstack by drawing the text in the current foreground color.
input_data: ImageJ stack or hyperstack (time series).
output_data: Modified stack/hyperstack with the time stamp text drawn into each frame (burned in).
use_when: Use it when you want to permanently burn elapsed time labels (or fixed text) into every frame of a time-lapse stack/hyperstack with control over start time, interval, position, font, decimals, units, and anti-aliasing.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need non-destructive, editable annotations (overlays) or when the image is not a stack/hyperstack.
typical_use_cases: Burning elapsed time stamps onto live-cell time-lapse stacks
Adding time labels with a specified starting time and frame interval
Positioning the stamp using a rectangular ROI to set X/Y location
Drawing only custom text on each frame by setting interval to zero and using the Suffix field

name: TransformJ
description: TransformJ is a suite of plugins for geometrical image transformation and manipulation.
input_data: ImageJ images (2D/3D; stacks supported); specific file formats not specified.
output_data: Geometrically transformed/manipulated images.
use_when: Use it when you need to apply geometric image transformations or manipulations within ImageJ/Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need non-geometric (elastic/nonlinear) warping or feature-based registration not provided by TransformJ.
typical_use_cases: Applying geometric transforms to microscopy images
Transforming and manipulating image stacks
Correcting image geometry prior to downstream analysis

name: DiameterJ
description: Analyzes segmented fibrous microscopy images to measure nanofiber or microfiber diameters along fiber centerlines and outputs diameter distributions with summary statistics, with optional pore-space and orientation analysis.
input_data: 8-bit SEM (or other microscopy) images for segmentation and analysis, or pre-segmented binary (black/white) images; file types: tif, jpeg, png, bmp, gif.
output_data: Binary segmented image; distance-transform image; diameter histogram saved as CSV with mean, standard deviation, median and mode; optional pore (mesh hole) particle-analysis results and orientation histogram image.
use_when: Use this when you have (or can create) a good binary segmentation of a fibrous scaffold image and need validated fiber diameter distributions and summary statistics.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when segmentation cannot accurately represent fibers or when fiber diameters are <10 px, >10% of the smallest image dimension, or >512 px (documented accuracy/limits).
typical_use_cases: Measuring electrospun nanofiber diameter distributions from SEM micrographs
Quantifying microfiber diameters in fibrous scaffold images after threshold-based segmentation
Generating CSV diameter histograms and summary statistics for comparing fabrication conditions
Estimating pore (mesh hole) area distributions from segmented scaffold images
Assessing fiber orientation distributions using the bundled OrientationJ/Directionality workflow

name: SIMcheck
description: A package of ImageJ tools for assessing the quality and reliability of Structured Illumination Microscopy (SIM) data.
input_data: Structured Illumination Microscopy (SIM) image data (e.g., GE Healthcare OMX, Zeiss ELYRA, Nikon N-SIM).
output_data: Quality assessment measurements and diagnostic outputs for SIM data.
use_when: Use it to evaluate the quality and reliability of SIM datasets, including OMX, ELYRA, and N-SIM data.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for non-SIM microscopy data or for general-purpose image processing tasks unrelated to SIM quality assessment.
typical_use_cases: Assessing quality and reliability of GE Healthcare OMX SIM datasets
Evaluating SIM data quality from Zeiss ELYRA systems
Evaluating SIM data quality from Nikon N-SIM systems

name: TAPAS
description: TAPAS is a framework for running shareable, text-defined batch processing and analysis workflows (pipelines) on multi-dimensional images from local files or an OMERO database.
input_data: Multi-dimensional microscopy images loaded from local files via Bio-Formats or from an OMERO server, plus pipeline definitions written as simple text files.
output_data: Processed images and associated analysis results stored alongside the processed images (saved to files or to OMERO).
use_when: Use TAPAS when you need to apply the same modular processing/analysis pipeline to many multi-dimensional images and share the pipeline as a text file.
do_not_use_when: Do not use TAPAS when you only need interactive, one-off processing of a small number of images without a reusable pipeline.
typical_use_cases: Batch processing multi-dimensional light-sheet microscopy datasets with a reusable pipeline
Running processing and analysis on images stored in OMERO and storing results back in OMERO
Applying 2D/3D filtering and auto-threshold/iterative threshold segmentation across many images
Batch labeling objects and computing measurements, signal quantification, and numbering
Computing colocalization and object-to-object distances in a standardized workflow

name: Marker-controlled Watershed
description: Segments 2D/3D grayscale images by flooding an input image from labeled marker regions using a marker-controlled watershed algorithm, optionally restricted by a binary mask and with optional watershed dams.
input_data: 2D or 3D grayscale input image (8-, 16-, or 32-bit) plus a same-size labeled marker image (or binary markers that can be labeled), optionally a same-size binary mask image.
output_data: Labeled image of catchment basins with optional watershed lines (dams).
use_when: Use it when you have marker regions (manual or automatic, e.g., local minima of a gradient) to guide watershed segmentation in 2D/3D and reduce over-segmentation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you do not have meaningful markers or when you need an interactive tool for placing seed points.
typical_use_cases: Separating touching objects by watershed on a gradient image seeded by labeled minima
Segmenting 3D image stacks using precomputed labeled seed regions
Restricting watershed segmentation to a region of interest using a binary mask
Generating watershed dams to explicitly delineate boundaries between adjacent regions

name: Interactive Marker-controlled Watershed
description: Segments 2D/3D grayscale images using a marker-controlled watershed flooding algorithm from a labeled marker image, optionally constrained by a binary mask and optionally producing watershed dams.
input_data: 2D or 3D grayscale image (8-, 16-, or 32-bit) plus a same-size marker image with distinct integer labels per seed region; optional same-size binary mask image; optional binary markers that can be auto-labeled.
output_data: Labeled image of catchment basins with optional watershed lines (dams).
use_when: Use when you have seed markers (manual or computed) and want controlled watershed segmentation in 2D/3D, optionally restricted to a mask region.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you need to interactively place seed points during segmentation (use the Interactive Marker-controlled Watershed plugin instead).
typical_use_cases: Watershed segmentation on a gradient image using precomputed labeled minima as markers
3D marker-controlled watershed of volumetric microscopy data using labeled seed regions
Restricting watershed segmentation to a binary mask region (e.g., within a tissue/organ ROI)
Separating touching objects by enabling watershed dams

name: RATS
description: Computes an adaptive threshold map for a 2D grayscale image using pixel intensities and Sobel-gradient information, producing a binary mask.
input_data: Single-channel 2D grayscale images (8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit); expects bright objects on a dark background (invert if needed).
output_data: Binary (bilevel) mask image with "-mask" appended to the original image name.
use_when: Use when you need robust 2D thresholding of bright objects in noisy images with spatially varying background.
do_not_use_when: Do not use for multi-channel images, for dark objects on bright background without inversion, or when you need 3D/stack thresholding.
typical_use_cases: Creating a binary mask from fluorescence images with uneven background
Segmenting small bright particles in noisy microscopy images
Adaptive thresholding of images where a global threshold fails due to background variation
Batch macro thresholding using run("RATS", "noise=... lambda=... min=...")

name: CiliaQ
description: CiliaQ is a set of three ImageJ plugins for quantifying ciliary shape, length, and fluorescence in 2D, 3D, and 4D fluorescence/confocal microscopy images of cilia.
input_data: 2D/3D/4D fluorescence or confocal microscopy images of cilia (including z-stacks and time-lapse stacks).
output_data: Measurements/tables quantifying ciliary shape, length, and fluorescence.
use_when: Use this when you need quantitative analysis of cilia morphology and fluorescence in fluorescence/confocal 2D, 3D, or 4D datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your images are not fluorescence/confocal microscopy data of cilia or when you need analysis of other structures.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying cilia length in 3D confocal z-stacks
Measuring ciliary fluorescence intensity across experimental conditions
Analyzing cilia shape and length changes over time in 4D time-lapse data

name: SpermQ
description: An ImageJ plugin to analyze the flagellar beat of sperm and sperm steering.
input_data: Microscopy images of sperm (format not specified).
output_data: Quantitative measurements of sperm flagellar beat and steering (format not specified).
use_when: Use this when you need to quantify sperm flagellar beating and steering from microscopy data.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not sperm flagella or you do not need flagellar beat/steering measurements.
typical_use_cases: Measuring sperm flagellar beat characteristics
Quantifying sperm steering behavior
Comparing flagellar beating across experimental conditions

name: CARE
description: Runs CSBDeep/CARE deep-learning models in Fiji to restore microscopy images using an exported network ZIP.
input_data: An open image in Fiji (e.g., tribolium.tif; 2D/3D stacks) plus an exported CSBDeep/CARE model .zip (for running your own model).
output_data: A new image window showing the result of the model execution (restored image).
use_when: Use it when you want to run the CSBDeep demo or apply an exported CARE/CSBDeep model ZIP to an image in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you have not exported a compatible CSBDeep model as a ZIP (or you are not using the provided demo).
typical_use_cases: Running Plugins > CSBDeep > Demo on tribolium.tif to verify the installation
Applying an exported CARE/CSBDeep model ZIP via Plugins > CSBDeep > Run your network to restore a microscopy image
Checking that a trained model executes correctly in Fiji before processing a larger dataset

name: DenoiSeg
description: DenoiSeg is a CSBDeep Fiji plugin that trains and applies a neural network to jointly denoise noisy microscopy images and predict per-pixel probabilities for instance segmentation from a few manually labeled images.
input_data: Noisy microscopy images (2D/3D) for training/prediction plus matching indexed instance label images for a subset (background=0, each object has a unique integer ID), provided as files in X_train/Y_train and X_val/Y_val folders; pretrained models as .bioimage.io.zip.
output_data: Trained model(s) saved to disk (bioimage.io zip) and prediction images with four channels: denoised image plus background/foreground/border probability maps.
use_when: Use when you need robust segmentation on low-SNR microscopy data and can manually create high-quality instance labels for only about 2–10 images to train a model.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you need an out-of-the-box final instance label image without additional postprocessing or when you cannot provide matching indexed instance labels for training.
typical_use_cases: Training a DenoiSeg model from X_train/Y_train and X_val/Y_val to denoise and segment a microscopy dataset
Predicting denoised images and background/foreground/border probability maps from a pretrained .bioimage.io.zip model
Few-shot training (2–10 labeled images) to handle difficult low-SNR fluorescence segmentation tasks
Exporting a DenoiSeg model trained in Python and running prediction in Fiji

name: ilastik
description: Machine learning-based image segmentation, classification, and analysis. Provides interactive pixel classification, object classification, counting, and tracking through a user-friendly graphical interface backed by random forest and other ML models.
input_data: 2D or 3D grayscale or multi-channel fluorescence images where a few manual annotations (painting) can teach the classifier
output_data: Probability maps, segmentation label images, object counts, classification results, exported HDF5 datasets
use_when: You want interactive machine learning segmentation by painting labels, need pixel or object classification, or want to combine multiple image features automatically
do_not_use_when: Images have very consistent, threshold-able intensity (use Auto Threshold); you need deep learning (use StarDist or DeepImageJ); you need a purely scriptable batch pipeline
typical_use_cases: Segmenting cells in complex fluorescence images by painting foreground/background examples
Classifying tissue regions in histology images
Counting objects with overlapping intensities that defeat simple thresholding
Tracking dividing cells across time-lapse datasets

name: DeepImageJ
description: Plugin for running pre-trained deep learning models (TensorFlow, PyTorch via Torchscript) inside Fiji. Compatible with models from the BioImage Model Zoo. Enables inference without coding.
input_data: 2D or 3D images compatible with the selected pre-trained model (varies by model: fluorescence, brightfield, EM, etc.)
output_data: Model-specific outputs: segmentation masks, probability maps, denoised images, feature maps
use_when: You want to apply a published deep learning model to your images without writing code, or want to deploy your own model for others to use via the BioImage Model Zoo
do_not_use_when: No suitable pre-trained model exists for your image type; you need to train a model (use ilastik or external tools); images don't match the model's expected format
typical_use_cases: Running a published nuclei segmentation model from BioImage Model Zoo
Applying a domain-specific denoising model to fluorescence images
Deploying a custom-trained TensorFlow model for batch inference
Testing multiple pre-trained models on the same dataset

name: SAMJ
description: Segment Anything Model (SAM) integration for Fiji. Enables interactive point-and-click or bounding-box-guided segmentation of arbitrary objects in microscopy images using Meta's SAM foundation model.
input_data: Any 2D microscopy or biological image; user provides point prompts or bounding boxes to guide segmentation
output_data: ROI masks or label images for prompted objects
use_when: You need fast interactive segmentation of arbitrary objects without training a model, want zero-shot segmentation of novel object types, or need to segment a few objects quickly
do_not_use_when: You need fully automated batch segmentation of hundreds of images; objects are 3D volumes; you need sub-pixel precision measurements
typical_use_cases: Quickly segmenting a few cells or organelles by clicking on them
Annotating ground truth data for training other models
Segmenting irregular objects that defeat threshold-based methods
Rapid exploration of segmentation quality on new image types

name: Mastodon
description: Mastodon is a large-scale tracking and track-editing framework for large, multi-view microscopy images (typically 3D over time) that supports extensive annotations and lineage tracing.
input_data: Large microscopy image datasets, typically 3D over time, possibly multi-channel and multi-view (e.g., light-sheet).
output_data: Large sets of editable tracking annotations (spots/links) and lineage information.
use_when: Use this when you need to track and curate large numbers of objects and lineages in very large 3D+time, possibly multi-view microscopy datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your dataset is small or you do not need interactive track editing and management of large annotation sets.
typical_use_cases: Cell tracking in light-sheet 3D+time in toto embryo imaging
Lineage tracing in developmental biology time-lapse datasets
Managing and editing large annotation sets generated by automated or semi-automated tracking
Tracking cells in multi-view acquisitions for stem-cell or cell biology experiments

name: JIPipe
description: Provides a graphical pipeline editor in ImageJ/Fiji to build and run batch image analysis workflows as node-based graphs.
input_data: Any image data processable by Fiji; pipelines can handle folders of images, hyperstacks, tables
output_data: Processed images, measurements tables, reports, or any output of the integrated Fiji plugins
use_when: Use it when you want to assemble and execute multi-step ImageJ/Fiji workflows via a visual pipeline interface for batch processing.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you only need a single simple ImageJ operation or when a script/macro-based workflow is more appropriate.
typical_use_cases: Batch-processing folders of images through a multi-step workflow
Building a reproducible analysis pipeline as a visual graph
Running a sequence of ImageJ/Fiji processing steps without coding

name: GDSC SMLM2
description: Provides ImageJ tools for single-molecule localisation microscopy (SMLM) including PSF fitting, localisation results management, drift correction, tracing/clustering, and super-resolution reconstruction and analysis.
input_data: ImageJ images/stacks and folders of images for fitting (time-series), plus localisation results loaded from supported results formats or delimited text files.
output_data: Localisation results in memory and as tables/files, overlays, reconstructed/density images, traced/clustered molecule datasets, drift estimates/corrections, and analysis outputs (e.g., diffusion metrics, FRC resolution).
use_when: Use this for SMLM/PALM/STORM-style datasets when you need 2D Gaussian PSF fitting and downstream localisation-based processing such as filtering, drift correction, tracing/clustering, and reconstruction.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not sparse single-emitter localisations (e.g., conventional widefield) or when you need analysis tailored to other super-resolution modalities rather than localisation microscopy.
typical_use_cases: Fit an image stack or folder with a 2D Gaussian PSF to generate localisation results and a reconstruction
Correct drift in long acquisitions using fiducials, sub-image alignment, reference stacks, or a drift file
Trace or cluster localisations through time to identify molecules and analyse diffusion or residence time
Create localisation density images and inspect localisations in an interactive 3D viewer
Estimate image resolution using Fourier Ring Correlation (FRC)

name: NanoJ-SRRF
description: Super-Resolution Radial Fluctuations (SRRF) algorithm for live-cell super-resolution imaging. Generates super-resolution images from short bursts of diffraction-limited frames with minimal phototoxicity.
input_data: Time-lapse burst of widefield or TIRF fluorescence images (typically 100-500 frames of the same field)
output_data: Super-resolution reconstructed image with improved spatial resolution, SQUIRREL quality map
use_when: You need live-cell super-resolution from standard fluorescence microscopes, have time-lapse bursts of diffraction-limited images, and want to avoid SMLM sample preparation requirements
do_not_use_when: You have fixed samples suitable for STORM/PALM; you need single-molecule localization data; images have very low signal-to-noise
typical_use_cases: Super-resolution imaging of live cells on a standard widefield microscope
Resolving subcellular structures below the diffraction limit in live samples
Rapid super-resolution of dynamic processes without specialized hardware
Comparing live-cell super-resolution across different acquisition conditions

name: NanoJ-Core
description: Core library and drift correction tools from the NanoJ suite for super-resolution microscopy. Provides NanoJ-Livem and cross-correlation-based drift correction for timelapse and SMLM datasets.
input_data: Timelapse fluorescence image stacks affected by stage drift
output_data: Drift-corrected image stack, drift trajectory plot
use_when: You need to correct stage drift in timelapse or SMLM acquisitions before analysis or reconstruction
do_not_use_when: Drift is negligible or already corrected; you need elastic/non-rigid registration
typical_use_cases: Correcting thermal drift in long STORM acquisitions
Pre-processing timelapse data before SRRF reconstruction
Estimating and plotting stage drift in live-cell experiments
Drift correction using fiducial markers

name: NanoJ-SQUIRREL
description: Super-resolution quality control tool. Quantifies the quality of super-resolution images by comparing them to widefield references, producing error maps and resolution-scaled Pearson coefficient metrics.
input_data: Pairs of super-resolution images and corresponding widefield/confocal reference images
output_data: Error maps, resolution-scaled Pearson coefficient (RSP), FRC resolution estimate
use_when: You need to validate the quality of super-resolution reconstructions, compare different SR methods, or detect artifacts in SR images
do_not_use_when: You don't have a reference widefield image; images are not from super-resolution experiments
typical_use_cases: Assessing quality of STORM/PALM reconstructions against widefield reference
Comparing SRRF quality under different acquisition conditions
Detecting reconstruction artifacts in super-resolution images
Reporting quantitative SR image quality for publications

name: Mastodon-DeepLineage
description: Mastodon is a large-scale framework for tracking, track editing, and lineage tracing in very large multi-view 3D+time microscopy datasets.
input_data: Large multi-view 3D over time microscopy image datasets (possibly multi-channel).
output_data: Tracking annotations (spots and links/tracks) and lineage tracing annotations.
use_when: Use this when you need to track cells and curate tracks/lineages in very large 3D+time, possibly multi-view microscopy data.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your dataset is small/simple or you only need basic 2D tracking without extensive annotation and curation.
typical_use_cases: Cell tracking in light-sheet 3D+time embryo imaging
Manual curation and editing of automated tracking results in large movies
Lineage tracing in developmental biology datasets
Tracking in stem-cell or cell biology large-scale time-lapse datasets

name: TrackMate-StarDist
description: Adds StarDist-based detector modules to TrackMate to segment objects in 2D (or 3D via slice-by-slice) for subsequent tracking and feature measurement.
input_data: 2D single- or multi-channel images and time-lapse movies (e.g., TIFF); can also be applied to 3D stacks using a slice-by-slice approach.
output_data: TrackMate spots/objects (with StarDist-derived shapes), tracks, overlays/ROIs for display, and exportable CSV tables (spots and edges).
use_when: Use it when you want to segment objects with StarDist inside TrackMate and then track them and quantify per-object features (including intensities from other channels).
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need true volumetric 3D StarDist segmentation (not slice-by-slice) or when you cannot run/provide a suitable StarDist model for your data.
typical_use_cases: Tracking fluorescent nuclei in 2D time-lapse microscopy
Segmenting nuclei in one channel and measuring reporter intensity in another channel during tracking
Using a custom-trained StarDist model as the TrackMate detector
Processing 3D stacks slice-by-slice to obtain TrackMate detections and tracks

name: TrackMate-Cellpose
description: A TrackMate detector module that runs Cellpose to segment cells in 2D and uses the resulting masks/contours as detections for tracking.
input_data: 2D (time-lapse) images opened in Fiji/ImageJ, including multi-channel composite images with a selected target channel (RGB stacks must be converted); requires a working external Cellpose installation in a conda environment.
output_data: TrackMate spots/detections derived from Cellpose masks (with optional contour simplification), plus TrackMate tracks and computed spot/track features.
use_when: Use this when you want to detect and track cells in 2D with TrackMate using Cellpose pretrained or custom models, especially for difficult cytoplasm or bright-field segmentations.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need 3D segmentation/tracking, when your data are RGB stacks you cannot convert to composite, or when Cellpose/conda is not installed and working independently.
typical_use_cases: Tracking cytoplasm-stained cells in 2D time-lapse using the cyto3 model with an optional nuclei channel
Tracking nuclei in 2D time-lapse using the nuclei pretrained model
Tracking cells in 2D bright-field time-lapse using a custom trained Cellpose model
Segmenting a chosen channel in multi-channel composite images to generate TrackMate detections for downstream tracking

name: TrackMate-Weka
description: A TrackMate detector module that uses a Trainable Weka Segmentation pixel classifier to segment objects in 2D or 3D images for subsequent tracking in TrackMate.
input_data: 2D or 3D images (including time-lapse stacks) opened in Fiji, plus a Trainable Weka Segmentation classifier file.
output_data: TrackMate detections from Weka probability thresholding and the resulting TrackMate tracks and feature tables.
use_when: Use it when standard TrackMate detectors fail and you can provide a trained Weka pixel classifier to detect objects for tracking.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you do not have (or cannot train) a suitable Weka classifier or when a simpler TrackMate detector is adequate.
typical_use_cases: Tracking focal adhesions at the cell periphery in fluorescence time-lapse microscopy
Detecting and tracking objects with high intensity variability using a pixel-classifier-based detector
Running TrackMate tracking after Weka-based segmentation in 2D or 3D time series

name: TrackMate-Ilastik
description: TrackMate detector module that runs an ilastik pixel-classification project to segment objects in 2D or 3D images for subsequent tracking in TrackMate.
input_data: 2D or 3D images/stacks including time-lapse movies opened in Fiji (e.g., TIFF); an ilastik project file (.ilp); requires ilastik installed plus the ilastik Fiji import/export plugin.
output_data: TrackMate spot detections from ilastik segmentation, TrackMate tracks, and spot/track feature tables; exportable results such as tracks, lineage and segmentation.
use_when: Use when you have a trained ilastik pixel-classifier (.ilp) and want to use its segmentation as the detection step inside TrackMate for tracking.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when ilastik (and its Fiji import/export plugin) cannot be installed/configured or you do not have a suitable .ilp model for your data.
typical_use_cases: Tracking Neisseria meningitidis growth in a 2D time-lapse using a pre-trained .ilp detector
Detecting and tracking objects in 3D stacks using ilastik-based segmentation in TrackMate
Previewing and tuning probability threshold and number of classes before running TrackMate tracking
Exporting TrackMate results (tracks, lineage, segmentation) after ilastik-based detection

name: TrackMate-MorphoLibJ
description: Adds a TrackMate detector that runs MorphoLibJ Morphological Segmentation on border/contour-labeled timelapse images to generate objects for tracking.
input_data: Time-lapse image stacks (2D+t) of border/contour-labeled objects (e.g., membrane-stained cells), including multi-channel images with a selectable segmentation channel; supports 8-bit and 16-bit intensity images.
output_data: TrackMate segmented spots/objects with computed features (including area/morphological features), tracks, and TrackMate result tables/plots; can generate an RGB movie with overlay via TrackMate actions.
use_when: Use it when you have contour-labeled objects in a timelapse and want MorphoLibJ morphological segmentation applied per frame inside TrackMate for tracking.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when objects are not defined by contour/border signal or when you need segmentation of filled objects rather than border images.
typical_use_cases: Tracking membrane-labeled cells in Xenopus tissue timelapse movies
Segmenting a preprocessed channel (e.g., median + gaussian blur) and tracking the resulting objects in TrackMate
Filtering out small noisy segments by area and tracking remaining objects with the Overlap tracker
Capturing and exporting an RGB timelapse movie with TrackMate overlay of segmented objects and tracks

name: TrackMate-ExTrack
description: TrackMate action that analyzes existing TrackMate trajectories with the ExTrack algorithm to estimate two-state motion (diffusive vs bound or two diffusive states), state transition kinetics, and per-spot state probabilities robustly under localization noise.
input_data: Existing TrackMate tracks from a current TrackMate session or loaded from a TrackMate XML file (may be without an associated image).
output_data: New TrackMate numerical features including per-spot state probabilities (e.g., P stuck and P diffusive) plus estimated model parameters that can be saved/loaded as a JSON file.
use_when: Use it when you already have TrackMate single-particle tracks and want to infer two-state motion and switching (including parameter estimation) while accounting for localization error.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need to model more than two motility states or when you do not have TrackMate tracks to analyze.
typical_use_cases: Estimating diffusive vs bound fractions and unbinding probability from live-cell single-molecule trajectories
Running ExTrack on TrackMate XML tracks that have no associated image
Coloring spots/tracks in TrackMate by inferred state probability to visualize state switching
Batch scripting ExTrack analysis (parameter estimation and probability computation) on multiple TrackMate XML files

name: TrackMate-YOLO
description: Adds a YOLO-based object detector to TrackMate so you can detect objects as bounding boxes and track them over time in microscopy image sequences.
input_data: 2D or 3D microscopy images and time-lapse stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ
output_data: Tracked objects (spots/segments) with trajectories plus feature tables and track visualizations
use_when: Use it when you need to detect objects in each frame and link them into tracks for microscopy time-lapse data.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you only need static segmentation/measurement without tracking over time.
typical_use_cases: Tracking fluorescent spot-like particles over time
Tracking cells or nuclei in time-lapse microscopy
Analyzing track features such as velocity and displacement
Building and annotating cell lineages with splits/merges

name: TrackMate-Spotiflow
description: TrackMate-Spotiflow is a TrackMate detector that uses a Spotiflow deep-learning model to detect spot-like objects for tracking and analysis in TrackMate.
input_data: 2D or 3D microscopy images and time-lapse image stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ (especially fluorescence images with spot-like objects).
output_data: TrackMate spot detections and resulting tracks, with per-spot and per-track measurements that can be visualized and exported as tables/plots.
use_when: Use this when you want deep-learning-based detection of spot-like objects to build trajectories in TrackMate.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need accurate object contours/masks for cell-like objects or when targets are not spot-like.
typical_use_cases: Tracking sub-resolution fluorescent spots in time-lapse microscopy
Tracking vesicles or organelles as puncta in live-cell imaging
Single-particle/spot trajectory analysis (speed, displacement) in TrackMate
Tracking low-resolution nuclei or cells treated as spots

name: TrackMate-Lacss
description: Adds a TrackMate detector that uses the LACSS deep-learning model to segment cell-like objects and use their contours for tracking in 2D time-lapse images.
input_data: 2D or 3D microscopy images and time-lapse stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ.
output_data: Tracks/lineages plus TrackMate results tables/plots; optionally 2D object contours for detections.
use_when: Use it when you want to detect objects and link them into tracks/lineages in microscopy time-lapse data using TrackMate.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need 3D object contour display/shape-based tracking, since TrackMate object contours are 2D-only.
typical_use_cases: Tracking cells or organelles over time in microscopy time-lapse images
Building and annotating cell lineages with split/merge and gap-closing events
