Exporting per-object and per-track measurements (e.g., intensity, displacement, velocity) for analysis

name: TrackMate-Trackastra
description: A TrackMate tracker module that links already-detected spots or segmented objects over time using the Trackastra transformer-based deep-learning linker.
input_data: TrackMate detections (spots or segmented objects) from 2D or 3D time-lapse image stacks, optionally with 2D object contours.
output_data: TrackMate tracks/lineages (links, splits/merges, gap-closing as supported) plus TrackMate track/spot/edge feature tables and visualizations.
use_when: Use it when you have detections/segmentations in TrackMate and need a more robust linker for complex or crowded time-lapse sequences.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you cannot obtain reliable detections/segmentations in TrackMate or when a simpler classical linker is sufficient.
typical_use_cases: Linking dense fluorescent spot detections across frames in 2D time-lapse microscopy
Tracking nuclei or cells through close encounters and crossings using precomputed detections
Building cell lineages over long time-lapse sequences with division and gap-closing events
Tracking objects in 3D over time after detection/segmentation in TrackMate

name: TrackMate-Helper
description: TrackMate-Helper provides utility functions to automate and streamline running TrackMate tracking analyses, including batch execution and handling of TrackMate results.
input_data: 2D or 3D time-lapse image stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ for TrackMate tracking.
output_data: Tracking results including spot/track features, trajectories, and optional exports of measurements/tables.
use_when: Use this when you need to track objects over time in 2D or 3D microscopy data and analyze or export trajectories and features.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not time-lapse sequences or when objects cannot be reliably detected/segmented in each frame.
typical_use_cases: Tracking fluorescent spots or vesicles over time to measure motion statistics
Tracking cells in 2D or 3D time-lapse microscopy and extracting lineage events
Exporting spot and track feature tables for downstream analysis in other tools
Generating visualizations or movies that follow a single tracked object over time

name: TrackMate-GEFF
description: TrackMate-GEFF exports TrackMate tracking results to the GEFF (Generic Edge/Feature Format) for exchange with other tools.
input_data: TrackMate model/results (spots, links/edges, tracks) from TrackMate analyses in Fiji.
output_data: GEFF file(s) containing tracking graph data (nodes/spots, edges/links) and associated features.
use_when: Use this to exchange TrackMate tracking data with other software or workflows that support the GEFF format.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not have TrackMate results to export or if the destination tool cannot read GEFF.
typical_use_cases: Export TrackMate tracks for analysis in a GEFF-compatible Python workflow
Exchange tracking results between TrackMate and another GEFF-supporting tracking tool
Archive TrackMate spot and link features in a standardized interchange format

name: TrackMate-InTRACKtive
description: Exports TrackMate tracking results to the TrackMate-inTRACKtive interactive web viewer using the GEFF tracking exchange format.
input_data: TrackMate tracking results (spots/objects, tracks, and features) from 2D/3D time-lapse microscopy datasets.
output_data: GEFF tracking data for use in the TrackMate-inTRACKtive web-based interactive viewer.
use_when: Use it when you want to share or explore TrackMate tracking results in the inTRACKtive browser-based viewer via GEFF export.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you need to perform tracking, edit tracks/segmentations, or if you do not have the optional GEFF I/O module installed.
typical_use_cases: Exporting TrackMate tracks to GEFF for interactive viewing in inTRACKtive
Sharing TrackMate tracking results with collaborators via a web viewer
Publishing or presenting TrackMate tracking results with an interactive browser visualization

name: TrackMate-Kymograph
description: Generates kymograph images from TrackMate tracking results to visualize signal changes along object trajectories over time.
input_data: TrackMate tracking results (tracks) from time-lapse image sequences (2D/3D over time).
output_data: Kymograph image(s) representing intensity (or other signal) along track trajectories versus time.
use_when: Use it when you have TrackMate tracks and want a space-time (kymograph) view of signal along the tracked path.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you do not have reliable TrackMate tracks or if the motion is not well represented by a single trajectory for kymograph extraction.
typical_use_cases: Kymographs of vesicle transport along neurites from TrackMate tracks
Visualizing intensity changes along a moving particle trajectory over time
Comparing directional transport dynamics across multiple tracked objects
Summarizing track-associated signal fluctuations in a single space-time plot

name: TrackMate-Oneat
description: Adds a Oneat deep-learning-based detector to TrackMate to detect mitosis/cell-division events in time-lapse microscopy and create corresponding track split events.
input_data: Time-lapse microscopy image stacks (2D/3D over time) opened in Fiji/ImageJ.
output_data: TrackMate detections and tracks with split (division) events, plus TrackMate result tables/exports and lineage views (e.g., TrackScheme).
use_when: Use it when you want TrackMate to automatically detect division events and incorporate them into tracking and lineage reconstruction in time-lapse data.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you do not need division-event detection or when the available Oneat model is not suitable for your image type.
typical_use_cases: Detecting mitosis events in time-lapse cell microscopy and marking division times/positions
Building lineage trees in TrackMate from detected split events
Quantifying division frequency alongside track-based motility measurements

name: TrackMate-Pairing
description: TrackMate-Pairing is a TrackMate extension for pairing tracks/objects between two sets (typically from two channels or sources) to relate corresponding trajectories for downstream analysis.
input_data: 2D or 3D time-lapse microscopy images (image stacks/hyperstacks) opened in Fiji/ImageJ.
output_data: Tracks/trajectories with per-spot and per-track measurements, visualizations/plots, and exportable results tables.
use_when: Use when you need to associate corresponding objects or tracks across two detections/track sets (e.g., two channels) to analyze them jointly.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you only need single-set tracking or when there is no meaningful one-to-one correspondence to establish between the two sets.
typical_use_cases: Pairing tracks from two fluorescence channels to quantify co-movement
Matching detected objects from two segmentation methods to compare results
Linking organelle tracks to nearby cell/nucleus tracks for contextual analysis

name: TrackMateCSVImporter
description: Imports spot and track data from CSV files into TrackMate so they can be visualized, edited, and analyzed in the TrackMate interface.
input_data: CSV files containing spot coordinates and track/link information exported from external tracking tools.
output_data: TrackMate model (spots and tracks) loaded into TrackMate for visualization, editing, and feature/track analysis.
use_when: Use it when you already have tracking results in CSV form from another program and want to inspect or analyze them in TrackMate.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need TrackMate to detect/segment and track directly from image data or when your results are already in a native TrackMate file format.
typical_use_cases: Import trackpy (Python) CSV tracks into TrackMate to visualize trajectories
Load MATLAB-generated spot/track CSVs to compute TrackMate track statistics
Bring externally tracked cell trajectories into TrackMate for manual curation and editing
Compare tracking outputs from different tools by importing each as CSV into TrackMate

name: ELEPHANT
description: Semi-automated cell tracking platform using deep learning (U-Net-based) for detecting and tracking cells in 3D+time datasets. Supports active learning with user feedback to improve detection.
input_data: 3D+time fluorescence microscopy datasets (light-sheet, confocal timelapse)
output_data: Cell detections, tracks, and lineage information with confidence scores
use_when: You need deep learning-assisted tracking of cells in 3D+time data with active learning refinement
do_not_use_when: Data is 2D (use TrackMate); fully manual tracking is preferred; no GPU available for inference
typical_use_cases: Semi-automated tracking in light-sheet embryo datasets
Active learning-guided improvement of cell detection models
3D cell tracking with user-in-the-loop refinement

name: ElastixWrapper
description: Fiji wrapper for the Elastix image registration toolkit. Provides access to Elastix's full registration capabilities including rigid, affine, b-spline deformable, and diffeomorphic registration with multiple similarity metrics.
input_data: 2D or 3D fixed/moving image pairs for registration
output_data: Registered images, transformation parameters, deformation fields
use_when: You need powerful multi-modal image registration, complex deformable registration, or want to use Elastix's full parameter control from within Fiji
do_not_use_when: Simple rigid registration is sufficient (use StackReg); you need elastic consistent registration (use bUnwarpJ)
typical_use_cases: Registering pre/post-treatment 3D fluorescence volumes
Multi-modal registration (fluorescence to EM)
Deformable registration of histology serial sections
Atlas-based registration of tissue samples

name: MOSAIC ToolSuite
description: Collection of image processing and analysis tools from MPI-CBG Dresden, including particle tracking, image segmentation (Region Competition), and background subtraction (PPM).
input_data: 2D/3D fluorescence images for tracking or segmentation tasks
output_data: Particle tracks, segmented regions, background-subtracted images
use_when: You need MPI-CBG's particle tracking algorithms, region competition segmentation, or their background subtraction methods
do_not_use_when: You need deep-learning-based tracking; simple nearest-neighbor linking is sufficient
typical_use_cases: Tracking fluorescent particles in live cells
Segmenting cells via region competition energy minimization
Removing heterogeneous background in fluorescence images

name: Colocalization by Cross Correlation
description: Computes a 2D/3D cross-correlation function between two single-channel images (optionally over time) using an analysis mask to estimate mean association distance, its standard deviation, and CCC-specific statistical measures.
input_data: Two 2D/3D single-channel images (optionally time-series stacks) plus an analysis mask image defining possible localizations; can run without a mask if the whole image is valid.
output_data: Correlogram/radial profile plot, a results table with Gaussian-fit parameters (mean distance, standard deviation, peak height) and statistics (confidence, r-squared), and optional intermediate and contribution images.
use_when: Use this to quantify non-random spatial association and typical separation distance between two signals (including non-zero offsets) while controlling the search space with a mask.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you cannot define an appropriate localization mask (when needed) or when you only need simple intensity-based colocalization coefficients.
typical_use_cases: Estimating average separation between two labeled structures in 3D confocal or super-resolution data
Measuring spatial association of two nuclear proteins using a DAPI/Hoechst-derived nuclear mask
Running masked cross-correlation colocalization across a time-lapse series
Visualizing which pixels in each channel contribute to the measured association using contribution images

name: GDSC
description: Provides ImageJ plugins for single-molecule localisation microscopy (SMLM) to fit PSFs and manage, analyse, and visualise localisation datasets including drift correction, tracing/clustering, and super-resolution reconstruction.
input_data: 2D images and image stacks/series (including folders of images) from SMLM acquisitions; Z-stack calibration images for PSF extraction; delimited text files containing localisation coordinates.
output_data: Localisation results tables/datasets (in memory and/or saved to file), reconstructed localisation density/super-resolution images, overlays/visualisations (including 3D viewer), masks, and traced/clustered molecule tracks with analysis summaries.
use_when: Use this for SMLM workflows where you need to detect and fit single-molecule spots, curate/convert localisation results, correct drift, reconstruct density images, and perform tracing/clustering and related analyses.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for general-purpose segmentation or conventional object tracking where single-molecule PSF fitting and localisation-based reconstruction are not required.
typical_use_cases: Fit SMLM image stacks with a 2D Gaussian PSF to generate localisation tables and reconstructions
Correct stage drift in long SMLM time series using fiducials or alignment methods
Create localisation density (super-resolution) images and overlay localisations on raw images
Trace or cluster localisations through time to identify molecules and analyse diffusion/residence time
Estimate image or reconstruction resolution using Fourier Ring Correlation (FRC/FIRE)

name: clij2
description: CLIJ2 is a GPU-accelerated image processing library for ImageJ/Fiji that provides OpenCL-based operations for filtering, segmentation/labeling, transformations, projections, and measurements on images and related matrices/graphs.
input_data: 2D/3D ImageJ/Fiji images (including binary and label images) and matrix/graph data represented as images and tables (e.g., point lists, matrices).
output_data: Processed images (including binary, label, and parametric images), overlays/ROIs, and measurement/results tables exportable back to ImageJ/Fiji.
use_when: Use CLIJ2 when you want to accelerate classical image processing and measurement workflows on large 2D/3D datasets using an OpenCL-capable GPU.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when no compatible OpenCL GPU is available or when data are small enough that GPU transfer overhead negates speed gains.
typical_use_cases: GPU-accelerated filtering and mathematical operations on large 3D stacks
Binarization, connected-component labeling, and watershed-style segmentation workflows
Generating parametric images and pixel/object statistics from labeled images
Processing multi-channel time-lapse data with GPU-accelerated operations
Working with matrices/graphs to analyze neighborhood relationships between cells or pixels

name: clijx-assistant
description: An intuitive user interface in Fiji for building custom GPU-accelerated image processing workflows using CLIJ2.
input_data: Images opened in Fiji for processing with CLIJ2 on a GPU.
output_data: Processed images generated by the configured CLIJ2 workflow.
use_when: Use it when you want to interactively assemble a GPU-accelerated CLIJ2 image-processing workflow in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when GPU/CLIJ2 is unavailable or when you need features not provided by the assistant interface.
typical_use_cases: Interactive prototyping of CLIJ2 image-processing pipelines
Building GPU-accelerated preprocessing workflows in Fiji
Applying a saved/defined CLIJ2 workflow to open images

name: apoc
description: Experimental Fiji plugin that applies pre-trained APOC pixel and object classifiers on the GPU via CLIJ2 for fast segmentation and object classification.
input_data: 2D/3D ImageJ images (including label images for object classification) and pre-trained APOC classifier files (.cl).
output_data: Result label images and object classification outputs (e.g., updated/produced label images) plus generated ImageJ Macro code snippets via CLIJx-assistant.
use_when: Use this when you already trained an APOC model elsewhere and want to run GPU-accelerated prediction/segmentation in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need to train classifiers in Fiji or you cannot run CLIJ2 on a compatible GPU/OpenCL setup.
typical_use_cases: Apply a pre-trained pixel classifier to segment structures in 2D/3D images on the GPU
Run a pre-trained ObjectSegmenter to generate a label image from an input image
Classify objects in an existing label image using a pre-trained ObjectClassifier
Generate ImageJ Macro code to batch-apply APOC models using CLIJx-assistant

name: BaSiC
description: Background and Shading Correction (BaSiC) for optical microscopy. Retrospectively corrects both spatial shading (uneven illumination) and temporal background bleaching from image data without requiring blank reference images.
input_data: Fluorescence image stacks or timelapse data with uneven illumination or background fluorescence drift
output_data: Shading-corrected and background-subtracted images, flatfield and darkfield correction profiles
use_when: You have uneven illumination (vignetting), background fluorescence that bleaches over time, or need to correct both spatial and temporal background without reference images
do_not_use_when: You have proper flatfield/darkfield calibration images (use standard correction); illumination is already uniform; images are not fluorescence
typical_use_cases: Correcting uneven illumination in widefield fluorescence images
Removing background bleaching in long timelapse experiments
Preprocessing whole-slide scanner images for uniform analysis
Retrospective correction of high-content screening data

name: ModularImageAnalysis (MIA)
description: A Fiji plugin that provides a modular framework (200+ modules) to build automated image and object analysis workflows from loading through processing, detection/tracking, measurement, visualization and export.
input_data: Spatially calibrated 5D images (2D/3D, time, channels) loaded in Fiji, including Bio-Formats-supported microscopy files; can also load objects from ROIs/overlays.
output_data: Processed images, object sets (including label/class images and ROI/overlay exports), measurement results (tables/plots), tracks, and exported datasets/annotations (e.g., ACC, VOC).
use_when: Use this when you need a reproducible, automated, high-throughput workflow that chains multiple image/object analysis steps and exports measurements or annotations.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only need a single simple interactive ImageJ operation and do not want to build or run a workflow.
typical_use_cases: Batch processing a folder of microscopy images to detect objects and export measurements
Building a spatially calibrated 5D workflow to measure objects in pixel and physical units
Running tracking within a larger pipeline using TrackMate or built-in tracking modules
Applying Weka pixel/object classification as part of an automated analysis workflow
Exporting object annotations or datasets (e.g., VOC, ACC) for downstream tools

name: Tissue Analyzer
description: Plugin for automated segmentation and analysis of epithelial tissues. Detects and segments cells in fluorescent images of epithelial monolayers and measures cell shapes, sizes, and topological properties.
input_data: 2D fluorescence images of epithelial cell membranes or junctions (e.g., E-cadherin, ZO-1 staining)
output_data: Segmented cell boundaries, cell shape measurements (area, perimeter, eccentricity), neighbor relationships
use_when: You need to segment and analyze cells in epithelial tissue images, measure cell shape statistics, or study tissue topology
do_not_use_when: Cells are not epithelial/packed; images are 3D or timelapse; you need instance segmentation of isolated cells
typical_use_cases: Quantifying cell packing in Drosophila wing epithelium
Measuring cell shape changes during tissue morphogenesis
Analyzing polygon statistics of epithelial cell sheets

name: PTBIOP
description: Collection of plugins and scripts from the BioImaging and Optics Platform (BIOP) at EPFL. Includes tools for microscopy data management, analysis automation, QuPath bridges, and various utility functions for core facilities.
input_data: Varied microscopy images; specific tools depend on the workflow used
output_data: Analysis results, converted files, quality metrics depending on selected tools
use_when: You need EPFL BIOP-developed tools for microscopy data handling, QuPath integration, or core facility automation workflows
do_not_use_when: You need a specific single-purpose plugin; BIOP tools are not relevant to your workflow
typical_use_cases: Automating image analysis at a microscopy core facility
Bridging Fiji and QuPath for pathology workflows
Using BIOP utility macros for data management

name: Zeiss Quick Start Reader
description: Plugin from EPFL BIOP for quickly reading Zeiss CZI files in Fiji with simplified metadata handling. Provides faster loading of CZI files compared to the full Bio-Formats reader for certain workflows.
input_data: Zeiss CZI microscopy image files
output_data: ImagePlus with image data from CZI file
use_when: You need a quick way to open Zeiss CZI files in Fiji, especially for batch processing where full Bio-Formats metadata is not required
do_not_use_when: You need full metadata extraction (use Bio-Formats); files are not CZI format
typical_use_cases: Batch opening Zeiss CZI files for automated analysis
Quick preview of CZI datasets without full metadata loading

name: MIST
description: Microscopy Image Stitching Tool (MIST) from NIST. Provides accurate and reproducible 2D image stitching for tiled microscopy acquisitions using phase correlation with statistical validation.
input_data: Set of overlapping 2D microscopy tile images with known or estimated grid layout
output_data: Stitched full-field image, translation vectors, confidence statistics per tile
use_when: You need accurate 2D tile stitching with statistical validation of overlap translations, or reproducible stitching for publication-quality results
do_not_use_when: Data is 3D multi-view (use BigStitcher); tiles have no overlap; simple grid stitching suffices (use Grid/Collection Stitching plugin)
typical_use_cases: Stitching large tiled fluorescence scans of tissue sections
Creating whole-slide overview images from tiled confocal data
Reproducible stitching pipelines for high-content imaging

name: NucleusJ
description: Plugin for quantifying 3D nuclear morphology and chromatin organization. Segments nuclei in 3D confocal images and measures nuclear shape, volume, and internal chromatin distribution parameters.
input_data: 3D confocal fluorescence images of stained nuclei (DAPI or similar)
output_data: Segmented nuclei as 3D label images, Results table with nuclear volume, surface area, sphericity, chromatin compaction metrics
use_when: You need to quantify 3D nuclear shape parameters and chromatin distribution in confocal z-stacks
do_not_use_when: Images are 2D (use particle analysis); you need tracking; nuclei are not the primary object of interest
typical_use_cases: Measuring nuclear volume and shape in different cell types
Quantifying chromatin compaction changes during differentiation
Comparing nuclear morphology between conditions

name: MiToBo
description: Microscope Image Analysis Toolbox. Collection of algorithms for microscopy image analysis including cell detection, segmentation, shape analysis, and neuron morphology tools.
input_data: 2D/3D fluorescence microscopy images for cell or neuron analysis
output_data: Detected cells, shape measurements, neuron morphology parameters
use_when: You need MiToBo's specific algorithms for cell detection, neuron morphology, or shape analysis
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji analysis tools suffice; you need deep-learning-based detection
typical_use_cases: Detecting and measuring cell shapes in fluorescence images
Analyzing neuron morphology from microscopy data
Batch processing with MiToBo pipeline tools

name: BigDataProcessor
description: BigDataProcessor2 is a Fiji plugin for interactive, lazy loading and processing of TB-sized image datasets with BigDataViewer visualization and script recording support.
input_data: TIFF or HDF5 file series (including predefined EM TIFF plane, Leica DSL TIFF, Luxendo HDF5, Viventis TIFF) and microscopy formats readable via Bio-Formats.
output_data: BigDataViewer views plus saved processed datasets written as TIFF stacks/plane series or HDF5 chunked multiresolution datasets; optional JSON drift track files.
use_when: Use it to interactively inspect and build processing workflows for very large (hundreds of GB to TB) microscopy datasets without loading them fully into RAM.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need standard in-memory ImageJ processing on small datasets or when your data cannot be opened via TIFF/HDF5 series or Bio-Formats.
typical_use_cases: Browsing and preprocessing TB-scale light-sheet time-lapse datasets with lazy loading
Cropping and binning large 4D datasets before exporting to TIFF or HDF5
Correcting drift by creating and applying a manual 3D track over time
Aligning multi-channel datasets to compensate chromatic or camera offsets
Applying an affine transform to correct non-orthogonal acquisition geometry

name: Morphology (Landini)
description: Collection of morphological image analysis plugins by Gabriel Landini, including advanced binary operations, color segmentation, edge detection, and mathematical morphology tools not in standard ImageJ.
input_data: Binary, grayscale, or color 2D images
output_data: Morphologically processed images, edge images, segmented regions
use_when: You need advanced morphological operations not available in standard ImageJ, such as granulometry, top-hat transforms, or color-based segmentation
do_not_use_when: Standard MorphoLibJ or built-in ImageJ morphological tools are sufficient
typical_use_cases: Advanced binary morphological operations
Color-based image segmentation
Granulometry analysis of textures

name: CMTK Registration
description: Fiji wrapper for the Computational Morphometry Toolkit (CMTK) for brain image registration. Primarily used for Drosophila brain registration to standard atlases.
input_data: 3D fluorescence images of Drosophila brains or similar anatomical structures for atlas registration
output_data: Registered brain images aligned to standard atlas space, transformation parameters
use_when: You need to register Drosophila or similar brain images to a standard atlas for comparative neuroanatomy
do_not_use_when: Images are not neuroanatomy data; you need general-purpose deformable registration (use ElastixWrapper or bUnwarpJ)
typical_use_cases: Registering confocal Drosophila brain images to JRC2018 atlas
Comparing neuron morphologies across specimens
Mapping neuron locations to atlas coordinates

name: Mastodon-Tomancak
description: Mastodon is a large-scale framework for tracking and editing cell tracks in very large 3D+time, multi-view microscopy datasets.
input_data: Large multi-view microscopy images (3D over time), possibly multi-channel.
output_data: Tracking annotations such as cell tracks and lineage information within Mastodon projects.
use_when: Use this for tracking and curating cell lineages in very large 3D+time multi-view microscopy datasets (e.g., light-sheet).
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not need large-scale tracking/track-editing or are not working with 3D+time microscopy data.
typical_use_cases: Tracking cells in light-sheet 3D+time embryo imaging datasets
Editing and curating tracks in large multi-view time-lapse movies
Lineage tracing in developmental or stem-cell biology datasets

name: BACMMAN
description: BACMMAN is a modular software for automated pre-processing, segmentation, tracking and measurement of bacteria and intracellular spots in high-throughput 2D/3D time-series multi-position microscopy images (especially mother machine data).
input_data: High-throughput 2D/3D time-series multi-position microscopy images (phase-contrast/transmitted light and fluorescence) in Bio-Formats-supported file formats.
output_data: Interactive kymographs with object contours and tracking links, plus exported tabular measurement files (one per object class; one row per object per frame).
use_when: Use it when you need an automated, configurable pipeline to segment and track bacteria (and intracellular spots) and export per-frame measurements from large time-lapse microscopy datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for non-microscopy data or for simple single-image segmentation tasks where a full configurable pre-processing/segmentation/tracking pipeline is unnecessary.
typical_use_cases: Segmenting and tracking bacteria in mother machine time-lapse experiments
Detecting and tracking intracellular fluorescent spots within bacteria over time
Exporting per-object per-frame measurements for downstream analysis in R or Python
Manually curating segmentation contours and track links using interactive kymographs

name: Mars
description: Mars is an ImageJ2/Fiji framework that provides commands to find, fit, track, and characterize single molecules and store results in reproducible Molecule Archives.
input_data: Bioimage data from multicolor single-molecule TIRF microscopy and DNA flow stretching experiments (ImageJ2/Fiji images).
output_data: Molecule Archives containing per-molecule records with UUIDs and associated image/biomolecule metadata for querying, merging, and downstream analysis/plotting.
use_when: Use Mars when you need a reproducible workflow to detect/fit/track single molecules and manage results and metadata in scalable archives.
do_not_use_when: Do not use Mars when your images are not single-molecule datasets or you do not need archive-based storage, querying, and reproducible reporting.
typical_use_cases: Finding and fitting single-molecule spots in multicolor TIRF images
Tracking single molecules across time-lapse TIRF movies
Generating Molecule Archives from primary image analysis with per-molecule metadata and UUIDs
Merging and processing very large single-molecule datasets using Molecule Archives
Querying subsets of molecules by arbitrary properties for downstream plotting in Fiji or Python/Jupyter

name: ImagingFCS
description: Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS) analysis plugin. Performs spatial and temporal FCS, FCCS, and imaging FCS analysis directly on fluorescence microscopy image stacks.
input_data: Fluorescence microscopy image stacks acquired for FCS (fast timelapse, typically TIRF or confocal point measurements)
output_data: Autocorrelation curves, diffusion coefficients maps, flow velocity maps, FCS parameter images
use_when: You perform imaging FCS experiments and need to compute correlation functions, diffusion maps, or flow measurements from fluorescence image stacks
do_not_use_when: Data is not FCS-type (too slow acquisition, not point measurements); you need single-particle tracking instead
typical_use_cases: Measuring diffusion coefficient maps of membrane proteins with imaging FCS
Dual-color FCCS analysis of protein interactions
Flow measurements in microfluidic channels via FCS

name: Ultrack
description: Ultrack is a Fiji plugin that connects to the Python Ultrack framework to perform large-scale 2D+t and 3D+t cell tracking under segmentation uncertainty using segmentation selection.
input_data: Microscopy time-lapse image sequences (2D+t or 3D+t) and optionally user-provided foreground and edge images or labeled segmentation images (e.g., from StarDist, Cellpose, MicroSAM).
output_data: Cell tracks and selected segments, with results viewable via TrackMate.
use_when: Use this when you need scalable cell tracking on large 2D+t or 3D+t microscopy datasets, especially when segmentation is uncertain or provided as multiple hypotheses.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot install and run the required Python Ultrack environment (e.g., via conda) or you need full parameter control only available in Python.
typical_use_cases: Tracking cells in terabyte-scale 3D+t fluorescence microscopy datasets
Tracking directly from the raw image using the Auto Detection From Image workflow
Tracking using user-provided label segmentations from StarDist, Cellpose, or MicroSAM
Tracking using user-provided foreground and edge images

name: Multi-Template-Matching
description: Finds multiple occurrences of one or more template images within an ImageJ/Fiji image using template matching (via OpenCV).
input_data: Images opened in ImageJ/Fiji plus one or more template images (2D).
output_data: Detected match locations (e.g., ROIs and/or a results table with coordinates and match scores).
use_when: Use when you need to locate repeated instances of a known template pattern in an image.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the target varies strongly in scale/rotation/appearance such that simple template matching is unreliable.
typical_use_cases: Detecting repeated structures in microscopy images using a reference template
Finding multiple instances of a particle or feature with a known appearance
Screening images for occurrences of a specific pattern and exporting match coordinates

name: 3D ImageJ Suite
description: A suite of ImageJ/Fiji plugins and a core library for 3D image stack processing, segmentation, 3D object/ROI management, visualization, and quantitative object-based analysis (geometry, intensity, distances, colocalization, spatial statistics).
input_data: 3D image stacks (calibrated microscopy z-stacks) including grayscale intensity images and labeled/segmented images.
output_data: Processed/filtered stacks, binary or labeled segmentation images, 3D ROI/object sets managed in 3D Manager (including .3droi), visual overlays/3D Viewer renderings, and results tables (geometry/shape/intensity/distances/colocalization/statistics).
use_when: Use it when you need automation-friendly 3D segmentation and object-based measurements/relationships from microscopy z-stacks, including managing objects in 3D Manager.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you only have 2D images or when your segmentation requires machine-learning/deep-learning methods for complex tissues (e.g., Weka/StarDist/Cellpose).
typical_use_cases: Seeded 3D watershed segmentation to separate touching objects in fluorescence z-stacks
3D spot segmentation using local maxima seeds with local thresholding and optional Gaussian fitting
Managing, saving/loading, and visualizing labeled 3D objects as 3D ROIs in 3D Manager and 3D Viewer
Measuring 3D object geometry/shape and intensity (centroid, volume, surface, compactness/sphericity, mean/min/max)
Computing object-to-object relationships such as distances (center/border/Hausdorff) and object-based colocalization/surface contact

name: 3Dscript
description: 3Dscript volume-renders 3D multi-channel image stacks and generates scripted animations of transformations and rendering parameters as a rendered movie stack.
input_data: 3D (and possibly 4D) multi-channel ImageJ/Fiji image stacks with per-channel display min/max set before rendering.
output_data: A new ImageJ movie/stack window containing rendered 2D frames (exportable e.g. as AVI).
use_when: Use this when you want reproducible, text-scripted 3D volume-rendered animations (rotation/translation/zoom and rendering-property changes) from an ImageJ stack.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for quantitative analysis tasks (e.g., segmentation/measurement) or for non-volumetric 2D images.
typical_use_cases: Render a 3D fluorescence stack with independent or combined transparency ray-casting
Create a scripted 360-degree rotation animation for presentations or figures
Animate dynamic zoom/translation while keeping a structure centered under the mouse pointer
Animate scroll-through by changing bounding box/clipping parameters over time

name: 4d-Tools
description: 4d-Tools imports several legacy 4D microscopy dataset types into Fiji as (virtual) hyperstacks and provides an experimental on-the-fly maximum-intensity projection hyperstack viewer.
input_data: Legacy 4D movie files (.avi, .mov, .mp4), classic 4d Viewer folders of .mov files, Perkin-Elmer UltraView LCI AVI series and TIFF series, Imaris .ims files, and Micro-Manager 1.4/2.0 dataset directories.
output_data: Virtual hyperstacks (and in some cases RAM-loaded hyperstacks) plus an experimental Hyperstack Projector display window for live maximum-intensity projections over a Z-range.
use_when: Use this when you need to import legacy 4D movie-based datasets, Imaris IMS, or Micro-Manager acquisitions into Fiji as hyperstacks for viewing and downstream analysis.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when standard Fiji/Bio-Formats import already works for your data or when Bio-Formats import would require loading an unmanageably large dataset into RAM.
typical_use_cases: Importing a classic 4D AVI plus matching focal-plane text file into a virtual hyperstack
Importing QuickTime .mov or MPEG-4 .mp4 4D movies into a virtual hyperstack via FFmpeg
Importing an Imaris .ims file (highest-resolution dataset) into Fiji as a virtual hyperstack
Importing a Micro-Manager 1.4/2.0 acquisition directory into a hyperstack using its metadata
Playing a live maximum-intensity projection over a user-defined Z-slice range with Hyperstack Projector

name: ActogramJ
description: ActogramJ loads column-based ASCII activity time-series files, displays them as actograms, and provides tools to compute circadian metrics and export actograms to PDF.
input_data: ASCII text actogram files containing activity data in columns (optionally with extra header/footer text), with user-specified row/column ranges and sampling interval.
output_data: Rendered actogram displays with optional environmental bars and markers, calculated periodogram/period and acrophase/on-off results (with optional lists export), derived calculated actograms (average/sum/smoothed), and exported PDF files.
use_when: Use this when you have circadian activity recordings in column-formatted ASCII files and need to visualize actograms and quantify period/phase and activity on-off timing.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not available as column-based ASCII time-series (e.g., primarily image stacks) or when you need general-purpose time-series analysis beyond actogram/periodogram workflows.
typical_use_cases: Loading multi-subject activity recordings (one column per animal) and browsing individual actograms
Adding environmental bars (e.g., light on/off intervals) to contextualize actograms
Estimating freerunning period manually with the period tool or automatically via periodograms (Fourier, Chi-Square, Lomb-Scargle)
Computing and visualizing acrophases and activity on-/offsets across successive periods
Exporting the currently displayed actograms (with calibration and environmental bars) to a single PDF

name: AdipoQ
description: Provides Fiji/ImageJ workflows to segment and quantify adipocyte morphology in cultured-cell fluorescence images and adipose tissue histology sections.
input_data: Microscopy images of adipocytes, including fluorescence images of cultured cells and histology section images (e.g., H&E).
output_data: Measurement results tables and processed/segmented/annotated images produced by the selected workflow.
use_when: Use this when you need guided, standardized adipocyte segmentation and morphometric quantification in Fiji for either fluorescence cultured-cell data or histology sections.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when analyzing non-adipocyte samples or when you need a general-purpose segmentation/measurement tool not tailored to adipocyte workflows.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying adipocyte size and morphology in H&E-stained adipose tissue sections
Measuring adipocyte morphology in fluorescence images of cultured adipocytes
Running the provided example datasets and walkthrough guides for adipocyte morphometry

name: ALPACA
description: A collection of macros to study cilia.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need ALPACA's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying ALPACA tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: AMPT
description: AMPT (Aquatic Mammal Photogrammetry Tool) is an ImageJ/Fiji plugin that guides users through photogrammetric measurements of aquatic mammals from aerial images and exports the results.
input_data: Aerial photographs/images opened in ImageJ/Fiji (any ImageJ-readable image format) with required scale/metadata for photogrammetry.
output_data: Measurement results in ImageJ Results tables and exported CSV files, plus associated ROIs/annotations.
use_when: Use this when you need a standardized, guided workflow to measure lengths and body-width profiles of aquatic mammals from aerial imagery.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you lack appropriate aerial images and the scale/metadata needed for photogrammetric conversion to real-world units.
typical_use_cases: Measuring total length of whales from drone images
Extracting body width profiles for morphometric analysis
Exporting measurement tables to CSV for downstream statistics
Applying a consistent measurement protocol across multiple analysts

name: AngioTool
description: Segments and quantifies vessel networks in microscopy images by tuning diameter/intensity-based vessel selection and producing an overlay with outlines, skeleton, and branch points plus an Excel results file.
input_data: Microscopy images opened via the Open Image function (specific file formats not stated).
output_data: Result image with overlay (vessel outline, skeleton, branching points) and an appended Excel .xls file containing analysis settings and computed parameters.
use_when: Use this when you need interactive tuning and quantification of tubular vessel networks (including skeleton and branching points) from microscopy images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the vessel selection overlay cannot be tuned to match the vessels or when the structures are not vessel-like tubular networks.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying vessel network morphology and branching in angiogenesis assay images
Adjusting diameter and intensity thresholds to capture faint vessels while excluding bright nonspecific pixels
Removing small background particles and filling holes to improve vessel selection before analysis
Appending per-image measurements and analysis settings from multiple sessions into a single .xls file

name: Animove
description: Animove is a Fiji plugin to process zebrafish behavior videos by converting them to grayscale, generating heatmaps, tracking adults or larvae, and computing related activity metrics.
input_data: Zebrafish behavior video files (including RGB videos, which are converted to single-channel grayscale using OpenCV).
output_data: Heatmap images/videos, tracking results, and quantitative analysis outputs (e.g., tracking frequency and thigmotaxis-related measurements).
use_when: Use this for adult or larval zebrafish behavior experiments when you need tracking plus heatmap generation and derived metrics inside Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you must keep separate RGB channels or when the input is not zebrafish behavior video data.
typical_use_cases: Tracking adult zebrafish in tank, gutter/track, or plus-maze recordings
Tracking larval zebrafish in six-well plate recordings
Generating binary heatmaps (larvae) or summed heatmaps (adults) from activity videos
Extracting tracking frequency data and computing thigmotaxis-related metrics

name: Archipelago
description: Fiji Archipelago enables distributing Fiji/ImageJ computations across multiple networked computers as a cluster.
input_data: Any Fiji/ImageJ data used by the submitted jobs; for included workflows, TrakEM2 alignment projects and 2D serial-section images plus Trainable Weka Segmentation .model classifier files.
output_data: The same outputs as the executed Fiji workflows (e.g., TrakEM2 alignment results and Weka segmentation outputs), plus optional saved cluster configuration XML files.
use_when: Use this when you need to speed up Fiji processing by running jobs in parallel on multiple machines via a managed cluster.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this on untrusted networks if you must rely on insecure socket communication or when you cannot meet requirements like matching Fiji versions, SSH key authentication, and shared storage for file transfer.
typical_use_cases: Running TrakEM2 least-squares and elastic alignment using a cluster
Batch-running Trainable Weka Segmentation 2D on many serial-section images using a saved .model classifier
Adding volunteer or on-the-fly nodes to scale long-running Fiji computations
Submitting Serializable Callables/Runnables to a cluster ExecutorService from custom Fiji plugins or scripts

name: AxoNet
description: AxoNet is a Fiji/ImageJ plugin that uses a deep-learning model to estimate total axon counts from 100x light-microscope images of optic nerves by integrating a pixelwise axon density map.
input_data: 2D 100x light-microscope images of optic nerves (any size/shape; optionally cropped and cleared outside to black).
output_data: Estimated total axon count and related results reported in the Console/log (interpreted via the AxoNet Output Explanation PDF).
use_when: Use this to obtain an automated axon count estimate from 100x light-microscope optic nerve images (validated for rat and non-human primate, including damaged nerves).
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for images that are not 100x light-microscope optic nerve preparations or for species/preparations outside the validated domain.
typical_use_cases: Estimating total axon counts from 100x rat optic nerve micrographs
Estimating total axon counts from 100x non-human primate optic nerve micrographs
Quantifying axon loss in glaucoma models from damaged or variable-quality nerve images
Running axon count estimation after cropping the nerve and clearing outside background

name: BACMMAN-DL
description: BACMMAN-DL provides deep-learning modules within BACMMAN to segment and track bacteria and intracellular spots in high-throughput 2D/3D time-series multi-position microscopy images.
input_data: High-throughput 2D/3D time-series multi-position microscopy images (phase-contrast, fluorescence, transmitted light) in Bio-Formats-supported formats.
output_data: Interactive kymographs with object contours, tracking links and measurements, plus exported tabular measurement files (one per object class; one row per object per frame).
use_when: Use when you want to run deep-learning-based segmentation/tracking (e.g., transmitted-light bacteria segmentation) as part of a BACMMAN pipeline for time-lapse microscopy datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you are not using BACMMAN or your dataset is not a microscopy time-series/multi-position dataset supported via Bio-Formats.
typical_use_cases: Segmenting and tracking bacteria in mother machine time-lapse datasets (phase-contrast or fluorescence)
Deep-learning-based segmentation of bacteria imaged in transmitted light (TaLiSSman) within BACMMAN
Segmenting and tracking intracellular fluorescent spots inside bacteria over time
Exporting per-object per-frame measurement tables for downstream analysis in R or Python

name: BACMMAN-DL-GPU
description: Enables GPU-accelerated deep-learning modules in BACMMAN for automated segmentation and tracking in high-throughput microscopy time-series datasets.
input_data: High-throughput 2D/3D time-series multi-position microscopy images (phase-contrast/transmitted light and fluorescence) in Bio-Formats-supported formats.
output_data: Segmentation and tracking results visualized as interactive kymographs with object contours/links, plus exported per-object-class tabular measurement files.
use_when: Use when running BACMMAN deep-learning-based modules (e.g., transmitted-light bacteria segmentation) and you want GPU acceleration.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you are not using BACMMAN deep-learning modules or you cannot provide a compatible GPU/CUDA/TensorFlow environment.
typical_use_cases: segmenting and tracking bacteria in mother-machine phase-contrast time-lapse datasets with GPU acceleration
segmenting bacteria in transmitted-light stacks using TaLiSSman within BACMMAN on a GPU
segmenting and tracking intracellular fluorescent spots in bacteria time-series data
exporting per-frame per-object measurements tables for downstream analysis in R or Python

name: BAR
description: BAR is an optional Fiji update site providing a curated collection of ImageJ macros, scripts, and plugins for data analysis, annotation, and segmentation tasks, accessible via a dedicated BAR menu.
input_data: Any ImageJ-supported images and image stacks opened in Fiji; also folders of image files for batch-processing snippets.
output_data: Varies by routine, including processed images, ROIs/ROI Manager content, plots, and results tables; also saved TIFF outputs for some batch processors.
use_when: You want a curated set of task-oriented ImageJ routines (analysis, annotation, segmentation, tools) installed via an update site and accessible from a single menu.
do_not_use_when: You need one specific, well-defined algorithm with fixed inputs/outputs rather than a broad collection of heterogeneous scripts and plugins.
typical_use_cases: Counting spots using LoG-DoG Spot Counter on microscopy images
Generating plot profiles (multichannel, ZT-axis) and exporting results
Applying segmentation utilities such as threshold-to-ROI or removing isolated pixels
Annotating images by combining orthogonal views or color-coding ROIs
Batch-processing a folder of images and saving processed outputs as TIFF

name: Batch Tools
description: Provides a toolbar for batch processing and analysis of light microscopy images, including Bio-Formats extraction, channel merging/composite conversion, Z-projection, export, and cell area/intensity/count measurement.
input_data: Common proprietary life science microscopy files readable by Bio-Formats (e.g., .nd2, .lif, .lsm) and TIFF images (including per-channel TIFFs with _chXX suffix).
output_data: Exported TIFF/PNG images, composite images and max-intensity projections, and per-image and summary CSV measurement tables (optionally ROI review images).
use_when: Use this when you need to batch extract, standardize channels/contrast, export, and run repeated cell/object measurements across tens to hundreds of microscopy images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for very large/high-throughput datasets beyond typical light microscopy sizes or when you need fully automated segmentation/thresholding without manual adjustment.
typical_use_cases: Extracting series and channels from .nd2/.lif/.lsm files to TIFF for downstream analysis
Merging separately exported per-channel TIFFs into a reordered multi-channel image
Creating max-intensity projections for all open stacks
Batch exporting processed images to PNG or TIFF
Measuring area, mean intensity, and count of positive objects per ROI and saving results to CSV

name: BD-CellView
description: BD-CellView provides Fiji tools to inspect and batch-process multichannel TIFF images produced by the BD CellView imaging flow cytometer using a table-driven workflow.
input_data: Multichannel TIFF images (typically 8-channel) from BD CellView/ICS plus a semicolon-separated table file listing id, gate and image path.
output_data: Exported/processed image files with user-defined channel visualization settings; interactive display of selected images.
use_when: Use this when you have BD CellView-generated multichannel TIFFs organized in the required folder structure and want to preview and batch-export images by gate using a table file.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your data are not BD CellView/ICS multichannel TIFFs or you cannot provide the required table and folder structure.
typical_use_cases: Previewing a random image from a selected gate to tune channel colors and min/max gray levels
Batch exporting processed images for a specific gate across one or multiple tables
Organizing and processing BD CellView datasets using id/gate/path table files

name: BigVolumeBrowser
description: FIJI plugin for interactive 3D exploration of multiple large (and small) volumetric datasets and geometric shapes, built on BigVolumeViewer. For documentation, see wiki.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need BigVolumeBrowser's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying BigVolumeBrowser tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: BigVolumeViewer Demo
description: Demo of BDV volume rendering. See this forum post for getting started.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need BigVolumeViewer Demo's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying BigVolumeViewer Demo tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: Biomat
description: Biomat provides ImageJ/Fiji plugins for 3D image preprocessing, 3D fiber enhancement, and 2D tensor-based directionality analysis.
input_data: 3D image stacks (e.g., confocal or microct volumes) and 8-bit binary 2D images for the heat-kernel tensor tools.
output_data: Filtered/enhanced images and stacks (including 3D distance maps and tensor stacks), plus summary statistics tables for tensor images (optionally within an ROI).
use_when: Use this when you need depth-dependent contrast compensation, 3D background removal, 3D distance maps, fiber enhancement in 3D, or tensor-based directionality statistics in 2D.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not 3D stacks or when you need general-purpose segmentation/analysis not covered by these specific filters and tensor tools.
typical_use_cases: Compensating for contrast loss with depth in thick confocal stacks using Stack Linear Contrast
Removing slowly varying background in 3D volumes using the Lipschitz 3D top-hat filter
Computing a 3D Euclidean distance transform of a binary volume with Distance Map 3D
Enhancing sparse bright fibers in 3D (e.g., capillaries) using Tensor Line 3D Filter or Vector Line 3D Filter
Quantifying 2D directionality from an 8-bit binary image using 2D Heat Kernel Tensor and 2D Tensor Statistics

name: BioVoxxel 3D Box
description: Provides GPU-accelerated (CLIJ2-based) 2D/3D filtering, segmentation and label-based analysis tools, producing label images rather than ROI-based results.
input_data: 2D images and 3D stacks (8/16/32-bit; some tools accept binary images and label images, including labels from MorphoLibJ; RGB not supported for flat-field correction).
output_data: Processed images (often 32-bit), binary masks, label images with consecutive integer IDs, and analysis/quality metrics (e.g., Jaccard and Dice) shown during threshold checking.
use_when: Use this when you want GPU-accelerated 2D/3D preprocessing and segmentation workflows that output label images suitable for further CLIJ2-based processing.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need ROI-based outputs, RGB support (for some functions like flat-field correction), or when pseudo flat-field correction would invalidate intensity quantification.
typical_use_cases: Correcting uneven illumination in microscopy stacks using flat-field and optional dark-field images
Testing and comparing ImageJ auto-threshold methods using Jaccard/Dice feedback to pick a threshold
Segmenting and separating touching objects into individual labels using Voronoi Threshold Labeler and Label Splitter
Adding separation gaps between touching labels to improve downstream morphology operations
Analyzing secondary objects inside primary labeled objects with Object Inspector

name: BioVoxxel Figure Tools
description: A set of Fiji tools to optimize contrast, create inset zooms, record processing metadata, and export images with overlays as editable SVG files for figure assembly in Inkscape.
input_data: Open ImageJ/Fiji images including multichannel composite images, RGB images, and 5D hyperstacks (channels, z, time), plus overlays/ROIs (e.g., from ROI Manager, scale bars, arrows).
output_data: SVG files with embedded image data and exported overlays as vector objects; optionally additional inset/zoom images and updated metadata stored in image Info and SVG description.
use_when: Use this when you need publication-quality figure export from Fiji to Inkscape with lossless scaling and editable overlays, plus consistent contrast adjustment across comparable images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a full figure-layout editor inside Fiji or if your workflow requires exporting to non-SVG formats only.
typical_use_cases: Export multichannel composite hyperstacks with scale bars and ROI overlays as editable SVG for Inkscape
Batch-export all open images (or time points from a time series) as individual SVG files for figure panels
Apply consistent contrast optimization across multiple related images while limiting oversaturation
Create framed inset zoom images (rectangular, rotated, or circular) for figure insets without interpolation artifacts
Record per-image processing steps into metadata (Meta-D-Rex) for reproducible reporting and carry it into exported SVGs

name: Blind Analysis Tools
description: Provides tools to support blind (unbiased) analysis by encrypting file names for randomized selection and by opening images under encrypted names while recording user decisions and macros.
input_data: Image files in a user-selected directory filtered by chosen file extensions (including Bio-Formats-readable microscopy files; single-series only).
output_data: Copied files with encrypted filenames in a new output folder, a mapping file linking encrypted to original names, and decision/log text files saved to the input folder.
use_when: Use this when you need to randomize and blind image file identity during manual review/scoring while keeping a recoverable mapping and audit trail.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for datasets where files contain multiple Bio-Formats series that must be handled correctly or when blinding is unnecessary.
typical_use_cases: Creating a blinded randomized subset of microscopy images for unbiased scoring
Keeping multi-channel files grouped during filename encryption using a shared substring
Blindly opening images one-by-one and assigning categorical decisions (e.g., weak/medium/strong)
Applying a predefined macro to each image before blinded display (e.g., hide a channel) and saving the recorded macro and decision log

name: BTrack
description: BTrack is a TrackMate detector that uses ImgLib2 skeletonization to detect tissue-branch end points from a segmentation channel for tracking growth in 2D time-lapse or 3D images.
input_data: 2D time-lapse or 3D images/hyperstacks readable by Fiji/Bio-Formats (e.g., .tif, .nd2), typically with a segmentation image in one channel (optionally alongside a raw channel).
output_data: TrackMate spot detections at skeleton end points and TrackMate tracks with standard TrackMate results/tables for downstream analysis (e.g., growth rates).
use_when: Use this when you have a segmentation of a branched tissue and want to detect and track growing branch end points using TrackMate.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you do not have a suitable segmentation channel/image or when you need to track features other than skeleton end points of branched structures.
typical_use_cases: Tracking growing branch tips in 2D time-lapse microscopy using TrackMate
Detecting skeleton end points from a segmentation channel in a two-channel hyperstack
Tracking end points with TrackMate (e.g., Simple LAP tracker) after BTrack detection
Computing growth rates of individual branch-tip tracks using TrackMate analysis tools

name: CALM
description: A collection of Fiji plugins developed by the Advanced Light Microscopy STP at the Francis Crick Institute.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: Use this when you want to access the CALM collection of Fiji plugins from the Francis Crick Institute.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a specific, clearly documented single-purpose plugin rather than a general plugin collection.
typical_use_cases: installing the calm plugin collection in fiji
browsing and using calm-provided tools in microscopy workflows
using crick alm stp-developed fiji plugins

name: CAMDU
description: CAMDU provides the autoQC Fiji plugin to automate microscope quality control analyses including PSF, field uniformity, colocalization and stage reproducibility checks.
input_data: Z-stacks of sub-resolution fluorescent beads (autoPSF), single 2D images of a uniform fluorescent slide (autoFOV), 4D XYCZT images of larger-than-resolution fluorescent beads (autoColoc), and 3D time-lapse stacks acquired after repeated stage moves (autoStageRepro); supports multiseries files via Bio-Formats.
output_data: Microscope QC results saved to disk in the folder of the first input file (reports/metrics and derived outputs depending on the selected autoQC module).
use_when: Use this to run standardized, automated microscope performance/QC measurements on appropriate bead, slide, colocalization or stage-repeatability datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your data do not match the required dimensionality for the chosen module or if a multiseries file mixes different objective calibrations.
typical_use_cases: Measuring PSF from Z-stacks of sub-resolution fluorescent beads
Assessing illumination/field uniformity from a fluorescent slide image
Evaluating channel colocalization using 4D bead datasets (XYCZT)
Testing stage reproducibility using time-lapse stacks after repeated stage moves

name: CATS
description: CATS is a Fiji plugin for context-aware trainable pixel classification that learns from user-drawn labels and generates pixel probability maps for large 2D/3D, multi-channel and time-lapse images using random forest classification with multi-scale features.
input_data: Currently active ImageJ image window; supports 2D/3D, multi-channel, and time-lapse images, including big datasets via VirtualStack/BigDataProcessor.
output_data: Pixel probability maps (results image/overlays) and saved training/label data (.ARFF); can export probabilities (e.g., TIFF stacks or HDF5).
use_when: Use when you need interactive, annotation-driven segmentation/pixel classification on large 2D/3D microscopy data with probability-map output.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you cannot provide correct image calibration (z/t and voxel sizes) or when you need fully automatic segmentation without manual labeling.
typical_use_cases: Trainable segmentation of large 3D electron microscopy volumes using tiling
Pixel classification on multi-channel fluorescence microscopy images
Segmentation of 3D time-lapse datasets with anisotropic voxel sizes
Exporting probability maps for downstream thresholding or further processing

name: CellTrackingChallenge
description: Provides Fiji GUI front-end plugins to run Cell Tracking Challenge (CTC) measures for quantitative evaluation of tracking, segmentation and dataset quality in biomedical time-lapse data.
input_data: Tracking/segmentation result data and (for some measures) corresponding microscopy time-lapse sequences; exact supported file formats not specified.
output_data: Quantitative scores for CTC measures (e.g., TRA, SEG, DET, CT, TF, BCi, CCA and dataset-quality measures such as SNR, CR, Hetb, Heti, Res, Sha, Spa, Cha, Ove, Mit).
use_when: Use this when you need to compute official CTC evaluation measures for tracking/segmentation results or to quantify dataset quality.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not evaluating with CTC measures or you need to perform tracking/segmentation rather than score existing results.
typical_use_cases: Computing TRA/SEG/DET scores to compare tracking and segmentation methods
Running AOGM-based tracking accuracy evaluation on time-lapse cell sequences
Calculating biological measures such as CT, TF, BCi and CCA for tracked cells
Assessing dataset quality using SNR and related CTC quality measures
Batch-mode evaluation of multiple result folders via Fiji command line

name: CIP
description: CIP (Classic Image Processing) is a scripting toolbox for ImageJ that provides a simplified, discoverable API to build image analysis workflows (formatting, filtering, segmentation, math, and assessment) across common ImageJ image types.
input_data: ImageJ images including ImagePlus, Dataset, ImgPlus, and Img (2D/3D; supports anisotropic spacing).
output_data: Processed images, label images (labelmaps), regions, tables/measurements, and tracks (visualizable in hyperstack viewer or TrackScheme).
use_when: Use CIP when you want to prototype or build end-to-end ImageJ workflows in scripts with consistent functions that work on multiple ImageJ image types without manual conversions.
do_not_use_when: Do not use CIP if you need a dedicated interactive GUI application or algorithms beyond its classic processing and experimental tracking functions.
typical_use_cases: Filtering and background removal (e.g., gaussian/median/tophat) before downstream analysis
Segmenting 2D/3D objects into label images using thresholding, watershed, or maxima detection
Measuring segmented regions and exporting results as tables
Tracking segmented regions over time using the experimental track function (TrackMate LapTracker backend)

name: CircleSkinner
description: Automates detection and quantification of circular structures in images in Fiji.
input_data: Images containing circular structures (specific supported image types/formats not stated).
output_data: Quantification results for detected circular structures (exact output types not stated).
use_when: Use this when you need automated detection and quantification of approximately circular structures in images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the objects of interest are not approximately circular or when you need general-purpose segmentation.
typical_use_cases: Detecting and quantifying circular features in microscopy images
Measuring size-related metrics of circular structures
Automated analysis of images containing circular objects

name: clijx-assistant-extensions
description: CLIJx-Assistant is an experimental GPU-accelerated workflow builder for ImageJ/Fiji that adds in-development operations and can generate/export workflows as code for other platforms.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji images (typically 2D/3D and time-lapse microscopy) that can be processed with CLIJ/CLIJ2 on a compatible GPU.
output_data: Processed images plus exported/generated code such as CLIJx/Fiji plugins, Groovy scripts for QuPath, and Python scripts for clEsperanto/Napari.
use_when: Use this to interactively design GPU-accelerated image processing workflows and deploy them via multi-lingual code generation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a stable, finalized toolchain or if you cannot run CLIJ GPU processing on your system.
typical_use_cases: Interactive construction of GPU-accelerated image processing pipelines
Drift correction for time-lapse microscopy stacks
Intensity projection of 3D/4D image data
Generating a Fiji plugin from a designed CLIJx workflow
Exporting a workflow as Groovy for QuPath or Python for clEsperanto/Napari

name: clijx-deconvolution
description: Provides GPU-accelerated FFT-based Richardson–Lucy deconvolution in Fiji via CLIJ2/clij2-fft, including non-circulant edge handling and total variation regularization.
input_data: 3D ImageJ/Fiji images (microscopy stacks) plus a corresponding PSF image, processed via CLIJ2/clij2-fft on an OpenCL device.
output_data: Deconvolved (restored) image.
use_when: Use this when you need GPU-based Richardson–Lucy deconvolution in Fiji, especially for 3D data with extended PSFs where classic RL can ring at high iteration counts.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for 2D-only deconvolution needs or when you lack a suitable PSF and a working OpenCL/CLIJ2 setup.
typical_use_cases: Deconvolving 3D fluorescence microscopy stacks with Richardson–Lucy on the GPU
Reducing ringing artifacts using non-circulant edge handling during deconvolution
Applying total variation regularization to stabilize deconvolution of noisy 3D volumes
Running deconvolution from the CLIJ2-assistant as part of a Fiji workflow

name: CMCI-EMBL
description: Adds an “EMBL” menu to ImageJ/Fiji to open course sample images and provide utilities for learning image processing algorithms.
input_data: No input required; opens bundled or linked sample images from the EMBL menu.
output_data: Opened sample images and access to course-related utility functions via the EMBL menu.
use_when: Use this when you need quick access to EMBL/CMCI course sample images and learning utilities inside ImageJ/Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need a specific analysis plugin rather than a course sample-image loader and training utilities menu.
typical_use_cases: Opening predefined sample images for an image analysis course
Loading example datasets to follow along with CMCI/EMBL tutorials
Accessing assorted course utility functions from the EMBL menu
Installing a course menu via Fiji Updater for automatic updates

name: CMP-BIA tools
description: CMP-BIA tools is an ImageJ/Fiji plugin package for image segmentation and registration, including the jSLIC superpixel clustering method.
input_data: 2D images opened in ImageJ/Fiji (grayscale or color images).
output_data: Superpixel segmentation results as contour overlays, random-color label visualizations, polygon ROIs in the ROI Manager, and optional text-file label matrices.
use_when: Use it when you need SLIC-like superpixels (jSLIC) as an intermediate step for further segmentation, classification, or registration.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need end-to-end semantic segmentation or when superpixel clustering is not appropriate for your images.
typical_use_cases: Generating superpixels as a preprocessing step for downstream segmentation or classification
Exporting superpixel regions as polygon ROIs for measurement workflows in ImageJ
Overlaying superpixel boundaries on biomedical images for visual quality control
Saving a superpixel label matrix to a text file for external processing

name: ColonyArea
description: Analyzes clonogenic colony or focus formation assay images by detecting wells, thresholding each well, and reporting per-well colony area percentage and intensity percentage.
input_data: A single .tiff image of a colony formation assay plate (6-, 12-, 24-well or custom layout), optionally with a user-drawn rectangular ROI selecting the wells to analyze.
output_data: Saved TIFF image stacks of cropped wells and thresholded wells plus TXT files with per-well applied thresholds and a results table (well number, colony area percentage, colony intensity percentage).
use_when: Use this when you need per-well quantification of colony coverage (area and intensity) from scanned/photographed multi-well clonogenic or focus formation assays.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need colony counts or when wells cannot be reliably detected/thresholded due to issues like poor illumination or unsuitable images.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying clonogenic survival assays in 6-, 12-, or 24-well dishes by area and intensity percentage
Comparing focus formation between conditions using per-well area coverage rather than colony counts
Analyzing only a subset of wells by drawing a rectangular ROI around selected wells
Re-measuring a sub-region of a well to avoid areas where background was incorrectly thresholded

name: ColorBlindLUTs
description: Adds Okabe & Ito color-blind-friendly lookup tables (CB_*) to Fiji/ImageJ for unambiguous image display.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji images where a LUT can be applied (typically single-channel/grayscale images and stacks).
output_data: Image display using the selected CB_* LUT from the LUT menu (no new image data generated).
use_when: Use this to apply color-blind-safe colormaps for clearer on-screen viewing and figures.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need to change pixel values or perform quantitative processing rather than adjust display colors.
typical_use_cases: Applying CB_* LUTs to confocal z-stacks for accessible visualization
Preparing microscopy figures with color-blind-friendly colormaps
Standardizing single-channel image display colors across a dataset
Improving interpretability of grayscale images during manual inspection

name: Colour Deconvolution2
description: Separates up to three subtractively mixed dyes in 24-bit RGB brightfield images using the Ruifrok and Johnston colour deconvolution method.
input_data: 24-bit RGB image or RGB stack generated by colour subtraction (e.g., brightfield histology stains, transparent inks); avoid lossy-compressed formats such as JPEG.
output_data: Three dye-separated images as 8-bit transmittance, 32-bit transmittance (optionally with simulated LUTs), or 32-bit absorbance, and/or a 24-bit RGB intensity image with a selected dye removed or retained.
use_when: Use it to unmix transparent dyes/inks that mix subtractively in RGB brightfield images to obtain separate stain channels for visualization or downstream segmentation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for fluorescence (additive mixing), reflectance images, or when dyes are not reliably modeled as absorbers (e.g., strong scatterers such as some silver methods) or when quantifying non-stoichiometric stains like DAB for antigen expression.
typical_use_cases: Unmixing H&E brightfield sections into hematoxylin and eosin channels
Separating hematoxylin and DAB to segment immunostained structures
Generating absorbance (optical density) images for Beer-Lambert based analysis on suitable stoichiometric stains (e.g., Feulgen)
Separating inks/pigments in scanned documents or artworks using ROI-derived vectors

name: Cookbook
description: A collection of example ImageJ/Fiji plugins, scripts, and macros from the ImageJ Cookbook for learning and reuse.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You want Cookbook example code and small utilities to learn from or adapt into your own ImageJ/Fiji workflows.
do_not_use_when: You need a single, well-defined analysis plugin with clearly specified inputs, outputs, and behavior.
typical_use_cases: Learning ImageJ/Fiji plugin, script, or macro development from examples
Copying and adapting Cookbook code for a custom workflow
Browsing small utility tools included with the Cookbook

name: CSIM Laboratory
description: A CSIM research-group collection of ImageJ plugins providing anomalous diffusion filtering, dynamic perfusion MRI metrics, noise generation, and 2D sample entropy measurement.
input_data: Digital images and image stacks opened in ImageJ, including dynamic perfusion MRI time-series stacks.
output_data: Filtered images, noise-modified images, and numeric measurement results (e.g., perfusion metrics and SampEn2D values).
use_when: Use this when you want CSIMLab implementations for anomalous diffusion filtering, perfusion MRI parameter extraction, noise simulation, or SampEn2D in ImageJ/Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need a single specialized, fully validated workflow for clinical or production analysis rather than experimental research implementations.
typical_use_cases: Enhancing MR images using anisotropic or isotropic anomalous diffusion filtering
Computing time-to-peak, peak, CBV, and CBF from dynamic perfusion MRI stacks
Adding synthetic noise (uniform, gaussian, rayleigh, or 1/f colored noise) to images for testing
Measuring 2D sample entropy (SampEn2D) as an image texture/irregularity feature

name: DeepClas4Bio-plugins
description: ImageJ plugin that connects to the DeepClas4Bio API to run deep-learning object classification on an opened image from within ImageJ.
input_data: An image opened in ImageJ (e.g., a 2D RGB or grayscale image) to be classified.
output_data: Predicted class/label results shown in the plugin output (visualized in ImageJ).
use_when: Use this when you want to classify the content of an ImageJ image using a deep-learning model provided via the DeepClas4Bio API.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if the DeepClas4Bio API is not installed/configured or if you need segmentation/detection rather than image classification.
typical_use_cases: Classifying a natural image (e.g., a lion photo) using VGG16 via Keras
Running different DeepClas4Bio-supported frameworks/models to classify the same image
Adding deep-learning classification to an ImageJ workflow without handling model code directly

name: Dendrite Dynamics Tracker
description: Tracks dendrite dynamics in 2D time-lapse movies in Fiji.
input_data: 2D time-lapse image sequences (movies) opened in Fiji/ImageJ.
output_data: Dendrite tracks over time and associated measurements.
use_when: Use this to quantify dendrite dynamics from 2D time-lapse microscopy movies.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for 3D datasets or single images without a time dimension.
typical_use_cases: Tracking dendrite dynamics in 2D fluorescence time-lapse movies
Measuring dendrite extension and retraction over time
Quantifying neurite movement trajectories in live-cell imaging

name: DHM Utilities
description: Collection of ImageJ2 plugins for digital holographic microscopy to reconstruct/propagate holograms, unwrap phase, and align multiple images using landmark correspondences.
input_data: Digital hologram images and multi-image datasets opened in ImageJ/Fiji (2D images and stacks).
output_data: Reconstructed/propagated images, unwrapped phase images, and aligned images.
use_when: Use this for digital holographic microscopy workflows needing hologram reconstruction/propagation, phase unwrapping, or landmark-based multi-image alignment.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not working with DHM data or you need general-purpose registration/processing not based on hologram reconstruction or landmark correspondences.
typical_use_cases: Reconstructing and propagating DHM holograms
Unwrapping wrapped phase maps from DHM reconstructions
Aligning multiple DHM images using landmark correspondences

name: DHPSFU
description: DHPSFU is a Fiji/ImageJ plugin suite for analyzing Double Helix PSF microscopy 3D SMLM/SPT localization data, including 2D-to-3D conversion, drift/blinking correction, and overlay/visualization.
