input_data: 2D/3D localization files (e.g., GDSC PeakFit .xls for 2D, ViSP .3d for 3D, or custom-column text) plus a DH calibration file; optionally raw image stacks for overlay and drift correction.
output_data: Tab-separated 3D coordinate files (.3d) in x y z Intensity Frame format, Fiji tables/in-memory localization lists, and localization density images; optionally overlays on raw image stacks.
use_when: Use this when you have Double Helix PSF SMLM/SPT localization data and need to convert 2D localizations to 3D and/or apply drift and blinking corrections with visualization in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not have DH-PSF localization data and a suitable DH calibration file for 2D-to-3D conversion.
typical_use_cases: Convert 2D DH-PSF localizations to 3D coordinates using a DH calibration file
Correct acquisition drift using cross-correlation of images of the same sample
Temporal grouping/tracking of localizations to reduce blinking artifacts
Load GDSC PeakFit .xls or ViSP .3d localization files and export results
Create localization density images and overlay localizations on raw stacks

name: DiversePathsJ
description: DiversePathsJ computes a collection of diverse shortest paths between exactly two user-selected endpoints in an image using dynamic programming on a cost (potential) map.
input_data: 2D images in ImageJ/Fiji (e.g., TIFF) with exactly two point selections (source and target); optionally an external cost map image loaded from a file path.
output_data: Path overlays and Polyline ROIs exported to the ROI Manager; optional cost map image display; optional per-path cost values in the Log window; optional per-path image copies with overlays.
use_when: Use it when you need multiple alternative, explicitly diverse shortest paths between two endpoints along image features using a configurable cost map.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need paths without specifying exactly two endpoints or when the structure cannot be represented well by a 2D cost map and local smoothness regularization.
typical_use_cases: Tracing multiple candidate centerlines through a bacterium or worm between two selected endpoints
Finding diverse routes along ridges or edges after applying ridge/edge filtering
Exporting several alternative paths as Polyline ROIs for measurement or downstream processing
Comparing Dijkstra vs Viterbi search behavior on the same endpoint pair

name: DNA FISH
description: Semi-automated Fiji plugin that detects DNA FISH spots using TrackMate and measures inter-channel distances between the closest spots at user-defined points.
input_data: Multi-channel fluorescence microscopy images (2D or z-stacks) with FISH spots; optional 2D ROI for restricting detection; ImageJ multi-point selections; optional background ROIs per channel.
output_data: Results table with per-selected-point spot positions and inter-channel distances, with interactive highlighting of the spots used for each row.
use_when: Use this when you want to measure distances between corresponding FISH spots across two or three channels at many manually indicated regions and QC the chosen spots.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need fully automated nucleus-aware pairing/segmentation or if you must restrict spot detection/analysis in 3D along the z-axis.
typical_use_cases: Measuring distances between two genomic loci labeled in two FISH channels within nuclei
Three-color FISH analysis by measuring pairwise distances between channels at selected regions
Counting and localizing detected FISH spots per channel with visual QC of detections
Restricting spot detection to a single nucleus using a 2D ROI before measuring distances

name: Dorsal Horn Mapping
description: Maps murine spinal cord dorsal horn cell ROIs to standardized lamina and segment coordinates by aligning a reference frame and exporting per-cell location data.
input_data: Images of murine spinal cord slices where the dorsal horn outline is visible (brightfield or autofluorescence in at least one channel) plus at least one cell ROI in the ROI Manager (optionally pre-sorted ROI Groups).
output_data: Results table of per-ROI data (ROI id, X/Y, group, Rx/Ry, lamina, segment) plus optional XLS (2-sheet) or two CSV files (cells + transform parameters), optional flattened RGB PNG overlay, and optional saved grouped ROIs.
use_when: Use when you need harmonized, reproducible lamina/segment location reporting for ROI-marked cells in murine dorsal horn slice images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the dorsal horn outline cannot be reliably aligned or when cells are not available as ROIs in the ROI Manager.
typical_use_cases: Assigning dorsal horn lamina and spinal segment labels to neuron/cell ROIs in mouse spinal cord slices
Exporting per-cell mapped coordinates (Rx/Ry) and transformation parameters for reproducible reporting
Manually grouping cells into 0–9 stain/marker groups during mapping and saving grouped ROIs
Saving a flattened PNG overlay with ROIs and the aligned frame for documentation or figures

name: Draw 3D ROI
description: Creates a 3D binary mask from the intersection of multiple user-defined 2D ROIs drawn from different projection perspectives of a z-stack.
input_data: Z-stack images opened in Fiji (including 4D/5D stacks); 2D time-lapse images only if the time axis is reassigned to z via Image > Properties; user-drawn 2D selections (most ImageJ selection tools except 1-pixel straight line and multi-point).
output_data: New 3D binary segmented mask image (exactly 3 dimensions) and an optional preview channel added to the working image during interaction.
use_when: Use it when you need a hand-drawn 3D mask by combining 2D ROIs set from multiple viewing perspectives of a stack.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for standalone ImageJ (non-Fiji) or when you need automated segmentation rather than manual ROI-based masking.
typical_use_cases: Manually segmenting a 3D structure in a microscopy z-stack using ROIs from multiple projections
Creating a 3D binary mask for downstream 3D analysis in tools like MorphoLibJ or 3D ImageJ Suite
Building a coarse 3D mask on a downsampled stack and scaling it back to full resolution after export

name: EasyFiji
description: EasyFiji is a Fiji GUI plugin that provides a curated set of fluorescence image visualization and processing tools and buttons to run built-in commands and bundled macro scripts.
input_data: Images opened and selected in Fiji (active image window), including fluorescence microscopy images; supports Bio-Formats if installed.
output_data: Processed/modified images in Fiji and any outputs produced by the invoked Fiji commands or bundled macro scripts (e.g., updated display/LUTs).
use_when: Use this when you want a panel-based GUI to quickly apply common fluorescence visualization and processing steps in Fiji without manually running commands or scripts.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need a specific standalone analysis algorithm or when you prefer to run the underlying Fiji commands/macros directly (or required dependencies like Bio-Formats are not installed).
typical_use_cases: Applying LUTs and channel display adjustments to fluorescence images
Running common processing steps such as filters or intensity correction from a GUI
Executing bundled macro scripts (e.g., color fusion) via buttons instead of manual scripting
Using Fiji features like Bio-Formats or kymograph tools through a simplified interface

name: EM Tool
description: A suite of ImageJ/Fiji tools for processing SEM/TEM electron microscopy images, including reading microscope metadata to set scale, cutting FEI databars, flat-field correction, feature measurement annotation, and exporting TEM .ser/.dm3 to TIFF.
input_data: SEM/TEM images including SEM TIFF files with FEI/ZEISS/Hitachi metadata tags, SEM images with associated .txt scale files (JEOL), and folders containing TEM .ser and Gatan .dm3 files.
output_data: Images calibrated with correct spatial scale (and scan rotation when available), separated image/databar files, flat-field corrected image sets, annotated images with feature measurements, and exported TIFF files plus measurement results.
use_when: Use this for EM-specific Fiji workflows such as extracting SEM metadata for calibration, batch flat-field correction for mapping, databar separation, feature-size annotation, or converting TEM .ser/.dm3 folders to TIFF.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are already correctly imported and calibrated by Bio-Formats or when required metadata/sidecar files are missing for the specific microscope workflow.
typical_use_cases: Read FEI or ZEISS SEM TIFF metadata to set pixel size calibration (and scan rotation for FEI)
Cut the databar from FEI SEM images and save the image and databar separately
Batch flat-field correction of large-area SEM mapping images to reduce stitching chessboard artifacts
Convert a folder of TEM .ser and Gatan .dm3 files to TIFF for downstream analysis
Annotate line-length feature measurements on calibrated EM images and export results

name: EpiGraph
description: Quantifies the organization of epithelial-like tessellations by computing graphlet-based metrics and graphlet degree distribution distances (GDDs) from a skeletonized cell-contact network and comparing them to reference tessellations.
input_data: Single 2D skeleton image of a mosaic/tessellation where region contacts are represented as edges (skeletonized cell-contact network).
output_data: Results tables (exportable to Excel) with graphlet counts and GDD-based organization metrics (Epi-Hexagons, Epi-Random, Epi-Voronoi5), selected ROIs, and an optional 3D visualization/classification window.
use_when: Use this to measure and compare the arrangement of a planar cellular mosaic/tessellation against hexagonal and Voronoi-based reference organizations using graphlet/GDD metrics.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for image stacks (must be converted to single frames) or when you do not have a skeletonized contact network with enough cells for coherent graphlet statistics.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying epithelial packing organization from a skeletonized cell-contact mosaic
Comparing tissue organization to hexagonal lattice, random Voronoi, and Voronoi5 references
Selecting an ROI of cells and exporting graphlet/GDD results for downstream statistical analysis
Classifying multiple samples and visualizing their positions in a 3D organization plot

name: EVAnalyzer
description: EVAnalyzer is a Fiji/ImageJ plugin for automated high-content analysis of extracellular vesicle (EV) microscopy images, including EV counting, colocalization, cell detection, batch processing, and XLSX report generation with statistics.
input_data: Monochromatic (non-RGB) microscopy images; supports VSI, TIFF, ICS, and CZI.
output_data: EV and cell counts/measurements, colocalization results, and XLSX (Excel) reports with automatically generated statistics.
use_when: Use this for automated, high-throughput quantification and colocalization analysis of EVs (and associated cells) in multi-channel microscopy datasets with Excel reporting.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for RGB images or image formats other than VSI, TIFF, ICS, or CZI.
typical_use_cases: Counting extracellular vesicles in multi-channel microscopy images
Computing colocalization between EVs and other particles across channels
Detecting cells and quantifying EVs associated with cells
Batch processing large image collections and exporting XLSX reports with statistics

name: Excel Functions
description: Provides recordable ImageJ/Fiji menu commands and macro/Java/Jython extensions to write ImageJ tables, images, and plots into Excel .xlsx workbooks and manage workbook sheets.
input_data: ImageJ ResultsTable and other open tables, ImagePlus images, and ImageJ Plot objects for writing into Excel .xlsx workbooks.
output_data: Excel .xlsx workbook files with worksheets containing exported tables, inserted images, and inserted charts/plots.
use_when: Use this when you need a scriptable and recordable way to save ImageJ tables and embed images/plots into specific sheets of an Excel .xlsx workbook (including headless macro workflows).
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you require accurate, distortion-free embedded images/plots in Excel or if you have the Read and Write Excel plugin installed in parallel (they clash).
typical_use_cases: Saving all open ImageJ results tables as worksheets in a new or existing .xlsx workbook
Appending a Results table as rows or arrays as columns/rows into a chosen worksheet
Embedding an ImagePlus (processed image or mask) into a specified workbook sheet at a given row/column
Creating an Excel chart from an ImageJ Plot and inserting it into a workbook sheet

name: EZFig
description: EZFig is a tool to build publication-ready scientific figures based on ScientiFig but simpler to use. It must be used in combination with the 'Java-8' update site.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need EZFig's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying EZFig tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: FAST
description: FAST is an ImageJ macro that batch-processes single-channel fluorescence images by applying a fixed intensity threshold to create/save ROIs and measure fluorescence within them.
input_data: Single-channel fluorescence microscopy images in CZI, ZVI, ND2, TIFF, or other user-specified file extensions within a selected directory and sub-directories.
output_data: ROI zip files saved in each image directory and an ImageJ Results table saved as a CSV file in the selected work directory.
use_when: Use this to quantify above-threshold fluorescence across many single-channel images while saving ROIs and optionally validating/editing them per image.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need multi-channel analysis, adaptive/advanced segmentation, or measurements not based on a fixed intensity threshold.
typical_use_cases: Batch thresholding and ROI creation for a directory tree of fluorescence images
Interactive validation and manual correction of auto-generated ROIs before measurement
Silent re-measurement of previously saved ROIs to regenerate a results CSV
Excluding blurred or unusable images during a batch fluorescence quantification run

name: FAST-HIPPOS
description: FAST-HIPPOS is a collection of Fiji scripts for analyzing and visualizing multi-cell time-lapse microscopy (including FLIM), segmenting cells, extracting single-cell traces, selecting hit cells by criteria, and exporting their stage coordinates for downstream targeting such as photoactivation.
input_data: Multi-channel .tif time-lapse stacks and Bio-Formats-supported proprietary microscopy files (including Leica .lif and multi-image files); FLIM inputs including LASX-exported lifetime component TIFFs, TauSeparation from .lif, Fast FLIM, TauContrast, and Lambert Instruments frequency-domain .fli; also 2-channel ratio TIFF and single-channel intensity TIFF; supports batch processing of multiple files.
output_data: Label image of segmented cells, ROI set (ROIs.zip), per-cell intensity and lifetime time-trace tables (TSV) and plots, kymographs/density plots/movies, weighted lifetime and RGB overlay images, cell statistics table (TSV), and (when screening) hit lists with XY stage coordinates plus a .rgn file for Leica LAS X Navigator.
use_when: Use this for end-to-end analysis of multi-cell time-lapse FLIM/ratio/intensity experiments where you need cell segmentation, per-cell trace quantification/visualization, and optional hit selection with exported stage coordinates.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot provide Bio-Formats-readable time-lapse data or do not have a working Cellpose environment for segmentation, or if cells move substantially such that a single collapsed projection is not suitable for segmentation.
typical_use_cases: Pooled optical screening: detect hit cells from FLIM time-lapse data and export .rgn stage positions for photoactivation in Leica LAS X
Extract and visualize single-cell lifetime or ratio traces across a time-lapse experiment
Batch-process multiple microscopy files to generate label maps, ROIs, and per-cell trace tables
Stitch multi-tile Leica .lif experiments into a corrected stitched TIFF prior to analysis
Interactively inspect traces and manually select cells to export a targeted .rgn file

name: Fast4DReg
description: Fast4DReg is a Fiji macro suite that estimates and corrects translational x/y and/or z drift in 3D time-lapse stacks and can align misregistered channels in 3D multichannel datasets using cross-correlation.
input_data: Single-channel 3D time-lapse stacks (x,y,z,t) and split-channel 3D multichannel stacks; microscopy files readable via Bio-Formats.
output_data: Corrected image stacks saved to an output folder, drift plots, drift tables, and a settings.csv file for reapplying the correction to other images/channels.
use_when: Use this when you need fast cross-correlation-based correction of translational x/y and/or z drift in 3D time-lapse data or to align offset channels in 3D datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the image lacks stable structures for reliable cross-correlation or when motion is non-translational (e.g., deformation or complex sample movement).
typical_use_cases: Correcting x-y drift in a 3D fluorescence time-lapse (x,y,z,t) dataset
Correcting z-drift in a 3D time-lapse where focus shifts over time
Estimating drift on a stable reference channel and applying it to other channels using settings.csv
Aligning misregistered channels in a 3D multichannel acquisition from a microscope with channel offsets

name: FFMPEG
description: Imports and exports video files via JavaCV/FFmpeg, opening movies as ImageJ virtual stacks or 5D hyperstacks and saving stacks/hyperstacks back to video.
input_data: Video files in FFmpeg-supported formats; active ImageJ image stacks or 5D hyperstacks for export.
output_data: Virtual image stack (optionally decimated) or 5D hyperstack on import; video file in chosen format/compression on export.
use_when: Use this when you need to open or write movies in virtually any FFmpeg-supported container/codec and work with them as ImageJ stacks or hyperstacks.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot install the required JavaCV/FFmpeg components (via the JavaCV Installer) or you only need standard still-image I/O.
typical_use_cases: Importing a movie with a selected frame range as a virtual stack for frame-by-frame analysis
Importing a movie and converting it to a 5D hyperstack with specified z-slices, time-frames, and field order
Applying simple import-time operations such as flip or grayscale conversion
Exporting an ImageJ stack or hyperstack to a compressed video with a chosen format and compression settings

name: FFmpegVideoImportExport
description: The FFmpegVideoImportExport allows to import compressed video files into a virtual stack or hyperstack and export a stack as compressed video file using JavaCV interface to FFmpeg.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need FFmpegVideoImportExport's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying FFmpegVideoImportExport tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: FibrilJ
description: Macros developed to measure the diameters and persistence lengths if amyloids fibrils by AFM, EM, STM etc. iamges. It provides histogram distribution of the average fibril diameter and average fibril persistence length. Was validated for AFM, SEM and TEM images of Sup35NM fibrils on mica, silicon, gold surfaces and formvar film.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need FibrilJ's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying FibrilJ tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: FiloQuant
description: FiloQuant is an ImageJ/Fiji macro tool for automated detection and quantification of filopodia (finger-like protrusions) extending from the cell edge in 2D images, with optional batch processing and time-lapse tracking export.
input_data: 2D microscopy images and time-lapse stacks in .tif/.tiff format (optionally within a user-defined ROI).
output_data: CSV result tables (e.g., filopodia lengths, edge lengths, x/y coordinates), settings.csv, and output images including a time-projection of detected filopodia plus an optional tracking file for downstream tools (e.g., TrackMate).
use_when: Use this to quantify filopodia length/density and related measurements from 2D images where protrusions extend from a clearly detectable cell edge, including batch and live-cell time-lapse datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for 3D datasets or for filopodia at cell-cell junctions or dorsal/ventral filopodia not extending from the cell edge.
typical_use_cases: Measuring filopodia length and edge length from 2D fluorescence images of migrating cells
Batch screening filopodia phenotypes across many .tif images
Analyzing live-cell time-lapse stacks and exporting a tracking file for TrackMate
Quantifying filopodia in cells with complex morphology (e.g., neurons)

name: Foci Analyzer
description: Foci Analyzer is a Fiji macro that segments nuclei/cells and detects, quantifies, and visualizes nuclear/cellular foci in 2D/3D multichannel fluorescence microscopy images, with optional two-channel colocalization.
input_data: Multichannel fluorescence microscopy images (2D or 3D stacks), including TIFF and Bio-Formats-readable proprietary microscopy formats (including multi-series files); RGB images are accepted and converted to composite channels; optionally ImageJ ROI .zip files or external label images can be loaded for segmentation.
output_data: Per-image .tsv tables with per-nucleus foci statistics (counts, intensity, size/volume, nucleus intensity), saved overlay images showing segmented nuclei/cells and detected foci on the original image, and overlap/colocalization maps when two foci channels are analyzed.
use_when: Use this for automated, batch-capable per-nucleus (or per-cell) foci detection and quantification in 2D/3D multichannel fluorescence microscopy, optionally including two-channel foci colocalization.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not multichannel fluorescence images with a usable nuclei (or provided ROI/label) segmentation, or when you require fully manual foci annotation rather than automated detection.
typical_use_cases: Quantify nuclear foci counts and intensities per nucleus in confocal or widefield 2D/3D datasets
Batch-process Bio-Formats microscopy files (including multi-series) and export per-image .tsv results
Run two-channel foci analysis to compute colocalization and generate overlap maps
Analyze foci within whole cells using Cellpose-based cell segmentation

name: FOCUST
description: FOCUST is a GPU-accelerated Fiji/ImageJ plugin for interactive optimization, batch segmentation, and analysis of 3D fluorescence microscopy datasets to relate biological objects and export consolidated results.
input_data: 3D fluorescence microscopy image stacks (multi-channel) in supported image formats; optionally pre-segmented label images for Analysis Only mode.
output_data: Segmented images saved as .tif, appended results tables saved as .csv, a parameter/optimization record saved as .json, and a log file.
use_when: Use this when you need an interactive parameter-optimization workflow and GPU-accelerated batch segmentation plus object-based measurements/relationships in 3D fluorescence stacks.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot use CLIJ2/GPU acceleration or if your task is not 3D fluorescence object segmentation with downstream object relationship analysis.
typical_use_cases: Segmenting nuclei and whole cells in 3D and assigning each nucleus to its parent cell
Analyzing spheroids by relating nuclei to parent spheroid objects and counting nuclei per spheroid
Optimizing preprocessing, thresholding, and segmentation settings on representative images then batch-processing a folder
Quantifying morphology and multi-channel intensities per object and exporting a single appended CSV per run

name: FPBioimage
description: Uploads an open RGB image stack from ImageJ/Fiji to the FPBioimage web app for interactive 3D volumetric viewing and sharing with voxel ratio metadata.
input_data: Open ImageJ/Fiji image stack (3D), must be RGB Color; uses voxel size from Image > Properties (can be overridden in the plugin).
output_data: Uploaded volume in the FPBioimage web viewer with XYZ voxel ratio metadata.
use_when: Use this to quickly upload and share an RGB 3D stack for browser-based volumetric viewing in FPBioimage.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if the stack is not RGB or if you cannot upload data to an external web service.
typical_use_cases: Uploading an RGB-rendered microscopy z-stack for collaborator review in a browser
Sharing a 3D volume with correct voxel aspect ratio taken from Image Properties
Creating a one-click upload button via a Fiji startup macro for routine sharing

name: FracLac Suite
description: FracLac is an ImageJ plugin suite for fractal, lacunarity, multifractal, and related morphological analysis of digital images.
input_data: Digital images opened in ImageJ (image type not specified in the provided documentation).
output_data: Numeric analysis measurements (e.g., fractal dimension, lacunarity, multifractal and morphology metrics) reported as ImageJ results/log outputs.
use_when: Use this when you need quantitative fractal, lacunarity, multifractal, or morphology descriptors for complex patterns in digital images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need only basic morphology measurements or when the image is not suitable for fractal/morphology analysis.
typical_use_cases: Measuring box-counting fractal dimension of complex biological morphologies
Computing lacunarity indices to quantify texture or gap distribution
Estimating multifractal characteristics of patterns in microscopy images
Quantifying difficult-to-describe morphological features in digital images

name: FRAP-Tools
description: FRAP-Tools is a set of Fiji scripts to extract, double-normalize, plot, and curve-fit fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) time-series data from an open image stack using three ROIs.
input_data: An open FRAP time-lapse image stack plus three ROIs in the ROI Manager (FRAP/bleached region, non-bleached signal region, background region); CSV files containing XY recovery data for the plotting/combining scripts.
output_data: Saved FRAP recovery datasets (CSV/Results tables), curve-fit data/parameters, plots (ImageJ plots or JFreeChart with optional SVG export), and optional snapshot images and ROI ZIP files.
use_when: Use this when you want semi-automated FRAP recovery quantification with double normalization, manual time-interval entry, and plotting/curve-fitting including pooled analysis across experiments.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot provide exactly three appropriate ROIs or if you require automatic extraction of timing from image metadata.
typical_use_cases: Extracting and double-normalizing a FRAP recovery curve from a single image stack using three ROIs
Automatically finding the bleach timepoint and trimming to post-bleach recovery frames
Fitting single- or double-exponential models to FRAP recovery kinetics
Combining multiple FRAP CSV datasets to plot individual curves plus mean ± SEM/SD and fit the mean
Exporting FRAP plots as SVG using the JFreeChart plotting scripts

name: FreQ
description: An ImageJ plugin to analyze pixel intensity oscillations in time-lapse 2D images. For example, this allows to characterize the ciliary beat of multi-ciliated cells in time-lapse images acquired with a bright-field or phase-contrast microscope. For more details, a link to the corresponding publication, and information on how to cite the plugin visit the FreQ github pages at
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need FreQ's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying FreQ tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: FunImageJ
description: FunImageJ is a Lisp/Clojure framework for scientific image processing built on the ImageJ ecosystem, supporting functional-style programming for Fiji and standalone use with attention to big-data performance.
input_data: Images available in the ImageJ/Fiji ecosystem (ImageJ/ImgLib2 image data; specific file formats not specified).
output_data: Processed images and any analysis outputs produced by FunImageJ code (e.g., images and data structures; exact outputs depend on the functions used).
use_when: Use this when you want to implement ImageJ/Fiji image-processing workflows in Clojure/Lisp, including performance-conscious pipelines for large biological imaging datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a single purpose-built GUI plugin for a specific analysis task rather than a programming framework.
typical_use_cases: Running Clojure-based image processing scripts inside Fiji
Building reusable functional-style pipelines for biological image analysis
Creating standalone programs that use ImageJ/ImgLib2 via FunImageJ
Processing large image datasets where performance and memory use are important

name: Fuzzy logic and artificial neural networks image processing toolbox for ImageJ
description: A set of ImageJ plugins implementing fuzzy-logic and artificial neural network methods for edge detection and contrast enhancement.
input_data: ImageJ images (image stacks not specified; likely 2D images).
output_data: Processed images (edge-detected and/or contrast-enhanced).
use_when: Use this when you want to apply fuzzy-logic or neural-network based edge detection or contrast enhancement within ImageJ.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need segmentation, tracking, or quantitative measurement outputs rather than image filtering/enhancement.
typical_use_cases: Enhancing contrast in low-contrast images
Detecting edges as a preprocessing step before segmentation
Comparing fuzzy/ANN edge detection to standard ImageJ edge filters

name: Fuzzy Set
description: Applies a user-parameterized fuzzy-set intensity transformation to a grayscale image and shows the transformed image along with histograms before and after the transform.
input_data: Grayscale images opened in Fiji/ImageJ using gray levels (0-255 output range).
output_data: A new modified image window plus histogram windows for the original and modified images.
use_when: Use it when you want to remap grayscale pixel intensities with a fuzzy-set-based transform to enhance image appearance and compare histograms.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when you need segmentation/measurement outputs or when working with non-grayscale intensity models where a 0-255 gray-level transform is not appropriate.
typical_use_cases: Enhancing contrast in a grayscale microscopy image via a fuzzy-set intensity transform
Nonlinear remapping to emphasize a chosen gray-level range in an image
Comparing pre- and post-transform intensity distributions using the displayed histograms
Exploring fuzzy-set-based image enhancement by tuning transform parameters

name: Gamma_LUT_Toggle
description: Adds a Fiji toolbar button that toggles the active image display LUT between linear (gamma 1) and gamma 0.45 without changing pixel values.
input_data: Open ImageJ/Fiji images using grayscale or single-color LUTs.
output_data: Remapped display LUT toggled between gamma 1 and gamma 0.45; underlying pixel values unchanged.
use_when: Use this to improve visual visibility of dim structures or reduce apparent saturation by applying a nondestructive gamma correction to the display.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need to modify pixel intensities (e.g., for quantitative analysis or saving a gamma-corrected image).
typical_use_cases: Enhancing visibility of dim objects during fluorescence image inspection
Toggling between gamma 1 and gamma 0.45 while adjusting brightness/contrast
Reducing apparent saturation of bright regions for easier visual assessment

name: Gut Analysis Toolbox
description: Gut Analysis Toolbox provides semi-automated Fiji workflows to segment and quantify enteric neurons, neuronal subtypes and ganglia in 2D gut wholemount fluorescence images, with optional spatial, multiplex alignment and calcium imaging analysis.
input_data: 2D gut wholemount fluorescence microscopy images or maximum-intensity projections; if a Z-stack/3D image is opened it prompts for max projection; tested with Bio-Formats/Fiji-readable files including czi, lif, png, tif.
output_data: Segmentation label/mask images for neuron soma, neuronal subtypes and ganglia, plus quantitative results such as counts and analysis tables (e.g., spatial metrics and calcium traces).
use_when: Use this for semi-automated segmentation and quantification of enteric neurons/subtypes and ganglia from 2D or max-projected gut wholemount images, including downstream spatial or calcium-imaging analyses.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need reliable fully automated results without manual verification or if your experiment requires true 3D segmentation/quantification rather than 2D maximum projections.
typical_use_cases: Segmenting and counting Hu+ enteric neuron soma in 2D wholemount images
Segmenting ganglia and normalising neuron counts to ganglia number
Counting neuronal subtypes (e.g., ChAT, nNOS) using StarDist-based segmentation
Running spatial analysis based on neighboring-cell metrics in ENS images
Aligning calcium imaging sequences and extracting normalised fluorescence traces

name: HDF5
description: Reads and writes HDF5 files in ImageJ/Fiji, including loading 2D–5D datasets and saving hyperstacks as HDF5 datasets.
input_data: HDF5 (.h5/.hdf5) files containing 2D–5D datasets; ImageJ/Fiji images/hyperstacks to be saved as HDF5.
output_data: Opened ImageJ stacks/hyperstacks (including combined multichannel/time-series hyperstacks); HDF5 files with datasets written/appended from ImageJ hyperstacks.
use_when: Use this when you need to load, combine, or save large 2D–5D image datasets in the HDF5 format from within ImageJ/Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you require rich metadata beyond voxel size (only element_size_um is handled) or need to read/write a single dataset larger than 2GB.
typical_use_cases: Importing 3D confocal volumes stored in .h5 into Fiji
Combining multiple 2D/3D HDF5 datasets into a multichannel or time-series hyperstack
Saving a Fiji hyperstack to an HDF5 file with selectable lossless compression
Appending new time points or channels as additional datasets into an existing HDF5 file

name: HPC-Datastore
description: Provides Fiji client plugins to access and batch-download image data stored on an HPC DataStore server.
input_data: Image data hosted on an HPC DataStore server.
output_data: Downloaded image files (e.g., a series of TIFFs) and in-Fiji access to DataStore-hosted data via GUI client plugins.
use_when: Use this when you need to connect Fiji to an HPC DataStore server to browse or batch-download datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not have access to an HPC DataStore server or do not need to retrieve remote DataStore-hosted data.
typical_use_cases: Adding the HPC-DataStore update site to Fiji
Browsing DataStore-hosted datasets from Fiji using GUI client plugins
Batch downloading a DataStore dataset into a series of TIFF files

name: HPC-ParallelTools
description: Bridges Fiji with OpenMPI to run ImageJ macros, Jython scripts, and selected ImageJ2 Ops in parallel on an HPC cluster via a workflow manager GUI.
input_data: Fiji images/datasets and user macros or scripts (ImageJ macro language, Jython) uploaded to a remote OpenMPI-enabled cluster via the HPC Workflow Manager.
output_data: Downloaded remote job results (processed images and/or result files) plus job progress/status information and redirected stdout/stderr logs.
use_when: Use this when you need to parallelize Fiji image-processing tasks across large image sets or spatially large images using an OpenMPI-capable HPC cluster.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you do not have access to an OpenMPI-capable cluster (or cannot set up the provided virtual cluster) or when parallel execution is unnecessary.
typical_use_cases: Running an existing ImageJ macro in parallel over a large batch of microscopy images on an HPC cluster
Parallelizing processing of a single very large image by splitting it into sub-tasks and monitoring progress
Submitting, monitoring, and retrieving cluster jobs from Fiji using the HPC Workflow Manager GUI
Building parallel image-analysis pipelines using OpenMPI-enabled ImageJ2 Ops from Java or Jython

name: IamMM
description: Generates synthetic ImageJ images or stacks from 2D/3D mathematical functions or per-pixel boolean (ImageJ macro) expressions to create test patterns and example data.
input_data: User-defined 2D/3D functions f(x,y) or f(x,y,z) and per-pixel boolean conditional expressions written in ImageJ macro code over a specified coordinate range.
output_data: Newly generated ImageJ images or image stacks in any ImageJ image type, with intensities from function values or primitive patterns from conditionals.
use_when: Use this to create controllable synthetic images/test-cards for testing filters and algorithms, teaching concepts, or generating patterns for demos.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need to process or analyze acquired images rather than generate synthetic data.
typical_use_cases: Generating synthetic test-cards to evaluate filter behavior
Creating known-input images to test image-processing algorithms
Preparing example images for teaching or presentations
Generating primitive pattern images from boolean per-pixel conditions
Creating image stacks for demos, videos, or artistic visuals

name: IJ-Plugins
description: A core toolkit of ImageJ plugins providing 3D import/export, 3D stack operations, color tools, fast/aniso diffusion filtering, texture synthesis, and segmentation methods.
input_data: ImageJ images including stacks interpreted as 3D images, plus 3D datasets in supported 3D file formats via the 3D I/O plugins.
output_data: Imported/exported 3D datasets and processed images/stacks (e.g., filtered, color-converted, texture-synthesized, or segmented images).
use_when: Use this when you need the IJ Plugins Toolkit modules for 3D I/O, 3D stack operations, color processing, fast/aniso diffusion filtering, texture synthesis, or basic segmentation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you only need standard ImageJ/Fiji tools and do not require these specific 3D I/O, 3D stack, color, filtering, texture synthesis, or segmentation plugins.
typical_use_cases: Importing or exporting volumetric data in 3D file formats
Applying fast median or anisotropic diffusion filters to reduce noise
Performing 3D stack operations including morphological processing
Converting color spaces or running color edge detection on multiband images
Segmenting images using clustering, thresholding, or region growing

name: IJMMD
description: IJMMD is a Fiji scripting language plugin that runs ImageJ Macro files with Markdown-formatted multi-line comments and generates corresponding Markdown and HTML documents when executed.
input_data: IJMMD (.ijmmd) scripts or ImageJ Macro scripts containing Markdown in multi-line comments, executed from the Fiji script editor.
output_data: Generated markdown.md and HTML files documenting the script alongside its macro code.
use_when: Use this when you want to write ImageJ macros that double as tutorial-style notebooks with Markdown text and auto-generated HTML/Markdown output.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you only need to execute ImageJ macros without producing Markdown/HTML documentation.
typical_use_cases: Writing ImageJ macro tutorials with formatted Markdown explanations
Generating notebook-style Markdown and HTML pages from macros for teaching
Sharing reproducible macro workflows with embedded narrative text
Maintaining analysis notes and macro code together in a single script

name: Image Viewer
description: Provides ImageJ/Fiji plugins, tools, and macros to improve visualization and interactive handling of microscopy images, including LUT/contrast control, multichannel montages, thumbnail-based opening, and enhanced mouse interactions.
input_data: Opened ImageJ images and stacks (including multichannel composite images; virtual stacks supported) and LUT files from the ImageJ luts folder.
output_data: Updated display settings (LUTs, channel order, visibility, display mode, contrast ranges), thumbnail montage image saved to the source folder (Preview Opener), and optional ROIs (e.g., rectangular selections) created/edited via Multi Tool.
use_when: Use this when you need convenient, repeatable visualization control and faster interaction for multichannel microscopy images (LUT palettes, contrast, montages, scale bars, and thumbnail-based reopening).
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need quantitative image processing or analysis results, since it mainly changes display/interaction and produces limited saved outputs.
typical_use_cases: Managing multichannel display mode (composite/color/grayscale) and per-channel contrast with auto-contrast options
Building LUT palettes, reordering channels, and applying favorite LUTs consistently across images
Creating a saved Preview Opener thumbnail montage to reopen images by clicking thumbnails (optionally as virtual stacks)
Generating quick multichannel split-view montages (up to 5 channels) for visualization
Adding an estimated scale bar overlay with adjustable size and optional text

name: ImageJ Latex
description: Parses a LaTeX expression and uses an online equation editor to render it as an image that can optionally be inserted into an existing ImageJ window for annotation.
input_data: A LaTeX expression string and optionally an already-open ImageJ image window as the insertion target.
output_data: A rendered equation image and optionally the equation inserted into the target image as a selected region for further manipulation.
use_when: Use this when you need to generate and place typeset LaTeX equations into ImageJ/Fiji images with controllable size and resolution.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you cannot access the required online LaTeX rendering service (codecogs).
typical_use_cases: Adding a LaTeX equation annotation to a microscopy image
Generating a standalone equation image at a specified resolution for export
Inserting an equation into an existing image and then moving it with the arrow keys

name: ImageJ-ITK
description: Enables calling SimpleITK (ITK) functionality from ImageJ via the ImageJ-ITK update site, including automatic conversion between ImageJ Datasets and SimpleITK Images for scripting and commands.
input_data: ImageJ Dataset images, including images passed to scripts via @itkImage parameters.
output_data: SimpleITK Image objects in scripts and ImageJ Dataset outputs produced by SimpleITK filters.
use_when: Use this when you want to run ITK/SimpleITK image processing (especially segmentation or registration) from within ImageJ/Fiji using the Script Editor or ImageJ2 ops/commands.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot install the required large SimpleITK native library download or you do not need access to SimpleITK/ITK algorithms.
typical_use_cases: Calling SimpleITK classes from the ImageJ Script Editor
Running Otsu multiple-threshold segmentation via SimpleITK on an ImageJ Dataset
Writing an ImageJ2 Op or Command that wraps a SimpleITK filter
Automatically converting an ImageJ Dataset to a SimpleITK Image using @itkImage script parameters

name: ImageJ-MATLAB
description: Integrates ImageJ2 with MATLAB to run MATLAB scripts from ImageJ2 and convert data between ImageJ2 Datasets and MATLAB matrices, including launching ImageJ2 from MATLAB.
input_data: ImageJ2 Datasets/images (active Dataset) and MATLAB .m scripts executed via the ImageJ2 Script Editor; MATLAB matrices/arrays via a licensed MATLAB installation.
output_data: MATLAB matrices/arrays created from ImageJ2 images, ImageJ2 Datasets shown/created from MATLAB matrices, and execution of MATLAB scripts with explicit #@output parameters.
use_when: Use it when you need to execute MATLAB scripts from ImageJ2 or exchange image data between ImageJ2 Datasets and MATLAB matrices, including starting ImageJ2 inside MATLAB.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you lack a licensed MATLAB installation or need to evaluate MATLAB functions/return values directly (only scripts are supported and outputs must be explicit).
typical_use_cases: Running a MATLAB script from the ImageJ2 Script Editor on the active Dataset
Converting the active ImageJ2 Dataset to a MATLAB matrix using IJM.getDataset/getDatasetAs
Displaying a MATLAB matrix in ImageJ2 using IJM.show
Launching an ImageJ2 instance from MATLAB using the provided ImageJ.m script

name: IMCF Uni Basel
description: Fiji update site providing IMCF/Uni Basel custom macros, scripts and shortcut toolbars plus bundled plugins such as JaCoP for colocalization.
input_data: Any ImageJ/Fiji images supported by the installed macros and plugins (image types/formats not specified).
output_data: Installed macros/scripts/toolbars and plugin commands; analysis outputs depend on the selected tool (e.g., processed images and colocalization results).
use_when: Enable this update site when you need IMCF Uni Basel facility-provided macros/scripts, shortcut toolbars (ActionBars), or bundled plugins not included in standard Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use if you cannot enable the required IBMP-CNRS update site (needed for ActionBars) or if you only need standard Fiji components.
typical_use_cases: Installing IMCF shortcut toolbars via ActionBars
Running IMCF Auto Run macros for automated startup workflows
Using IMCF Tools macros/scripts for facility microscopy routines
Performing colocalization analysis with the JaCoP plugin

name: ImgLib2-Imaris-Bridge
description: Shares Imaris datasets with Fiji/ImageJ2 as lazily cached, readable and writable ImgLib2/ImageJ2 images (and optional BigDataViewer sources) without duplicating image data in memory.
input_data: Image data from datasets opened in a running Imaris instance accessed via the ImarisXT API (Imaris 5D XYZCT, including multi-resolution pyramids and larger-than-RAM data).
output_data: ImgLib2 CachedCellImg/Img views, ImageJ2 ImgPlus and Dataset objects with metadata, and BigDataViewer ChannelSources for the Imaris resolution pyramid (all backed by the same read/write cache).
use_when: Use this when you want to view, process, or write back very large Imaris-opened images from Fiji/ImageJ2 without loading the full dataset into RAM.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot connect to a running Imaris with an ImarisXT-capable license or if your data is not in Imaris and can be opened directly in Fiji.
typical_use_cases: Open an Imaris dataset in Fiji as an ImageJ2 Dataset/ImgPlus for scripting and UI display
Run ImgLib2 algorithms on larger-than-RAM Imaris images via lazy block caching
Visualize the full Imaris multi-resolution pyramid interactively in BigDataViewer
Create a new Imaris dataset from Fiji and write computed results back into Imaris

name: IMOD-ZOLA
description: Zernike Optimized Localization Algorithm for 3D single molecule localizations ZOLA-3D developed in Institut Pasteur, group IMOD -- a CUDA-enabled localization algorithm for extended PSF single-molecule localization microscopy. Paper is under review.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need IMOD-ZOLA's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying IMOD-ZOLA tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: Intensity Profile tools
description: Adds toolbar macro tools to display interactive horizontal and vertical intensity line profiles and profile panels for the active image.
input_data: Active ImageJ image (intensity image) in an open image window.
output_data: Two intensity profile plot windows (horizontal and vertical) and a cross-over overlay showing the current X/Y line profiles.
use_when: Use this when you want interactive inspection of horizontal and vertical intensity profiles at the current mouse position or as profile panels.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need profiles along arbitrary ROIs (angled/curved) or automated batch extraction.
typical_use_cases: Interactive inspection of X/Y intensity profiles in a microscopy image
Checking illumination gradients or shading via horizontal/vertical profiles
Exploring signal changes at specific pixel locations by hovering with the line profile tool
Quick QC by opening X/Y intensity profile panel windows

name: IsletJ
description: Plugin for evaluation of microscopy images of Langerhans islets. Provides estimate of area and count of islets, and an estimate of purity (endocrine-to-exocrine tissue ratio).
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need IsletJ's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying IsletJ tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: JavaCVInstaller
description: Centrally installs and manages JavaCV (Bytedeco) components and versions in Fiji/ImageJ, resolving dependencies and conflicts via Maven and the ImageJ updater.
input_data: User interface selections or macro/programmatic parameters specifying JavaCV version and components to install; no image data.
output_data: Installed/updated JavaCV jar dependencies via the ImageJ update mechanism and a local Maven cache folder (local-maven-repo) inside the ImageJ installation; may require restart.
use_when: Use this when you need to install, update, or switch JavaCV components/versions in Fiji in a centralized way for plugins or development code that depend on JavaCV.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for image analysis or file import/export by itself, since it only installs libraries and does not process images directly.
typical_use_cases: Installing JavaCV components (e.g., opencv, ffmpeg) required by another Fiji plugin
Resolving JavaCV version conflicts between multiple installed plugins
Silently installing missing JavaCV libraries from an ImageJ macro
Creating a local Maven cache to quickly switch between JavaCV versions during development

name: JOGL Canvas Deep Color / 3D
description: Replaces the ImageJ1 ImageCanvas with a JOGL OpenGL GLCanvas for GPU-accelerated deep-color (HDR) display and inline 3D volume rendering of image stacks.
input_data: ImageJ1 ImagePlus images, including multi-slice image stacks/volumes (and associated overlays/ROIs).
output_data: OpenGL-rendered display canvas (in-place replacement or external mirror window) and optional captured images/movies via the JOGL Canvas Recorder plugin.
use_when: Use it for interactive GPU-accelerated viewing of stacks as 3D volumes and/or higher-than-8-bit-per-channel display on HDR-capable hardware.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you need to save the rendered 3D view via normal ImageJ File->Save or if JOGL/OpenGL support is not available.
typical_use_cases: Interactive 3D volume rendering of a microscopy z-stack with rotate/pan/zoom
Viewing images in deep color/HDR on an HDR-capable monitor
Using an external JOGL mirror window to view 3D alongside the original 2D stack
Capturing screenshots or recording movies of the 3D render with JOGL Canvas Recorder

name: KTZ-LUTs
description: Provides a handmade collection of LUT (lookup table) color maps for ImageJ/Fiji for improved visualization of single- and multi-channel microscopy images and for temporal/depth color-coded projections.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji images where LUTs can be applied (single-channel or multi-channel stacks).
output_data: Images displayed with selected LUTs applied (including multichannel composite color schemes).
use_when: Use this when you want curated, aesthetically tuned LUTs for clearer single-channel display, better multichannel composites, or temporal/depth color coding.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need quantitative outputs or when applying a LUT could bias interpretation of intensity values (especially for non-visualization color coding).
typical_use_cases: Applying black-to-white style LUTs to single-channel fluorescence images
Using divergent/asymmetrical LUTs to visualize high dynamic range intensity images
Assigning custom 2-, 3-, or 4+-channel LUT sets for multichannel composite display
Applying temporal or depth color-coding LUTs to projection images

name: Lab-utility-plugins
description: A Fiji update site providing Jython utility scripts for common lab workflows including file blinding, batch format conversion, batch projection, ROI-based cropping, and quick display toggling.
input_data: Active ImageJ images (grayscale/color/composite), directories of microscopy image files (e.g., .tif and Bio-Formats-readable vendor files such as .czi, .lsm, .nd2, .oib, .lif), and ROI Manager .roi files.
output_data: Copied/renamed blinded file sets plus CSV key files, converted TIFF files in new output folders, projected image files saved to subdirectories, and cropped images generated from a provided ROI.
use_when: Use this when you need lightweight Fiji tools to automate repetitive lab tasks like blinding datasets, converting vendor formats to TIFF, batch projecting stacks, or applying the same ROI crop across many images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need robust, validated analysis workflows (segmentation/quantification) or when you specifically need Leica LIF tile extraction (the lif-tiles script is marked as not recommended).
typical_use_cases: Blinding a folder of microscopy images by copying them to a new 'blinded' directory with UUID filenames and generating a KEY.csv mapping
Batch converting vendor formats (CZI/LSM/ND2/OIB/LIF) to TIFF using Bio-Formats into a 'converted-tif' folder
Batch projecting Z-stacks with a chosen projection method and saving the results with cleaned filenames
Cropping a directory of images using a single saved ROI (.roi) and writing outputs to a 'cropped' subfolder

name: Leiden University
description: Provides access to Leiden University-developed Fiji/ImageJ plugins and an interactive teaching tutorial by enabling the LeidenUniv update site in Fiji.
input_data: Not specified; depends on the specific LeidenUniv plugin used within Fiji.
output_data: Not specified; depends on the specific LeidenUniv plugin used within Fiji.
use_when: Use this when you want to install Leiden University plugins via the LeidenUniv update site or run their interactive Fiji tutorial.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a single, well-defined analysis plugin with fixed inputs and outputs.
typical_use_cases: Activating the LeidenUniv plugin site in Fiji to install Leiden University methods
Starting the interactive tutorial via Plugins > LeidenUniv > Teaching > Interactive Tutorial
Using Leiden University-developed image analysis methods in Fiji
Connecting Fiji to a Leiden University OMERO server for analysis of OMERO-stored data

name: Lineage-Mapper
description: Lineage-Mapper links objects across consecutive frames to track cells and infer lineage events from a time series of labeled segmentation images stored on disk.
input_data: A directory of per-frame labeled segmented mask images (integer label images with background=0 and ROIs labeled 1..N) selected via a filename pattern.
output_data: Tracked label images saved to an output directory plus tracking metadata files including tracking-parameters.txt, birth-death.csv, confidence-index.csv, division.csv, fusion.csv, and an auto-generated lineage-viewer webpage folder.
use_when: Use this when you already have labeled segmentation masks for each time point and want tracked IDs plus division/fusion lineage metadata exported to disk.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need to segment or label raw images first, since the plugin expects pre-labeled masks and does not automatically label inputs.
typical_use_cases: Tracking segmented cells across a time-lapse using labeled mask images from an external segmentation workflow
Detecting and exporting mitosis (division) events into a division.csv lineage table
Handling merged objects by enabling fusion logic and exporting fusion events to fusion.csv
Generating an output lineage-viewer webpage to browse tracks and lineage relationships

name: Live-Kymographer
description: Generates calibrated kymographs from time-lapse images with a live preview and tools to annotate time periods on both the kymograph and the source image.
input_data: Grayscale time-lapse image stacks (RGB time-lapses are not supported).
output_data: Kymograph image(s) plus a table of saved annotation parameters and ROIs/overlays marking annotated time periods on the kymograph and source image.
use_when: Use this when you need to generate calibrated kymographs from time-lapse data and interactively annotate multiple time periods/events with a live preview.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this with RGB time-lapse inputs or with wide ROIs that extend outside the image, as pixel values can be incorrect.
typical_use_cases: Generating a kymograph from a microscopy time-lapse along a user-defined ROI
Using live preview to verify the kymograph before generating it
Annotating multiple time periods/events on a kymograph and saving them in a table for later import
Marking the corresponding spatio-temporal coordinates back on the source image

name: LLTT
description: Tracks low-light sub-resolution objects in fluorescence microscopy image stacks and hyperstacks using Gaussian maximum-likelihood localization (including an EMCCD noise model option).
input_data: 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit float image stacks; supports 3D stacks and multichannel hyperstacks (fluorescence microscopy time series).
output_data: Saved tracking sessions with object traces/trajectories and per-frame localization and flux/background estimates; kymograph and projection view windows for navigation.
use_when: Use this when you need localization-based tracking of very dim, point-like objects across frames in 2D or 3D fluorescence stacks/hyperstacks.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for non-stack images or for tracking extended/segmented objects where point-spread-function Gaussian localization is not appropriate.
typical_use_cases: Tracking dim single emitters/particles in low-SNR fluorescence time-lapse stacks
EMCCD data tracking using gain and electrons-per-count calibration (EMCCD-GaussianML)
3D point-like object tracking in volumetric time series using ΔZ ratio and side projections
Tracking fast-moving particles using multiscale (pyramid) tracking to reduce target loss

name: LMCF-IMG
description: Wavelet Denoise performs wavelet-based denoising/filtering of ImageJ images or stacks and can optionally display wavelet coefficient images.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji images or stacks with width and height that are powers of 2 (e.g., 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048).
output_data: Filtered image window (prefix "Filtered-"), optional 8-bit intensity-scaled wavelet coefficient image (prefix "WT-"), and optional float wavelet coefficient image without scaling (prefix "WT-NoStretch-").
use_when: Use this when you want wavelet-transform denoising/filtering with coefficient suppression or soft/hard VisuShrink thresholding and optional coefficient inspection.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the image width/height are not powers of 2 or when you need a non-wavelet denoising approach.
typical_use_cases: Denoising microscopy image stacks using soft or hard VisuShrink thresholding
Suppressing approximation and/or detail coefficients to smooth images
Inspecting wavelet coefficient images (WT) to tune denoising parameters
Exporting float wavelet coefficients (WT-NoStretch) for analysis in MATLAB

name: Local Z Projector
description: Performs a local z-projection of a 3D image stack by determining a reference plane.
input_data: 3D image stacks (z-stacks).
output_data: 2D projected image(s).
use_when: Use this when you need a z-projection that follows a locally determined reference plane.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only need a standard global z-projection or you do not have a 3D stack.
typical_use_cases: Projecting curved or tilted specimens by following a reference plane
Creating 2D projections from 3D z-stacks for visualization
Generating projection images for downstream 2D analysis

name: LSM-W2
description: LSM-W2 is a workflow for Zeiss .lsm multi-channel, multi-frame 3D microscopy images that assembles frames, enhances image quality, extracts a 2D leaf surface, segments cell and nuclear layers, and computes cell/nucleus volume and arrangement measurements with saved tables and intermediate images.
input_data: Zeiss laser scanning microscopy .lsm files (multi-channel, multi-frame 3D images).
output_data: Assembled/processed image stacks, 2D leaf-surface projection and surface mask, segmented cell/nuclei label images, intermediate saved images, and results tables (e.g., cell/nucleus volumes and arrangement metrics).
use_when: Use this when you need to assemble and analyze Zeiss .lsm confocal stacks to extract a leaf surface, segment cells/nuclei, and quantify morphology and cell–nucleus relationships.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your input is not a Zeiss .lsm file or you do not need the leaf-surface extraction and cell/nucleus-specific workflow.
typical_use_cases: Assembling multi-frame .lsm fragments with specified X/Y fragment counts and overlap into a complete 3D stack
Improving image quality by correcting shear/overlap and applying anisotropic diffusion filtering per slice
Building a 2D leaf-surface projection and surface mask from a 3D confocal stack using threshold/diving/lattice parameters
Segmenting cell-wall and nucleus layers using the integrated Morphological Segmentation (marked watershed) and manually editing regions
Computing and exporting cell and nucleus volumes and comparing their mutual arrangement as results tables

name: LUMoS
description: Performs blind spectral unmixing of multichannel fluorescence microscopy images using k-means clustering to separate fluorophore signals and background without reference spectra.
input_data: 2D or 3D multi-channel fluorescence microscopy images (optionally with time-lapse).
output_data: New multi-channel image with one channel per unmixed fluorophore plus an additional background-noise channel; optionally a background-removed image in background-only mode.
use_when: Use when you need to separate mixed fluorophore signals and/or isolate background/autofluorescence in multichannel fluorescence images without known spectra.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when the image is not multichannel fluorescence data or when you require supervised/linear unmixing based on known reference spectra.
typical_use_cases: Unmixing overlapping fluorophores in multichannel fluorescence stacks without reference spectra
Removing background noise from a multichannel fluorescence image using background-only mode
Separating autofluorescence into its own output channel by adding an extra fluorophore class
Creating a separate channel for colocalized pixels by treating colocalization combinations as additional fluorophores

name: LysoQuant
description: LysoQuant segments lysosomes in multichannel microscopy images using a U-Net model (via U-Net Segmentation/caffe_unet) and reports summary and optional per-lysosome measurements within selected ROIs.
input_data: Multichannel microscopy images opened in Fiji (e.g., TIFF such as test.tif/testRGB.tif), optionally with a selected cell ROI or ROIs in ROI Manager.
output_data: Lysosome segmentation results and measurement outputs as summary values and optional per-lysosome values (tables).
use_when: Use this to automatically segment lysosomes and quantify signal/cargo delivery in specified channels within a cell ROI (or whole image) using a U-Net model.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot run U-Net Segmentation with access to a Linux workstation running caffe_unet or if your data are not suitable multichannel microscopy images with distinct lysosome and cargo channels.
typical_use_cases: Segment lysosomes from a lysosome channel in multichannel TIFF images
Quantify cargo/protein signal within segmented lysosomes inside a selected cell ROI
Report per-lysosome measurements using ImageJ measurement settings
Compare summary lysosome-associated signal metrics across cells/ROIs

name: Mars-Latest
description: Mars is a Fiji/ImageJ2 platform with commands and a GUI (Mars Rover) for single-molecule image processing, localization/tracking, fluorescence integration, archive-based analysis, and OME-aware metadata handling.
input_data: 6D microscopy images (X/Y/Z/C/T with multiple positions) opened via SCIFIO with OME metadata; ordinary ImageJ videos/stacks (e.g., opened via Bio-Formats); CSV or YAMT tables; IJ1 ResultsTable; SciJava TableDisplay; Lumicks .h5 datasets; SMD JSON single-molecule datasets; PointRoi sets for ROI transforms.
output_data: Molecule/DNA/object archives (including virtual stores), MarsTables, localized and tracked coordinates with integrated intensity measurements, drift-corrected traces, change-point (KCP) results, transformed PointROIs, and kymograph/montage/interval exports.
use_when: Use Mars when you need OME-aware single-molecule localization/tracking and archive-based analysis with reproducible exploration in the Mars Rover GUI.
do_not_use_when: Do not use Mars when your acquisition changes excitation/emission conditions between frames without separate channels and you cannot convert the data into channel-separated videos/stacks.
typical_use_cases: Subpixel peak finding and tracking of single molecules in time-lapse microscopy
Building, merging, and exploring Molecule Archives (including virtual stores) in Mars Rover
Integrating fluorescence signals (including multiview) and applying drift correction to trajectories
Running kinetic change-point detection on molecule intensity traces
Creating kymographs, montages, and interval exports from DNA/object archives

name: Maskflow
description: A Fiji plugin that uses a pre-trained Mask R-CNN model to detect, segment, and centroid-track objects in images.
input_data: ImageJ2 Dataset images opened in Fiji, including time-lapse stacks.
output_data: Mask images (segmentation masks), results tables, and optional ROIs added to the ROI Manager; tracking results table.
use_when: Use this when you want to run the provided pre-trained Mask R-CNN model (microtubules) for detection/segmentation and simple centroid-based tracking in Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a mature, validated workflow or models for objects other than the provided microtubule proof-of-concept model.
typical_use_cases: Detecting and segmenting microtubules in microscopy images with the provided model
Producing a mask image and per-object measurements table from detections
Tracking detected objects across frames in a time-lapse stack using centroid linking
Running the combined detect-and-track command to output masks plus a tracking table

name: Masks from ROIs
description: Generates binary or multi-class (semantic) mask images from ROIs stored in the ROI Manager for single images or Z-stacks/virtual stacks.
input_data: Single-plane images or stacks/virtual stacks with one dimension slider, annotated with ROIs stored in the ROI Manager (hyperstacks not supported).
output_data: Mask images: binary masks (0 background, 255 inside ROIs) or semantic masks with pixel values set to ROI group; one mask per slice for stacks; sets Glasbey on dark LUT for semantic masks.
use_when: Use this to convert ROI annotations into per-image or per-slice ground-truth masks for large datasets.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for hyperstacks or when you need instance-separated labels for overlapping ROIs (overlaps merge in binary masks and are resolved by ROI Manager order in semantic masks).
typical_use_cases: Creating 0/255 binary masks from manually drawn ROIs
Generating semantic masks where ROI groups encode tissue or class labels
Exporting one mask per Z-slice using ROI Z-position in a stack or virtual stack
Saving masks next to originals using a filename suffix such as -mask

name: Mastodon-Benchmark
description: Provides a scripting-based framework to benchmark Mastodon display performance by measuring reaction times of BigDataViewer and TrackScheme view changes.
input_data: Mastodon projects created from BigDataViewer (BDV) XML/HDF5 datasets; scripted view-change sequences.
output_data: Timing/benchmark measurements (reaction times) reported via logs or results output.
use_when: Use it when you need reproducible performance measurements of Mastodon/BDV and TrackScheme GUI view updates under scripted scenarios.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for cell tracking, segmentation, feature computation, or quantitative image analysis tasks unrelated to GUI performance benchmarking.
typical_use_cases: Benchmarking BigDataViewer window reaction time to scripted navigation/view changes
Benchmarking TrackScheme window reaction time to scripted view changes
Comparing Mastodon display performance across machines or Mastodon versions
Automating reproducible GUI performance tests during development

name: Mcat
description: Mcat is an ImageJ/Fiji plugin toolkit for batch importing MSOT datasets and running pixel-wise cluster analysis with configurable parameters, exporting results to a chosen output folder.
input_data: MSOT datasets imported via the plugin's Batch import from a user-selected input folder (e.g., the provided ExampleData BatchImport folder).
output_data: Results exported to a user-selected output folder (clustered/processed images and associated analysis outputs).
use_when: Use this when you need batch import and pixel-wise clustering analysis of MSOT image datasets within Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your data are not MSOT datasets or you do not need pixel-wise clustering/cluster analysis outputs.
typical_use_cases: Installing Mcat via the Fiji Update Site and running it from the Plugins menu
Batch importing MSOT datasets from a folder for analysis
Adjusting analysis parameters and running the MSOT cluster analysis
Exporting clustered/processed results to a selected output directory

name: MiC mask comparator
description: Compares a test segmentation mask to a ground-truth mask in 2D (single images or stacks slice-by-slice) at pixel and IoU-based object level and reports TP/FP/FN counts, precision/recall/Jaccard/F1, overlays, plots, and result tables.
input_data: Two 2D segmentation mask images (truth and test), as single images or as stacks compared slice-by-slice; masks are treated as object masks (connected components) for object-level analysis.
output_data: Composite overlay image/stack, results tables (counts and metrics; optional varying-IoU table; optional object correspondence/IoU table), plots of metrics vs IoU (per-slice and optional stack summary), and ROIs loaded into ROI Manager (from ground-truth objects).
use_when: Use it to validate a 2D segmentation by comparing its mask to a ground-truth mask with pixel-level and object-level (IoU threshold or sweep) performance metrics.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for true 3D volumetric comparisons or when truth and test masks are not aligned and slice-corresponding (same field of view and dimensions).
typical_use_cases: Quantifying cell or nuclei segmentation accuracy against manual ground-truth masks
Comparing two segmentation pipelines using precision/recall/Jaccard/F1 at pixel and object level
Sweeping IoU thresholds to assess object-level sensitivity and failure modes (splits/merges)
Quality control of segmentation performance across a 2D image stack (slice-by-slice)

name: MIC-MAQ
description: [MIC-MAQ] ( is a plugin for automatic segmentation of nuclei and/or cells for quantifications in other channels including foci detection. MIC-MAQ automatically segments cells and/or nuclei on 2D microscopy images using thresholding or deep-learning approaches. It takes Z-stack but works only on 2D projection. It provides intensity based and/or morphological measurements in nuclei and/or cells in.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need MIC-MAQ's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying MIC-MAQ tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: Micro-Magellan
description: Micro-Magellan is a Micro-Manager plugin for interactive acquisition of large multi-field-of-view microscopy datasets using explore mode and user-defined grids and surfaces.
input_data: Live microscope image streams via Micro-Manager hardware control (multi-channel, tiled XY, Z-stack, and time-lapse acquisitions).
output_data: Saved multi-dimensional acquisition datasets plus acquisition region definitions (grids and interpolated surfaces) and associated metadata (e.g., overlap, pixel size calibration).
use_when: Use this when you need to navigate a sample in X/Y/Z and then acquire large tiled and/or arbitrarily shaped 3D regions over time using Micro-Manager.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not controlling a microscope through Micro-Manager or only need offline processing of already-acquired images.
typical_use_cases: Whole-slide or tissue-section tiled imaging across many fields of view
Multi-well plate imaging with repeated XY positions and Z-stacks
Imaging uneven or tilted samples by defining an interpolated surface for focus/volume selection
Running multiple sequential or parallel acquisitions with different regions and time-lapse settings

name: Microglia-Morphometry
description: Microglia-Morphometry is a Fiji plugin suite for semi-automated segmentation, tracking via persistent label IDs, and morphometric feature extraction of microglia in calibrated 2D time-lapse images.
input_data: Calibrated single-channel 2D TIFF time-lapse stacks (recommended 8-bit; units set to micrometer/micron) and, for continuation/morphometry, a corresponding label-mask TIFF stack.
output_data: Label-mask TIFF stacks with consistent cell labels across time, skeleton TIFF stacks, point-annotation TIFF stacks (soma/centroid-related), and per-cell morphometry CSV tables.
use_when: Use this for calibrated 2D single-channel microglia time-lapse data when you want semi-automated segmentation with manual correction, label-based tracking, and morphometry export.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for uncalibrated or non-micrometer images, multi-channel workflows, 3D data, or when you must rely on robust 16-bit processing.
typical_use_cases: Creating a tracked label-mask stack from a calibrated 2D microglia TIFF time-lapse
Continuing and correcting a partially completed segmentation/tracking label-mask stack
Computing per-cell shape, skeleton, and intensity features from an intensity stack plus label masks and exporting CSV
Generating skeleton and point-annotation stacks to review microglia morphology over time

name: Mighty Data, Inc.
description: Dendritic Spine Counter provides an interactive wizard to trace dendrites and mark or automatically detect dendritic spines on a grayscale, contrast-maximized working copy of the active image.
input_data: An active ImageJ image/dataset or an image file opened via the plugin's File Open dialog; intended for brightfield microscopy 2D images (e.g., MinIP from z-stacks).
output_data: A grayscale, contrast-maximized working image plus ImageJ overlays for traced dendrites and Multi-point Tool point selections representing spines.
use_when: Use this when you want a guided, interactive workflow to trace dendrite segments and count spines by placing/editing points manually or via simple automatic detection.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your images are not well-represented by dark features on a light background (without inversion) or when you need fully automated, non-interactive batch quantification.
typical_use_cases: Tracing dendrite centerlines with the Polyline Tool and refining estimated dendrite contours
Manually marking dendritic spines with the Multi-point Tool for later tabulation
Automatically detecting candidate spines along traced dendrite edges and then editing the points
Setting feature window size in pixels or physical units after configuring ImageJ scale

name: MoMA
description: MoMA is a Fiji plugin suite that automatically registers mother-machine time-lapse images, separates growth channels, and provides an interface to curate an automatically computed cell-tracking solution and export results.
input_data: Mother-machine time-series microscopy data provided either as a .tif image stack or as a single folder of 2D .tif images (one per time frame; optionally two files per time point for two colour channels) named with pattern _tXXXX_cY.tif.
output_data: New subfolders created inside the input folder for processing steps plus an analysis results folder (e.g., "3 analysed") containing exported analysis/tracking result files.
use_when: Use this for mother-machine time-lapse datasets when you need automated registration and growth-channel separation followed by track curation and export.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your data are not mother-machine time series or are not organized as a TIFF stack or a folder of per-frame TIFFs with the expected _tXXXX_cY.tif naming.
typical_use_cases: Registering mother-machine time-lapse frames to correct drift
Automatically separating growth channels for per-channel analysis
Curating an automatically generated cell-tracking solution in the MoMA UI
Exporting analysis and tracking results to an output folder for downstream processing

name: MotiQ
