description: An set of ImageJ plugins to quantify dynamics, morphology, and fluorescence of microglial (or other) cells in 2D and 3D (also time-lapse) images. For more details, a link to the corresponding publication, and information on how to cite the plugins visit the MotiQ github pages at
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need MotiQ's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying MotiQ tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: MS-ECS-2D
description: Provides Fiji tools to batch-segment 2D epithelial cells from membrane and vertex probability maps and to manually correct and cell-type annotate the resulting label masks for downstream transcript analysis.
input_data: PathFinder: 2D .tif images in three folders (original membrane-channel images, membrane classifier probability maps, vertex classifier probability maps) matched by alphabetical order; Cell annotation: a 2D labeled mask image (1-channel labels or 2-channel labels+existing annotation) plus the corresponding raw image with matching XY dimensions.
output_data: PathFinder: per-image 3-slice TIFF saved under [original image folder]/results/ (segmented cell labels, original image, binary paths); Cell annotation: exported 2-channel mask image (cell labels and cell-type annotation) and updated labels after correction.
use_when: Use when you have 2D tissue images with membrane/vertex classifier probability maps and need automated membrane-based cell segmentation plus interactive correction and cell-type annotation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when you do not have the required membrane and vertex probability maps (for PathFinder) or when the raw image and mask do not match in XY size or are not 2D (for Cell annotation).
typical_use_cases: Batch segmenting cells in 2D tissue sections with a single bright membrane channel using PathFinder.
Creating per-image cell label masks from membrane and vertex classifier outputs generated by the MS-ECS-2D KNIME workflow.
Manually fixing over-segmentation by erasing boundaries or splitting cells by drawing boundaries in the Cell annotation tool.
Assigning segmented regions to cell/tissue types (e.g., EVL, YSL, DEL, outside) and exporting a 2-channel labels+annotation mask for transcript analysis.

name: MTrack
description: Detects microtubule seeds and tracks growing microtubule ends in TIRF time-lapse movies to generate trajectories and length measurements.
input_data: TIRF microscopy data as either a two-channel hyperstack (seeds + dynamics), a concatenated 2D seed image followed by a 2D time-lapse, or a single-channel 2D time-lapse; any Fiji/Bio-Formats readable format (e.g., .tif, .nd2).
output_data: Per-track trajectory .txt files plus displayed Track ID movie and overlay movie.
use_when: Use it to detect seeds and track microtubule end growth from in vitro TIRF movies, optionally using a denoised/preprocessed movie for segmentation while measuring on the original.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for general-purpose object tracking or if you need an actively supported workflow (it is obsolete and replaced by a Napari kymograph-based plugin).
typical_use_cases: Detecting stabilized microtubule seeds and tracking plus-end growth in TIRF time-lapse movies
Tracking microtubules from two-channel hyperstacks containing seeds and dynamic microtubules
Running tracking on low-SNR movies using a denoised/preprocessed movie for segmentation but measuring on the original
Batch-processing multiple movies after saving parameters from a prior run

name: Multifrac
description: MULTIFRAC is an ImageJ/Fiji plugin for multiscale fractal, multifractal, lacunarity, and related analyses on 2D images and 3D stacks.
input_data: 2D grayscale or binary images (non-square allowed but may require resize/crop and binarization) and 3D image stacks opened in ImageJ/Fiji.
output_data: Result tables and saved plots (e.g., chi(q,l), tau(q), Dq, f(alpha)) plus saved analyzed images written to subfolders in the input image directory.
use_when: Use this when you need fractal/multifractal and other multiscale characterizations (including lacunarity and configuration entropy) of 2D images or 3D stacks.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only need standard intensity/shape measurements or segmentation without scale-dependent fractal or multifractal analysis.
typical_use_cases: Computing 2D fractal dimension via box counting on binary or grayscale images (counting black or white pixels)
Estimating 2D multifractal spectra (chi(q,l), tau(q), Dq, f(alpha)) using sliding box-counting
Running 2D/3D multifractal analysis using the gliding box method
Measuring 2D lacunarity, configuration entropy, and characteristic length for texture/structure characterization
Analyzing 3D stack fractal dimension and multifractal properties in volumetric datasets

name: MultiStackReg
description: MultiStackReg registers ImageJ/Fiji image stacks by aligning a stack to itself, aligning one stack to a reference stack, or applying a previously saved transformation file using TurboReg.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji image stacks (one or two stacks: reference and moving) and optional saved transformation (matrix) files.
output_data: Registered/aligned image stack(s) and reusable transformation (matrix) files.
use_when: Use this when you need intensity-based subpixel registration of one stack to itself or to a reference stack, including reuse of saved transforms from scripts/macros.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need feature-based or non-intensity-based registration, or when your data are not image stacks.
typical_use_cases: Correcting drift in a time-lapse stack by aligning frames
Registering a moving stack to a reference stack (e.g., channel-to-channel alignment)
Applying a previously saved transformation file to a stack
Automating stack registration from scripts or macros

name: NanoJ-VirusMapper
description: NanoJ-VirusMapper is a high-throughput ImageJ plugin for single-particle analysis in fluorescence microscopy, particularly super-resolution microscopy.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need NanoJ-VirusMapper's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying NanoJ-VirusMapper tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: NeuroCyto LUTs
description: A collection of LUTs packaged as an easy to use extra menu. LUTs are from various sources detailled in the About section.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need NeuroCyto LUTs's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying NeuroCyto LUTs tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: NoiSee
description: An ImageJ/Fiji macro suite that measures confocal microscope signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) using either a 2D+t fluorescent bead time-lapse or paired single-frame fluorescein and dark-field images.
input_data: Bio-Formats readable microscopy files: a 2D+t time-lapse with beads in a single Z-plane (bead method) or two single-frame 2D images (fluorescein image plus dark-field/laser-off image; no Z-stack, no time-lapse).
output_data: Saved txt summary of results in an output subfolder, optional per-bead txt measurement files, ROIs in the ROI Manager (optionally visualized as images), optional kymographs/plots, optional PDF report.
use_when: Use it to quantify and document confocal SNR from standard bead time-lapse acquisitions or fluorescein plus dark-field image pairs.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it for sub-resolution beads (beads should be at least ~10 pixels in diameter) or for data that are not 2D+t (beads) or single-frame 2D (fluorescein/dark-field).
typical_use_cases: Measuring confocal SNR from a 2D+t fluorescent bead time-lapse
Inspecting drift and bleaching via automatically generated kymographs
Computing SNR from a fluorescein solution image paired with a laser-off dark-field image
Saving a txt summary and PDF report for routine microscope performance monitoring

name: Nscale-segmentation
description: A customizable Fiji segmentation plugin that uses a user-defined scale factor plus Gaussian blur and outlier removal settings to better separate touching or merged nuclei.
input_data: An open Fiji/ImageJ image containing cellular nuclei (typically a nuclear channel microscopy image).
output_data: A segmented nuclei result image separating touching nuclei.
use_when: Use this when standard Fiji watershed/segmentation merges closely packed nuclei and you need improved separation by tuning a scale factor and related parameters.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when nuclei are already well separated or when the nuclei signal/contrast is insufficient for reliable segmentation.
typical_use_cases: Separating clusters of touching nuclei in fluorescence microscopy images
Reducing merged-nuclei errors to improve average nuclear size analysis
Tuning scale factor (1-10) to calibrate segmentation for different magnifications
Applying Gaussian blur and outlier removal to clean up nuclei segmentation

name: OpenMIMS
description: OpenMIMS opens, processes, and analyzes multi-isotope NanoSIMS 50/50L MIMS images and stacks, producing ratio/HSI visualizations and ROI-based plots and tables.
input_data: NanoSIMS 50/50L (Cameca) MIMS images and image stacks with up to 7 mass/isotope channels (including associated metadata).
output_data: Mass, ratio, sum, and HSI images; ROI sets/groups (including auto-generated ROIs from segmentation); ROI statistics plots and tables; reports; optional PNG exports; optional libSVM-format SVM/data export.
use_when: Use this when working with NanoSIMS/MIMS multi-mass datasets and you need corrections, ratio/HSI display, ROI quantification, or stack-based analysis.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for non-MIMS microscopy images or when you need advanced 3D reconstruction beyond simple stack plots/tables and alignment/translation tools.
typical_use_cases: Opening a multi-mass NanoSIMS stack and synchronizing slice browsing across masses
Applying QSA and dead time correction and generating ratio images with count thresholds
Creating HSI maps (including percent turnover display) for an isotopic pair
Plotting or tabulating ROI statistics through stack planes (tomography tab)
Training an SVM-based segmentation to auto-generate ROIs for a selected plane

name: OPTIMISME
description: OPTIMISME is an ImageJ plugin that performs 3D deconvolution (image restoration) of biphoton/two-photon microscopy data using a majorization-minimization optimization algorithm and a provided PSF image.
input_data: At least two open images in ImageJ: a 3D image stack to deconvolve (optionally multi-channel with selectable channel) and a 3D PSF image; common formats mentioned include TIFF and LSM.
output_data: A deconvolved/restored image stack; optionally a resampled (regridded) PSF image if the PSF and image resolutions differ.
use_when: Use this when you have a 3D two-photon/biphoton microscopy stack and a corresponding PSF and want to restore the image via deconvolution.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not have a suitable PSF image or if your data are not 3D stacks requiring deconvolution-based restoration.
typical_use_cases: Deconvolving in vivo two-photon mouse brain 3D stacks using a measured PSF
Resampling a PSF to match an image stack resolution before repeated deconvolution runs
Restoring blurred 3D fluorescence microscopy volumes prior to downstream analysis
Selecting a single channel from a multi-channel stack for deconvolution

name: Oxford Oncology
description: Provides custom Fiji/ImageJ tools and resources for users of the University of Oxford Department of Oncology Microscopy Core.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: Use this when you are a University of Oxford Oncology Microscopy Core user needing their locally supported Fiji/ImageJ tools.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a specific, well-documented analysis plugin with clearly defined inputs, outputs, and algorithms.
typical_use_cases: Installing and using the Oxford Oncology Microscopy Core Fiji update site tools
Running core-provided ImageJ utilities within an internal microscopy workflow
Using core-supported Fiji tools for analysis of microscopy datasets acquired at the facility

name: ParticleSizer
description: Automatically segments particles in electron microscopy images and measures nanomaterial primary-particle size and shape distributions, including minimal Feret diameter.
input_data: Electron microscopy images (e.g., TEM micrographs) opened in Fiji/ImageJ; can also analyze an image stack.
output_data: Results table of particle size/shape measurements (including feret min) and optional plots; optionally a binary segmentation image and a recorded processing-step image stack.
use_when: Use it to obtain automated, number-based size/shape distributions of primary particles (especially minimal Feret diameter) from electron microscopy images of particulate nanomaterials.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when particles cannot be reliably segmented (e.g., strong overlap or poor object-to-background contrast) or when required dependencies (update site and optional R/Rserve) are not available.
typical_use_cases: Measuring minimal Feret diameter distributions of primary particles from TEM images for nanomaterial definition
Splitting touching/agglomerated particles with irregular-structure watershed to estimate primary particle sizes
Using ellipse fitting mode to size slightly overlapping ellipsoidal particles
Exporting cumulative mass function (CMF) results for upload to the NanoDefiner e-tool

name: Pendent Drop
description: A plugin for capillary length measurement through the pendent drop method.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need Pendent Drop's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying Pendent Drop tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: PET-CT
description: Fiji update site providing Pet Ct Viewer and related plugins to read, view, and analyze nuclear medicine PET/CT DICOM studies from files or supported PACS sources.
input_data: DICOM PET/CT and nuclear medicine studies read from local media (e.g., CD) or via ClearCanvas and Beth Israel miniPACS readers.
output_data: Interactive PET/CT viewing in Pet Ct Viewer plus analysis outputs from included tools (e.g., saved DICOM, masks, postage-stamp images).
use_when: Use this when you need Fiji plugins from the PET-CT update site to load and work with PET/CT or nuclear medicine DICOM studies, including optional PACS-based readers.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not work with DICOM PET/CT or nuclear medicine studies or you cannot use the supported ClearCanvas/Beth Israel miniPACS integrations.
typical_use_cases: Viewing and navigating PET/CT DICOM studies with Pet Ct Viewer
Reading studies from multiple sources and auto-launching the viewer (Read_CD)
Running gastric emptying analysis on nuclear medicine studies
Running renal clearance analysis on nuclear medicine studies
Exporting results as DICOM, masks, or postage-stamp summary images

name: PHANTAST
description: PHANTAST segments phase-contrast microscopy images in Fiji to generate a binary mask and optionally compute culture confluency, including batch processing via macros or image stacks.
input_data: Phase-contrast microscopy images opened in Fiji (single images or image stacks) converted to 32-bit.
output_data: Binary segmentation mask image(s) (optional) and a Results table with confluency values.
use_when: Use this when you need automated segmentation and confluency measurement from many phase-contrast cell culture images using macros or stacks.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for non-phase-contrast images or if you cannot convert the image/stack to 32-bit in Fiji.
typical_use_cases: Computing confluency from a stack imported as an image sequence
Batch processing a folder of phase-contrast images with a Fiji macro
Exporting binary segmentation masks for downstream analysis

name: PhotoBend
description: PhotoBend is a collection of Fiji/ImageJ plugins that use OpenCV template matching to track bending rod-like objects or a laser spot in time-lapse image series and compute motion/curvature-related measurements.
input_data: Time-lapse image sequences opened as a virtual stack (8/16/32-bit grayscale or 24-bit RGB); Bending_Crystal_Track also supports movies opened as image stacks, while Laser_Spot_Track4 requires an image sequence.
output_data: Results table with per-frame filenames, time, tracked coordinates and derived values (e.g., curvature, deformation, spot shift), including stabilized/corrected coordinates (pixels and for Laser_Spot_Track4 also millimeters).
use_when: Use it when you need template-matching-based tracking and measurement of bending/curvature in rod-like objects or laser-spot motion relative to fixed reference marks in time-lapse images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you cannot provide a time-lapse image sequence/virtual stack (especially for Laser_Spot_Track4) or if your optics introduce strong non-linear distortion (e.g., wide-angle lenses) that the plugin cannot correct.
typical_use_cases: Tracking a needle-like crystal during photobending and measuring curvature and elongation/shortening over time
Stabilizing a time-lapse sequence using an immobile reference region and tracking the crystal tip and midpoint
Tracking a laser spot on a screen relative to a 4-corner mark template and exporting corrected coordinates in mm
Live monitoring of an acquisition folder to extend tracking as new time-lapse images are saved

name: PhotonImaging
description: Processes stacks of single-photon event TIFF images by localizing photon blob centers to create a combined grayscale photon-count image, with optional thresholding and reconstruction.
input_data: Directories of TIFF files opened as an ImageJ virtual stack (single-photon event image frames with light blobs).
output_data: High-resolution combined grayscale photon-count image; optionally a thresholded photon-count image and a reconstructed image.
use_when: Use this when you have many single-photon event frames and want to localize events and aggregate them into a photon-count image (optionally enhanced by thresholding/reconstruction).
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the input is not single-photon event data with isolated light blobs or when you do not have enough frames/blob size for reliable sub-pixel localization.
typical_use_cases: Opening a directory tree of TIFF photon-event frames as a virtual stack
Combining a stack of photon-event frames into a single photon-count image using simple/accurate/sub-pixel localization
Thresholding the photon-count image to suppress noise and rescale intensities
Reconstructing a photon-limited image using the Morris et al. algorithm

name: PillarTracker
description: PillarTracker detects and tracks micropillar positions in time-lapse images, corrects drift, and performs grid-based analysis and visualization of trajectories and deflections.
input_data: Time-series microscopy image stacks of periodic micropillar arrays (e.g., MDA .dv stacks) with calibration or known pixel size/pillar spacing.
output_data: Drift-corrected pillar tracks and deflections written to .bin binary files plus CSV/text exports, along with ImageJ multipoint selections/ROIs, track/deflection map images, plots, and Results tables.
use_when: Use this when you have a regular micropillar array time-lapse and need robust pillar localization/tracking with drift correction and grid-referenced deflection analysis.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the image is not a periodic pillar array time series or when pillars cannot be localized reliably (e.g., strong background without mitigation for CM or non-Gaussian pillar profiles for GF).
typical_use_cases: Tracking micropillar deflections over time for traction force microscopy
Correcting stage/sample drift and stabilizing a time-lapse sequence
Estimating pillar spacing and grid orientation using FFT peaks to set parameters
Exporting drift-corrected tracks/deflections to CSV or binary for Matlab/Python analysis
Visualizing trajectories and deflection vectors as track/deflection maps and movies

name: PixFRET
description: PixFRET computes pixel-by-pixel sensitized-emission FRET and normalized FRET images from three-channel fluorescence stacks using donor/acceptor spectral bleed-through correction estimated from control stacks or entered manually.
input_data: ImageJ stacks: 3-slice stack (FRET, donor, acceptor) for sample; 2-slice donor-only stack (FRET, donor) and 2-slice acceptor-only stack (FRET, acceptor) for bleed-through estimation; typically TIFF stacks.
output_data: 32-bit FRET and NFRET images (with below-threshold pixels set to zero/blue in LUT), optional display of blurred images, and saved settings/log files (pixFret_settings.txt) containing parameters and SBT fit values.
use_when: Use when you have sensitized-emission three-channel FRET data and need pixelwise FRET/NFRET maps with donor and acceptor bleed-through correction derived from controls or manual SBT values.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when your FRET quantification is based on acceptor photobleaching or FLIM rather than three-channel sensitized-emission imaging with appropriate donor-only and acceptor-only controls.
typical_use_cases: Creating pixelwise FRET and NFRET images for cells imaged in FRET, donor and acceptor channels
Estimating donor bleed-through from donor-only control stacks using ROI-based constant/linear/exponential fits
Estimating acceptor bleed-through from acceptor-only control stacks using ROI-based constant/linear/exponential fits
Applying different NFRET normalizations (donor, acceptor, product, sqrt product) to compare samples

name: PlateViewer
description: PlateViewer is a BigDataViewer-based Fiji plugin for interactive visual inspection and browsing of high-throughput multi-well plate microscopy image data with optional segmentation label-mask and linked table/scatterplot visualization.
input_data: Microscopy image files in a plate images directory (optionally including sub-folders) matching a user regex (e.g., .h5 or .tif) and following supported plate/well/site naming schemes; optionally an associated per-image table.
output_data: Interactive BigDataViewer plate/well/site views with optional label-mask overlay plus linked table and scatterplot views; optional GitHub issue reports with screenshots and plate/well/site metadata.
use_when: Use this to visually QC and browse large multi-well screening datasets with lazy loading, optionally overlaying segmentation masks and linking to per-image tables/scatterplots.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your files do not follow one of the supported naming schemes or if you need automated batch processing/quantification rather than interactive inspection.
typical_use_cases: Visual QC browsing of multi-well plate screening images with lazy loading
Inspecting plate, well and site layouts for imaging artifacts or failed acquisitions
Overlaying and checking segmentation label masks on raw microscopy images
Exploring per-image metadata via linked table and scatterplot while browsing images
Creating GitHub issues with automated screenshots and plate/well/site context for collaboration

name: PQCT
description: Analyzes Stratec pQCT bone and soft-tissue cross-sectional images to compute standard bone/soft-tissue metrics and regional/concentric density distribution results.
input_data: An open pQCT cross-sectional image in ImageJ/Fiji (Stratec pQCT images and DICOM pQCT images).
output_data: Results tables of bone and soft-tissue measurements (e.g., CoA/CoD, ToA/ToD, SSI, marrow, fat/muscle areas and densities) plus an optional visual result image (displayed and/or saved to disk).
use_when: Use this for conventional pQCT bone and soft-tissue analysis and for regional, polar, or concentric density distribution calculations on pQCT slices.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need meaningful medullary-cavity-based density distribution outputs for bones without a medullary cavity.
typical_use_cases: Computing cortical area/density, total area/density, and SSI from tibia or radius pQCT slices
Quantifying muscle, lean muscle, intramuscular fat, and subcutaneous fat areas/densities from pQCT images
Running polar (10° sectors) density distribution to assess endo-/peri-cortical radii and densities
Running concentric (10-ring) density distribution for distal bone where medullary cavity is absent or unclear

name: Puncta Process
description: Pipeline plugin for preparing image data, identifying puncta counts, and exporting summary results to MS Excel and CSV.
input_data: Image data opened in Fiji/ImageJ (microscopy images containing puncta; exact formats/channels not specified).
output_data: Puncta count summaries exported to MS Excel and CSV files.
use_when: Use this when you want a Fiji pipeline to prepare images, quantify puncta counts, and export summarized results to Excel/CSV.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot run Fiji with required dependencies (e.g., MorphoLibJ) or if your images are not suitable for automated puncta counting.
typical_use_cases: Counting puncta in microscopy images and exporting results to CSV
Generating MS Excel summary reports of puncta counts
Running a standardized puncta-counting workflow in Fiji across multiple datasets

name: Qualitative Annotations
description: Annotates images or ROI-defined regions in Fiji with user-defined qualitative categories/keywords and optionally records selected Fiji measurements into a results table and ROI properties.
input_data: Open images in Fiji (full image or selected ROIs in the ROI Manager); for the dropdown plugin, a csv (comma or semicolon separated) or tsv file defining features and choices; optionally a text file with one category per line to initialize categories.
output_data: Results table containing annotations (single category column or one 0/1 column per category, or feature-choice columns for dropdown) plus optional Fiji measurements; ROI Manager entries with ROI name recorded in the table and annotations/measurements stored as ROI properties; optional pie/bar chart plots from a table column.
use_when: Use this to assign qualitative labels to whole images or specific ROIs for documentation, dataset curation, or generating ground-truth for downstream analysis/classifier training.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need ImageJ (non-Fiji) compatibility or if you require pixel-wise segmentation masks rather than per-image/per-ROI categorical labels.
typical_use_cases: Assigning one class per image using button-based single-class annotation
Tagging images with multiple keywords using checkbox-based multi-class annotation
Annotating multiple ROIs per image and saving labels as ROI properties retrievable by macros/scripts
Capturing standard Fiji measurements alongside qualitative labels in the same results table
Visualizing category distributions from an annotation table column as pie or bar charts

name: QuickFigures
description: QuickFigures is an ImageJ/Fiji plugin for assembling and editing publication-quality multi-panel figures from microscopy and other images with consistent layouts, labels, and accurate scale bars, with export to common figure formats.
input_data: Open ImageJ images (including multichannel hyperstacks) and image files dragged into a worksheet; microscopy formats can be imported via Bio-Formats (loci_tools).
output_data: Editable figure worksheets containing image panels/layouts, shapes, text labels, insets and scale bars, with optional export to SVG, PDF, EPS, and PowerPoint.
use_when: Use this when you need to quickly assemble and edit consistent multi-panel figures (split channels, merges, insets, labels, scale bars) directly in ImageJ/Fiji for publication.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your primary goal is quantitative analysis (e.g., segmentation, tracking, or measurement) rather than figure assembly and presentation.
typical_use_cases: Building multi-panel fluorescence microscopy figures with split channels and merged composites
Creating inset (zoom) panels from a selected region of an image panel
Assembling western blot/gel figures with lane-style layouts and labels
Exporting finished figures to SVG/PDF/EPS or PowerPoint for final editing

name: QuimP
description: QuimP is software for tracking cellular shape changes and dynamic distributions of fluorescent reporters at the cell membrane.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need QuimP's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying QuimP tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: Radial Symmetry
description: Localizes radially symmetric spots (e.g., smFISH puncta) with super-resolved accuracy in 2D/3D microscopy images including time series, using optional RANSAC outlier removal and optional Gaussian fitting.
input_data: 2D/3D microscopy images (including time dimension) and optional bead images for z-anisotropy calibration.
output_data: Results table of detected spots (coordinates plus time/channel/intensity columns), log output (anisotropy coefficient), and optional image overlays (support regions and detected spots).
use_when: Use it when you need accurate localization and intensity estimation of point-like, approximately radially symmetric spots in 2D/3D (and time-lapse) images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when targets are not spot-like/radially symmetric or when you need segmentation of extended structures rather than point localization.
typical_use_cases: Localizing smFISH RNA puncta in 2D images or 3D stacks
Estimating and applying z-anisotropy correction from bead images
Interactive parameter tuning (DoG + RANSAC) followed by manual processing
Batch spot localization on large datasets via ImageJ macro

name: RadialIntensityProfile
description: Measures the radial intensity distribution of a red-channel fluorescence signal in annular bands around the nucleus center determined by thresholding the blue channel in single-cell RGB images.
input_data: Cropped single-cell RGB images with nuclear stain in the blue channel (DAPI or similar) and signal of interest in the red channel.
output_data: Results table/profile of red-channel intensity measured in concentric annular bands around the nucleus center of mass.
use_when: Use this to quantify perinuclear versus peripheral signal localization by computing a radial intensity profile relative to the nucleus center.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when images are not RGB with nucleus in blue and signal in red, or when the crop contains more than one cell/nucleus or the nucleus cannot be reliably thresholded.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying perinuclear localization of a protein relative to the nucleus
Comparing radial fluorescence profiles between experimental conditions
Measuring annular-band intensity distributions around a DAPI-stained nucleus
Assessing changes in subcellular signal distribution after perturbations

name: ReadPlate
description: ReadPlate measures per-well RGB/gray intensity from a centered photograph of a 6–96 well microplate and computes absorbance with optional local blank suppression using surrounding reference circles.
input_data: Single color image of a multi-well plate opened in ImageJ (typically a .jpg photograph; RGB with selectable red/green/blue/gray channel).
output_data: Overlayed grid of measurement circles on the image and a Results table (per-well number/label, area, mean, stddev, mode, min, max, absorbance and corrected absorbance) with CSV export.
use_when: Use this to extract per-well intensity and (blank-corrected) absorbance from one well-plate photograph for standard 6, 12, 24, 48 or 96 well layouts.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when the plate image is not centered/approximately undistorted or when the plate layout is non-standard or outside 6–96 wells.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying a photographed 96-well colorimetric assay by measuring the green channel
Computing corrected absorbance without imaging an empty plate using local blank suppression circles
Exporting per-well absorbance values to CSV for analysis in Excel or statistics software
Comparing repeated images of the same plate using consistent grid parameters

name: ROI 1-click Tools
description: Adds 1-click toolbar ROI tools that create predefined ROIs centered on a click, optionally add them to ROI Manager, run Measure, and advance through stack slices.
input_data: Any ImageJ/Fiji image where ROIs can be drawn, including stacks.
output_data: Generated ROIs (optionally added to ROI Manager), measurements in the Results table, and optional additional images/actions via custom macro commands (e.g., Duplicate/crop).
use_when: Use this when you need fast, repeatable manual placement of the same ROI shape for measurements or annotations across many points or slices.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need fully automated ROI detection/segmentation or ROIs that must adapt to object boundaries without manual clicking.
typical_use_cases: Measuring mean intensity of many dots in dot blot images with a fixed circle ROI
Quantifying gel bands using a fixed rectangle ROI across lanes
Placing repeated ROIs through a stack while auto-advancing to the next slice
Reusing a freehand or polygon model ROI via the custom ROI tool
Running a custom on-click macro action such as duplicating/cropping the clicked region

name: ROI-group Table
description: Provides an interactive table interface to define and edit category names associated with ImageJ/Fiji ROI group integer values as an alternative to the ROI Defaults dialog.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji ROIs that use the ROI group (integer) attribute and the existing ROI group name settings (Edit > Options > Roi Defaults).
output_data: Updated ROI group category names stored in ImageJ/Fiji ROI Defaults settings.
use_when: Use this when you need to conveniently define or edit many ROI group category names for consistent ROI annotation.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not use ROI group numbers/colors for ROI categorization or only need to change a single group name in the standard ROI Defaults dialog.
typical_use_cases: Defining names for multiple ROI groups before starting manual ROI annotation
Editing an existing set of ROI group category names more efficiently than the ROI Defaults dialog
Standardizing ROI group naming conventions for a project that uses group colors to indicate classes

name: RT-Multiview-Deconvolution
description: Computes a single fused 3D reconstruction from multiple registered 3D views of the same specimen using iterative multi-view deconvolution with per-view PSF estimates, for single timepoints or time series.
input_data: Multiple registered 3D view image stacks (optionally time series) with corresponding registration files (*.registration, possibly time-series registrations) and PSF estimates per view (extracted from bead-based registration or provided as 3D PSF image(s)).
output_data: A fused/deconvolved 3D image (or time series) that can be displayed, saved as slices to an output directory, and optionally written into separate directories per timepoint; optional virtual-stack displays of extracted PSFs and intermediate iterations in debug mode.
use_when: Use it when you have multiple aligned 3D views of the same sample and want a higher-quality single volume via iterative deconvolution using per-view PSFs.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when registration files are missing or the views are not well aligned, or when you cannot provide reasonable PSF estimates for the views.
typical_use_cases: Deconvolving multi-view SPIM/light-sheet datasets into one 3D volume
Running multi-view deconvolution over an entire time-lapse series
Extracting PSFs from bead-based registration and using them for deconvolution
Running GPU (CUDA) block-wise deconvolution to reduce RAM usage

name: Sceptical Physiologist
description: A collection of assorted ImageJ plugins and other code projects distributed via the author's Fiji update site and GitHub.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: Use this when you want to install or browse the author's assorted ImageJ plugins via their Fiji update site or GitHub.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need a single specific, well-documented plugin with clearly defined inputs and outputs.
typical_use_cases: Adding the author's Fiji update site to install their plugins
Browsing the GitHub repository for ImageJ plugin source code
Contacting the author to report bugs or ask questions

name: ScientiFig
description: ScientiFig is an ImageJ/Fiji tool for assembling publication-ready scientific figures from images into multi-panel layouts for export.
input_data: Images opened in ImageJ/Fiji (e.g., microscopy images).
output_data: Assembled multi-panel figure layouts and exported figure files.
use_when: Use this when you need to assemble and export publication-ready multi-panel figures directly from ImageJ/Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need image processing/quantitative analysis rather than figure assembly.
typical_use_cases: Assembling multi-panel microscopy figures with consistent sizing and alignment
Creating composite figures combining multiple images into a single layout
Exporting final figures for manuscripts, posters, or presentations

name: Scijava Jupyter Kernel
description: Provides a SciJava-based Jupyter kernel for running Java/SciJava code in Jupyter notebooks (project retired).
input_data: Jupyter notebook code cells executed via a SciJava/Java kernel
output_data: Notebook cell outputs such as text and returned objects
use_when: Use this when you need a SciJava-based Java kernel to execute code inside Jupyter notebooks and are working with an existing setup.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for new installations because the project is retired; use an alternative such as IJava or BeakerX instead.
typical_use_cases: Executing Java/SciJava code in Jupyter notebooks
Interactive exploration of SciJava APIs in a notebook
Reproducing legacy notebooks that depend on the SciJava Jupyter kernel

name: SciJava Ops
description: A framework for extensible algorithm discovery, matching, and execution in Fiji, including an image-processing Op collection for building declarative workflows and scripts.
input_data: Op-dependent; commonly images (2D/3D), ROIs/masks, numeric parameters, and other SciJava-supported data structures provided via scripting or OpBuilder.
output_data: Op-dependent; commonly processed images, label images, ROIs, and result tables/measurements.
use_when: Use this when you need to search for, compose, and run reusable algorithms (Ops) in Fiji from scripts or declarative workflows rather than a single fixed plugin command.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you want a simple end-user GUI tool with a fixed purpose and fixed inputs/outputs instead of a general algorithm framework.
typical_use_cases: Running Richardson–Lucy total variation deconvolution on microscopy images
Performing fluorescence lifetime (FLIM) analysis with calibration and ROI subsampling
Applying denoising such as fast non-local means (OpenCV) or Gaussian blur subtraction
Running spatially adaptive colocalization analysis to generate result tables
Segmenting 3D puncta and producing labeling outputs and measurement tables

name: sciview
description: sciview is a Fiji/ImageJ plugin for interactive 3D visualization and exploration of volumetric images together with meshes, point clouds, and 3D segmentations, with optional VR/AR support.
input_data: 3D volumetric image stacks and derived 3D objects (meshes, point clouds, 3D segmentations/labels) available in Fiji/ImageJ.
output_data: Interactive 3D scene visualization (rendered volumes and objects).
use_when: Use this when you want to interactively inspect and navigate 3D image volumes alongside segmentations and other 3D objects, optionally in VR/AR.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only need standard 2D viewing or fully automated, non-interactive batch processing.
typical_use_cases: Viewing an EM volume together with segmented neurons
Interactive navigation and inspection of 3D volumetric datasets
Visualizing 3D segmentations/label volumes in context of the raw data
Exploring 3D scenes in VR/AR for immersive inspection

name: SimonKlein
description: Measures linear intercept distances (automatic on binarized two-phase images or interactive marking) and estimates domain fractions by point-grid or random point counting.
input_data: Binary (black/white) 8-bit two-phase images for automatic linear distance scanning; any image type for interactive linear distance marking; single images for point analysis with optional H/S/B thresholding (0-255).
output_data: Results/printed measurements including stripe-length statistics (means, optional standard deviations and counts) and point counts per domain.
use_when: Use this to quantify mean free path or grain size via linear intercepts, or to estimate phase/domain fractions using point counting in microstructure images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need full segmentation or area/volume measurements from labeled regions rather than intercept- or point-based estimates.
typical_use_cases: Compute mean free path from binarized two-phase microstructure images using automatic X/Y line scans
Measure grain size in single-phase metals by interactively placing intercept marks
Estimate phase fractions by counting grid or random points across an image
Batch-analyze multiple opened images using image calibration for units

name: SiMView
description: SiMView is a simultaneous multiview light-sheet microscope platform for high-speed volumetric in vivo imaging of large living specimens over long time periods with minimal phototoxicity.
input_data: Multiview light-sheet fluorescence microscopy image volumes/time-lapse datasets acquired with a SiMView microscope.
output_data: Multiview volumetric image datasets suitable for downstream reconstruction and analysis (e.g., cell tracking and functional imaging).
use_when: Use this when you need fast, long-term, non-invasive multiview light-sheet imaging of entire embryos or other large living specimens for quantitative analysis.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you do not have access to SiMView-style multiview light-sheet acquisition or only need conventional single-view/confocal/two-photon imaging.
typical_use_cases: In toto time-lapse imaging of entire Drosophila embryos for automated cell tracking during embryogenesis
Whole-embryo imaging of zebrafish development with comprehensive lineage reconstruction
Whole-brain functional imaging in larval zebrafish at single-cell resolution
Whole-animal functional imaging in embryonic or larval Drosophila

name: SIMworks
description: SIMworks is a Fiji suite for SIM-specific data format conversion, quality control, preprocessing, segmentation, and quantitative analysis of raw and reconstructed structured illumination microscopy images.
input_data: Raw and reconstructed SIM fluorescence image stacks, including raw SI datasets in formats/axis orders such as C;Z;P, C;Z;A(P()), C;Z(A(P())), C;A(Z(P()));T, C;-;A(Z(P())), C;T(Z(A(P()))), and Nikon N-SIM tiled Z(P*A).
output_data: Converted SIM stacks in C;A(Z(P()));T organization, QC visualizations (intensity profiles, CMY composites, Fourier projections/plots, histograms), projected-widefield images, preprocessed/registered/cropped images, per-object image files from main-object segmentation, foci/structure segmentations, and distance/chromatin-related measurement results.
use_when: Use this for end-to-end handling of SIM datasets when you need SIM-specific QC plus preprocessing, segmentation, and measurements such as nearest-neighbour distances and chromatin-class analyses.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for general non-SIM imaging workflows or when you only need basic Fiji operations without SIM format conversion and SIM-specific QC.
typical_use_cases: Convert raw SIM datasets from different microscopes into a consistent C;A(Z(P()));T format for downstream analysis
Check raw SIM acquisition quality using channel intensity profiles, CMY angle composites, Fourier projections, and modulation contrast
Assess reconstructed SIM quality using intensity histograms, spherical aberration mismatch plots, and Fourier plots with resolution rings
Preprocess reconstructed SIM in batch (threshold/16-bit conversion, bandpass filtering, modulation-contrast masking, channel registration and cropping)
Segment main objects and foci/structures and quantify nearest-neighbour distances and foci positions relative to chromatin classes

name: Slide Set
description: Slide Set organizes image files into project tables, lets you define per-image ROI sets, and runs ImageJ2/Slide Set commands in batch to store results in linked tables and export them.
input_data: Image files referenced in table columns (File > Image) plus optional ROI sets created/loaded as SVG files.
output_data: Linked results tables with numeric outputs (e.g., Pearson correlation coefficient), ROI set SVG files saved to disk, and optional CSV exports of tables.
use_when: Use this when you need to manage a dataset in tables and apply the same analysis command across many images with consistent parameter and ROI handling.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only need ad-hoc interactive analysis on a small number of images without table/project organization.
typical_use_cases: Creating a Slide Set project and adding image files to a data table column
Assigning experimental group labels in a text column for each image
Drawing and saving per-image ROI sets with the Slide Set ROI editor (SVG)
Running Pearson's correlation on two-channel images across a table and storing coefficients in a linked results table
Exporting a results table to CSV for downstream analysis

name: SlideBook
description: This Bio-Formats plugin enables the import of SlideBook SLD files.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need SlideBook's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying SlideBook tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: SLIM Curve
description: Fits per-pixel fluorescence/spectral lifetime decay curves from time-resolved datasets to generate lifetime parameter images and fit-quality outputs using rapid lifetime determination and Levenberg–Marquardt fitting.
input_data: Time-resolved lifetime image datasets loaded from .sdt or .ics files (photon counts over time bins; optionally an excitation/IRF dataset).
output_data: Summed-count grayscale intensity image, fitted-parameter images (e.g., tau, background, chisquare, fractions/means), fitted decay and residual plots, histogram/LUT panels, and optional text export of fitted values.
use_when: Use it when you need to fit single-pixel, ROI-summed, or per-pixel exponential (single/double/triple or stretched) lifetime decays from FLIM/SLIM data and visualize parameter maps.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it if you need a supported/maintained FLIM fitting workflow, since SLIM Curve is obsolete and discontinued in favor of FLIMJ.
typical_use_cases: Generating lifetime (tau) and chi-square maps from an .sdt FLIM acquisition
Refining rapid lifetime estimates with Levenberg–Marquardt single/double/triple exponential fits
Fitting and inspecting decay/residuals for a selected pixel while adjusting fit cursors and thresholds
Exporting ROI-based fitted parameters to a text file for downstream analysis
Using an excitation/IRF measurement to improve lifetime fitting accuracy

name: SLIM-Notts
description: Provides University of Nottingham SLIM image analysis information and links to software options for microscopy tasks such as deconvolution, colocalisation, measurement and tracking.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Information/resources about image analysis software and applications (no specific ImageJ outputs described).
use_when: You want guidance on microscopy image analysis software and common applications referenced by the University of Nottingham SLIM facility.
do_not_use_when: You need an actual ImageJ/Fiji plugin with defined functionality, inputs and outputs for processing images.
typical_use_cases: Finding software options for deconvolution of microscopy data stacks
Identifying tools for colocalisation measurement in fluorescence microscopy
Planning workflows for quantifying areas, volumes and ROIs
Reviewing options for 3D/4D cell or molecule tracking

name: Spindle3D
description: Automates 3D segmentation and morphometric measurement of mitotic spindle and chromatin morphology from two-colour confocal image stacks, optionally constrained by a cell mask.
input_data: Calibrated 2-channel 3D confocal fluorescence image stacks (microtubules/tubulin in one channel, chromatin/DNA in the other); optional binary cell mask TIFF named [input]_CellMask.tif with same dimensions and calibration (cell mask only supported for .tif inputs).
output_data: Results table of spindle/chromatin (and optional cell mask) morphometrics plus output images including spindle-axis-oriented views and binary spindle/chromatin masks for verification.
use_when: Use this to quantify 3D spindle and metaphase plate morphology from cropped, calibrated two-channel confocal stacks where spindle and chromatin are fully contained within the image volume.
do_not_use_when: Do not use when spindle or chromatin touches any image border, when voxel calibration is missing/incorrect, or when structures are far outside the expected size range (about 5–20 µm pole-to-pole).
typical_use_cases: Measuring spindle length, width (min/max/avg) and volume from tubulin-labelled 3D confocal stacks
Quantifying metaphase plate length/width, chromatin volume and chromatin dilation from DNA-labelled stacks
Batch-processing many cropped mitotic spindles to generate a morphometry results table
Restricting analysis to a single cell using an accompanying binary cell mask to report cell volume and surface area

name: Stereoscopic 3D Projection
description: Creates 3D stereoscopic projections (anaglyph or Google Cardboard compatible) from image stacks.
input_data: Image stacks (z-stacks); anaglyph mode requires grayscale (color stacks must be converted to grayscale).
output_data: 2D stereoscopic projection images (anaglyph or Google Cardboard/side-by-side format).
use_when: Use this to visualize depth in a 3D image stack via stereoscopic projection for anaglyph glasses or Cardboard viewing.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for quantitative 3D analysis or when you need to keep a color stack for anaglyph output.
typical_use_cases: Generating a red-cyan anaglyph from a microscopy z-stack for 3D viewing
Creating a Google Cardboard compatible side-by-side projection from a volumetric stack
Previewing 3D structure by adjusting slice spacing and rotation parameters

name: Stowers
description: A collection of ImageJ/Fiji plugins by Jay Unruh (Stowers Institute) distributed via the Fiji update site "Stowers" for microscopy image analysis.
input_data: Primarily 32-bit ImageJ images; some plugins also support 16-bit and 8-bit images.
output_data: Varies by plugin; typically processed images and analysis results within ImageJ/Fiji.
use_when: Use this when you want to install and use the Stowers plugin collection via the Fiji update site for microscopy workflows.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you need supported/guaranteed tools or cannot convert images to 32-bit when required.
typical_use_cases: Adding the Stowers Fiji update site and installing the plugin collection
Converting images to 32-bit to run Stowers plugins that require float images
Updating the Stowers plugins from the correct update URL after Fiji server changes
Using Stowers plugins in academic microscopy image analysis workflows

name: Tango
description: Tools for Analysis of Nuclear Genome Organization.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need Tango's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying Tango tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: TEM suite
description: TEM suite is a set of ImageJ macro plugins for analyzing TEM images and diffraction patterns, including d-spacing measurement, publication-quality montages, and DM3 metadata extraction.
input_data: TEM images and electron diffraction pattern images; DM3 files in a directory (for metadata extraction).
output_data: d-spacing measurement values; montage/figure images; a text file with extracted DM3 metadata saved in the source directory.
use_when: Use this for TEM image and diffraction-pattern analysis tasks such as measuring interplanar spacing, assembling publication montages, or extracting DM3 metadata in batch.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not working with TEM images/diffraction patterns or you do not have DM3 files for the metadata extraction tool.
typical_use_cases: Measuring interplanar spacing (d-spacing) from TEM diffraction patterns with calc dSpace
Computing more accurate d-spacing values from diffraction patterns with get dSpace
Creating publication-quality figure montages with pub Montage
Batch-extracting metadata from all DM3 files in a folder to a text report with get Meta

name: TensorFlow
description: Installs and selects TensorFlow Java native libraries (CPU or GPU) in Fiji so imagej-tensorflow-based tools can run, optionally with NVIDIA CUDA/cuDNN acceleration.
input_data: No image input; uses the TensorFlow installer UI at Edit > Options > TensorFlow... to download/select TensorFlow Java native libraries (CPU/GPU).
output_data: Installed TensorFlow native library files and an active CPU/GPU TensorFlow selection, with status/errors reported in the TensorFlow options dialog and the Console log.
use_when: Use this to configure which TensorFlow (CPU/GPU) native library Fiji should use when running commands that depend on imagej-tensorflow (e.g., CSBDeep).
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not running any imagej-tensorflow-based tools or if you cannot meet the required NVIDIA driver/CUDA/cuDNN requirements for GPU support.
typical_use_cases: Switching from a CPU TensorFlow build to the matching GPU build in Edit > Options > TensorFlow...
Installing a specific TensorFlow version to match a model's required TensorFlow/CUDA/cuDNN versions
Checking in Window > Console whether Fiji loaded the selected TensorFlow library
Setting environment variables (e.g., LD_LIBRARY_PATH, PATH, CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES) so TensorFlow GPU is found and used

name: Timebar
description: Decorates time-series stacks with a timestamp and optional progress bar overlay on each frame using the stack frame interval metadata.
input_data: ImageJ/Fiji image stacks with a temporal dimension (e.g., 2D+T, 3D+T) and a frame interval set in Image > Properties.
output_data: Decorated image stack with rendered timestamp text and optional progress bar drawn onto frames.
use_when: Use it to add readable timecodes (and optionally a progress indicator) to time-lapse stacks before exporting or presenting movies.
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when the stack has no time dimension or the frame interval metadata is missing/incorrect and accurate timestamps are required.
typical_use_cases: Adding timestamps to time-lapse microscopy stacks before exporting a movie
Overlaying an elapsed-time timecode with a custom format (e.g., D-HH:MM:ss.SSS)
Adding a progress bar to show position within a time series
Offsetting the displayed time so the first frame starts at a non-zero timecode

name: Tr2d
description: An assignment-based tracker for multi-object tracking in 2D time-lapse (2D+t) image sequences.
input_data: 2D time-lapse (2D+t) image sequences.
output_data: Object tracks over time (track assignments/trajectories).
use_when: Use this when you need assignment-based multi-object tracking across frames in a 2D+t time-lapse sequence.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for single images without time or for 3D/3D+t tracking.
typical_use_cases: Tracking multiple cells in 2D time-lapse microscopy
Tracking multiple particles across frames in 2D+t sequences
Linking detected objects between consecutive frames to build trajectories

name: TraJClassifier
description: Loads TrackMate-exported trajectories, computes TraJ motion features, and classifies and locally segments tracks into diffusion/motion types using a random-forest model.
input_data: TrackMate trajectory XML files exported via the TrackMate action "Export trajectories".
output_data: Trajectory segment motion-type labels with confidence values and optional overlay visualizations (classes/colors and IDs).
use_when: Use it when you need to classify TrackMate trajectories and detect local changes in motion type along a track (normal, subdiffusive/anomalous, confined, directed/active).
do_not_use_when: Do not use it when trajectories are shorter than the required sliding-window/minimal track length (typically <30 positions) or you do not have TrackMate-exported XML trajectories.
typical_use_cases: Classifying single-particle tracking trajectories from TrackMate into diffusion vs directed motion
Segmenting a single trajectory into successive regions with different motion types using a sliding window
Analyzing nanoparticle diffusion trajectories in cellular microenvironments as in the referenced PLOS ONE study
Removing global drift from trajectories before motion-type classification

name: TreeJ
description: TreeJ provides an interactive interface to recursively reconstruct, edit, and annotate a cell lineage tree from a static 2D or 3D labeled image and export related images and tree files.
input_data: Static 2D or 3D labeled (segmented) images in 8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit where label 0 is background; discontinuous labels and watershed lines are supported.
output_data: Extracted/modified label images (current view, subtree, or tag-based), saved tree files (.treeV and .tree/Newick), and a PDF export of the drawn tree.
use_when: Use this when you have a static labeled segmentation and need to manually merge sister cells to backtrack and document a lineage tree with tags and exports.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you only have raw intensity images without region labels or when you need automated time-lapse lineage tracking.
typical_use_cases: Backtracking an embryo cell lineage by iteratively merging sister cells in a segmented 3D stack
Tagging lineage nodes and extracting all cells descending from a tagged mother cell
Exporting the reconstructed lineage to Newick (.tree) for use in other tree viewers
Exporting the displayed lineage tree drawing to a PDF report

name: TWOMBLI
description: TWOMBLI is a Fiji/ImageJ macro plugin pipeline that generates mask images and a single CSV of multiple quantitative metrics describing extracellular matrix and other fibrous network patterns.
input_data: In-focus, sufficiently high-resolution microscopy images of fibrous networks (e.g., ECM-stained tissue, vascular or cytoskeletal networks) opened in Fiji/ImageJ.
output_data: A CSV file of network metrics plus accompanying processed images including ridge-detection-derived mask images for visual verification.
use_when: Use this when you need an automated end-to-end batch pipeline to quantify multiple aspects of fibrous network architecture from microscopy images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when images are out of focus, too low resolution, or contain artefacts/irrelevant regions that prevent reliable fibre mask generation.
typical_use_cases: Quantifying ECM organisation in stained tissue sections (e.g., Picrosirius red breast cancer biopsies)
Batch comparing fibre alignment, curvature, and fractal dimension across experimental conditions
Measuring endpoints, branch points, total fibre length, and gaps in collagen/fibronectin networks
Analyzing vascular or cytoskeletal filament network patterning using the same metric set

name: U-Net Segmentation
description: Connects Fiji/ImageJ to a local or remote caffe_unet backend to run U-Net inference for segmentation and detection in 2D/3D microscopy images.
input_data: 2D images and 3D image stacks opened in Fiji/ImageJ (sample data provided as stacks).
output_data: U-Net prediction images (e.g., probability maps and segmentation masks) returned to Fiji for further analysis.
use_when: Use this when you have a caffe_unet backend available and want to apply pretrained or finetuned 2D/3D U-Net models from within Fiji.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you cannot run or access a Linux caffe_unet backend or if transmitting images to a remote server is not permitted.
typical_use_cases: 2D cell segmentation using the provided pretrained 2D model
3D microspore segmentation in structured illumination fluorescence and brightfield stacks using the provided 3D model
3D neurite segmentation in EM volumes using the SNEMI-trained models
Testing U-Net predictions on sample datasets via an AWS EC2 GPU backend

name: UCB Vision Sciences
description: Creates a quiver plot image (2D vector arrow field) from two 32-bit images encoding per-pixel vector direction (angle in degrees) and magnitude.
input_data: Two open 32-bit (float) 2D images: an angle map (degrees) and a magnitude map (e.g., speed/intensity), with no NaN pixels.
output_data: A new image window containing the quiver plot, with vectors scaled relative to the maximum magnitude in the magnitude image.
use_when: Use this when you have angle and magnitude maps and need a quick visualization of the resulting 2D vector field at a chosen sampling resolution.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your inputs are not 32-bit angle/magnitude images, contain NaNs, or if you need vector lengths to be comparable across different plots.
typical_use_cases: Visualizing optical flow angle and speed maps as an arrow field
Displaying wave propagation direction and speed across an image
Plotting motion/velocity fields derived from time-lapse microscopy
Showing direction and magnitude maps from vector analysis outputs

name: Vale lab plugins
description: Plugins useful to the Vale lab: Saim, spot intensity analysis, spot counter.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need Vale lab plugins's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying Vale lab plugins tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: VCell Simulation Results Viewer
description: Opens VCell spatial simulation results exported as ImageJ-compatible N5 datasets for remote viewing in Fiji/ImageJ and supports automated measurement scripts that export reduced results to CSV.
input_data: VCell spatial simulation results exported as ImageJ compatible remote storage (*.N5), including shared N5 URLs/links from the VCell server.
output_data: Virtual stack streams or fully loaded images in Fiji/ImageJ (including an additional channel for geometry/structures), plus CSV files produced by measurement/statistical processing scripts.
use_when: Use this to view and analyze very large VCell spatial simulation exports stored on the VCell server in N5 format without downloading them first.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when your data are not VCell N5 exports or when you need offline/local-only access without streaming and buffering delays.
typical_use_cases: Opening a large VCell simulation time series as a streamed virtual stack in Fiji
Loading a VCell N5 export fully into memory for interactive inspection and analysis
Sharing a VCell N5 export link with a collaborator to open via Use N5 Link
Running a measurement script to batch-process simulation images and export reduced results to CSV

name: VerciniAnalysisJ
description: A Fiji ActionBar-based set of ImageJ scripts to denoise and register VerCINI time-lapse data, crop ring ROIs into individual images, and compute kymograph trace statistics from line ROIs.
input_data: VerCINI fluorescence microscopy TIFF time-lapse stacks/videos and kymograph TIFF images, with matching ImageJ ROI zip files (e.g., image.tif with image.roi.zip; kymograph.tif with kymograph.zip).
output_data: Denoised and registered TIFF stacks (e.g., *_denoise_reg.tif), cropped ring TIFF images in an output folder (e.g., Indiv_rings), and CSV files of kymograph trace statistics (length, time, speed, angle).
use_when: Use this for batch processing and quantitative analysis of VerCINI datasets where denoise+registration, ROI-based ring cropping, and kymograph line-trace statistics are needed.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not analyzing VerCINI-style data or if you cannot provide correctly named ROI zip files required for automated cropping and kymograph statistics.
typical_use_cases: Batch denoise and register VerCINI time-lapse TIFF stacks
Automatically crop individual Z-ring regions from a registered stack using saved ROIs
Apply ridge filtering to kymographs prior to manual line tracing
Export CSV statistics (length, time, speed, angle) for traced kymograph tracks

name: VersiLab plugins
description: ImageJ/Fiji plugin with a JavaFX GUI for real-time webcam acquisition and live OpenCV-based processing for pre-clinical optical endo-microscopy, with optional National Instruments digital I/O LED control.
input_data: Live webcam video stream acquired via OpenCV (Mat frames); optional NI-9402/cDAQ1 digital module for LED control.
output_data: Live processed image display/stream in the GUI; optional drawn circle overlays/annotations from core detection/population algorithms.
use_when: Use this for interactive, real-time OEM imaging where you want to view and process a webcam stream live and optionally drive LEDs via NI digital I/O.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this for offline/batch image analysis or when OpenCV native libraries and required camera/NI hardware cannot be installed or configured.
typical_use_cases: Streaming webcam images into Fiji and applying selectable OpenCV filters in real time
Detecting and visualizing fiber cores during OEM acquisition (circle drawing)
Controlling LEDs through an NI-9402 module while viewing a live camera stream
Prototyping and testing new drop-down OpenCV processing algorithms for live imaging

name: Virtual-Orientation-Tools-VOTj
description: Virtual-Orientation-Tools-VOTj centers and rotates a single masked object in 2D–5D Fiji images to a user-chosen horizontal or vertical orientation, optionally propagating the same transform across channels and timepoints and applying a polarity flip.
input_data: Fiji images (2D/3D/4D/5D; including multichannel, z-stacks, timelapse) plus a corresponding binary mask image (single plane or stack depending on dimensionality), or user-drawn annotations that generate the mask; batch mode can also use a custom macro to generate masks per image.
output_data: Transformed (rotated/centered and optionally flipped for polarity) image/stack; in batch mode, saved output images and optionally saved mask images in a Mask_VOTj subfolder.
use_when: Use this when you have one object of interest per image and want consistent centering and horizontal/vertical orientation across datasets, including multichannel and timelapse stacks, using a mask or annotations.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you need to align multiple objects independently within the same image or when you cannot provide or create a reliable binary mask/annotation of the object.
typical_use_cases: Rotating and centering a single organism or cell to a standard horizontal/vertical pose before measurement
Applying the same orientation correction to all channels of a multichannel fluorescence image
Aligning each timepoint in a timelapse using per-timepoint (multi-slice) annotations
Batch-orienting a folder of images using matching mask files or a macro that generates masks

name: Visualization Toolset
description: A macro Toolset helping daily handling and visualization of multichannel images and stacks. Feedback always welcome!
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need Visualization Toolset's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying Visualization Toolset tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: Void Whizzard
description: Void Whizzard is a Fiji plugin that automates high-throughput quantification of spontaneous void spot assay (VSA) filter paper images to measure urine spot number, area, optional volume, and spatial distribution.
input_data: Landscape-oriented .tif images of VSA filter papers (typically pre-cropped/straightened with the companion Cropping Tool) and an optional conv.txt standard-curve file (volumes and areas) for area-to-volume conversion.
output_data: Per-image spot measurements (spot count, spot areas, optional total/spot volumes), spot size-bin distributions, spatial localization metrics (e.g., center/corner offsets), and result tables in Fiji.
use_when: Use this to objectively and consistently quantify mouse spontaneous void spot assay filter paper images, especially for high-throughput/batch analysis with optional area-to-volume conversion.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if your inputs are not VSA filter paper .tif images (or the folder contains non-VSA files) or if you need general-purpose spot detection outside the VSA workflow.
typical_use_cases: Batch processing a folder of VSA .tif images to compute total spot count and total spot area per paper
Generating and using a conv.txt standard curve to convert spot area measurements into urine volume estimates
Quantifying spatial voiding behavior by computing percent of volume/spots in center vs corners using offset settings
Comparing voiding patterns by binning spots into user-defined area ranges and analyzing frequency distributions

name: Volumetric Tissue Exploration and Analysis
description: VTEA is an ImageJ/Fiji plugin for interactive 3D tissue cytometry, including loading 3D volumes, segmentation, feature scatter plots, and flow cytometry-like gating with visualization and labeling in the original volume.
input_data: 3D microscopy image volumes such as multi-channel fluorescence TIFF stacks (e.g., Kidney_Cortex_Human.tif) and VTEA object files (.obx); can import segmentations generated outside VTEA (e.g., Cellpose).
output_data: Cell/object feature scatter plots for gating, gated/labelled cell populations, and visualization/ROI overlays back in the original 3D image volume.
use_when: Use this when you need interactive 3D tissue cytometry with segmentation, feature-based scatter plots, and gating/labeling visualized in the original volume.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you only need basic 2D analysis or do not require cytometry-style gating and 3D volume visualization.
typical_use_cases: Loading a 3D tissue TIFF stack and running a quick segmentation-to-scatter-plot workflow
Previewing how preprocessing and parameter choices affect 3D segmentation
Importing an external segmentation (e.g., Cellpose) for downstream feature analysis and gating
Performing supervised gating/subgating and labeling cell instances with gates in the original volume

name: WhiskerTracking
description: WhisketTracking is a set of tools for tracking rodent whisker.
input_data: Fluorescence or biological microscopy images appropriate for this plugin's function
output_data: Processed images, measurements, or analysis results depending on the plugin used
use_when: You need WhiskerTracking's specific functionality for your image analysis task
do_not_use_when: Standard Fiji tools or other registry plugins already cover your analysis needs
typical_use_cases: Applying WhiskerTracking tools to fluorescence microscopy data
Integrating into multi-step image analysis workflows

name: WormBox
description: WormBox is a Fiji plugin for documenting landmark-based linear distance measurements and meristic counts, exporting results as CSV tables and saving landmarks as ROI sets for reproducibility.
input_data: Microscopy images opened in Fiji/ImageJ (preferably .tif with lowercase extension; other ImageJ-supported formats accepted) with spatial scale calibration (recognized or set in ImageJ).
output_data: ROISet.zip landmark files plus associated .txt files per image, and a compiled .csv (comma-separated values) results table with per-image variables and summary statistics.
use_when: Use this when you need reproducible landmark-based distance measurements (including summed multi-segment distances) and/or structure counts across a folder of scaled images.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you cannot provide a reliable spatial scale or when you need segmentation-based measurements rather than landmark-defined distances/counts.
typical_use_cases: Measuring multiple morphometric distances between predefined anatomical landmarks across many specimens
Summing distances across landmark chains to estimate curved or multi-part lengths (e.g., 1-2-3-4)
Counting meristic structures (e.g., testes) and exporting counts alongside distance variables
Revising measurements later by reloading saved ROI landmark sets for each image

name: WormSizer
description: WormSizer is a Fiji/ImageJ plugin for measuring nematode length, volume, and surface area from microscope images and exporting the results.
input_data: Images of nematodes opened in Fiji/ImageJ (specific file formats not specified).
output_data: Measurement results and WormSizer output files including XML and CSV (surface area reported in microns squared).
use_when: Use this when you need automated length/volume/surface-area measurements for nematodes in Fiji and want results saved to XML/CSV.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this if you are not measuring nematodes or if you need a general-purpose measurement workflow not based on worm geometry.
typical_use_cases: Measuring C. elegans length from microscope images in Fiji
Estimating nematode volume using the plugin's frustum-based calculation
Computing nematode surface area for phenotype or growth studies
Updating existing WormSizer XML/CSV outputs to add surface area using the included script

name: XitoSBML
description: Creates and exports a Spatial SBML (SBML Level 3 spatial package) model from segmented (binary) 2D/3D images and can optionally edit the model by adding species, reactions, and diffusion/advection parameters.
input_data: One or more opened segmented (binary) 2D images or 3D Z-stack images (e.g., TIFF) assigned to domain types; original microscopy images may also be opened but are optional.
output_data: Spatial SBML XML file and a merged segmented image (2D or 3D TIFF); also displays the SBML document and domain hierarchy in the plugin UI.
use_when: Use this when you already have binary segmentation masks for cellular domains and need a Spatial SBML model for downstream SBML-based simulation or editing.
do_not_use_when: Do not use this when you do not have segmented (binary) domain images or when you only need to segment or quantify images without exporting an SBML model.
typical_use_cases: Export nucleus and cytosol binary masks as a 2D Spatial SBML model
Export 3D Z-stack organelle masks as a 3D Spatial SBML model
Merge multiple domain masks into one labeled/merged image while exporting SBML
Add species, reactions, and diffusion coefficients to a spatial model derived from segmentation
Generate Spatial SBML input for external simulators that support the SBML spatial package

name: Xlib
description: Xlib is a collection of ImageJ plugins for 2D/3D image stacks providing filtering, reconstruction, quantitative evaluation, data import, and interactive segmentation and visualization tools.
input_data: 2D images and 3D image stacks; binary masks and labeled images; .dmp files (Import DMP); projection and sinogram image sets (reconstruction).
output_data: Filtered/processed images and stacks; gradient magnitude/angle images (Canny Edge); binary masks and label images; 3D renderings/volume views; quantitative evaluation results (e.g., particle/pore size distributions, phase evaluation).
use_when: Use Xlib when you need 2D/3D-capable filtering, reconstruction, evaluation, import, or interactive segmentation/visualization tools that remove common size/3D limitations of standard ImageJ commands.
do_not_use_when: Do not use Xlib if you only need basic built-in ImageJ/Fiji functions on simple 2D images and do not require its 3D/stack-aware or specialized import/reconstruction/evaluation features.
typical_use_cases: Applying true-3D or slice-wise filtering (anisotropic diffusion, median, wavelets, stripe removal) to microscopy/CT stacks
Reconstructing volumes from projection data using filtered backprojection or projection-sinogram conversion
Labeling, skeletonizing, and interactively editing 2D/3D binary masks and labeled regions
Computing particle size or pore size distributions from segmented binary structures
Importing 2D float images stored in .dmp files (e.g., from MATLAB) into ImageJ

name: ZedMate Suite
